name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page that loads and displays featured service categories and featured projects, and routes users into detailed service, project, and operational flows (services, projects, equipment rental, locations, training, cost calculator, and contact).

core_features: Load and display featured service categories and their sample services using getHomeFeaturedServiceCategories, with navigation into the Services listing and specific Service Detail pages.
Load and display featured project case studies using getHomeFeaturedProjects in a navigable carousel or list, with navigation into the full Projects listing and specific Project Detail pages.

Provide quick navigation shortcuts from the homepage to key flows: Services, Projects, Equipment Rental, Locations, Training, Cost Calculator, and Contact (preconfigured for design-build project inquiries).
Handle loading and error states for the featured service categories and featured projects data, without relying on any static or hardcoded business data.
user_workflows:
Browse featured services and go to a filtered Services list:
1) User opens Home (index.html).
2) home-data-loader component calls getHomeFeaturedServiceCategories.
3) featured-service-category-list renders the returned list of categories and their featured services.
4) User clicks a category tile.
5) The click handler reads the clicked category_code from the bound data and navigates to services.html?category={category_code}.

Open a specific service directly from Home:
1) User opens Home and data loads as above.
2) In featured-service-category-list, user clicks a specific featured service card.
3) The click handler reads the bound service_id for that card.
4) Browser navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={service_id}.

Browse featured projects and open a project:
1) User opens Home (index.html).
2) home-data-loader calls getHomeFeaturedProjects.
3) featured-projects-carousel renders project cards from the response.
4) User navigates between projects using carousel controls (pure client-side state).
5) User clicks a project card.
6) The click handler reads project_id from the bound project item.
7) Browser navigates to project_detail.html?projectId={project_id}.

Go from Home to complete project listing:
1) User opens Home, featured projects load.
2) User clicks the 'View all projects' call-to-action in featured-projects-carousel.
3) Browser navigates to projects.html without additional parameters.

Use homepage quick shortcuts to jump into key flows:
1) User opens Home.
2) quick-actions-strip renders navigation tiles/buttons for Equipment Rental, Find a Branch, Training & Certifications, Estimate Your Project, and Start a Design-Build Project.
3) User clicks one of the tiles.
4) The corresponding click handler navigates to:
   - equipment_rental.html
   - locations.html
   - training.html
   - cost_calculator.html
   - contact.html?inquiry_type=design_build_project

Fallback behavior when data interfaces return empty results:
1) User opens Home.
2) home-data-loader calls getHomeFeaturedServiceCategories and/or getHomeFeaturedProjects.
3) If getHomeFeaturedServiceCategories returns an empty array or an error, featured-service-category-list hides the service categories section and may display only generic navigation CTAs (non-data-driven) if implemented.
4) If getHomeFeaturedProjects returns an empty array or an error, featured-projects-carousel hides the projects section and only keeps the 'View all projects' navigation CTA if implemented.
5) The rest of the homepage navigation (quick shortcuts and global navigation) continues to function.
interactions: On page load: home-data-loader initializes, calls getHomeFeaturedServiceCategories and getHomeFeaturedProjects in parallel, and triggers loading states in dependent components until responses are received or an error is detected.
Click on global header 'Services' link: navigates to services.html with no additional parameters.
Click on global header 'Projects' link: navigates to projects.html with no additional parameters.
Click on a featured service category tile in featured-service-category-list: reads the tile’s category_code and navigates to services.html?category={category_code}.
Click on a featured service card in featured-service-category-list: reads the card’s service_id and navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={service_id}.
Click on 'Explore Services' CTA (if implemented within the services area): navigates to services.html.
Use next/previous controls in featured-projects-carousel: updates the currently visible project(s) using in-memory UI state (e.g., currentIndex) without any API calls or URL changes.
Click on a project card in featured-projects-carousel: reads project_id and navigates to project_detail.html?projectId={project_id}.
Click on 'View all projects' CTA in featured-projects-carousel: navigates to projects.html.
Click on 'Equipment Rental' quick action: navigates to equipment_rental.html.
Click on 'Find a Branch' quick action: navigates to locations.html.
Click on 'Training & Certifications' quick action: navigates to training.html.
Click on 'Estimate Your Project' quick action: navigates to cost_calculator.html.
Click on 'Start a Design-Build Project' quick action: navigates to contact.html?inquiry_type=design_build_project.
Click on retry action for a failed data load (if provided): re-invokes getHomeFeaturedServiceCategories and/or getHomeFeaturedProjects and re-renders the relevant sections based on the new responses.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page does not require any functional incoming URL parameters; its core behavior is the same for index.html and the root URL. Any incidental query parameters present in the URL are ignored by this page. The page state is managed entirely in client memory: loading states, error states, and UI-only states (such as the current index of the featured-projects-carousel or expanded/collapsed category sections) are not stored in URL parameters and are not persisted to localStorage. Outgoing navigation uses URL parameters to pass state to other pages as follows: services.html?category={category_code} when a featured service category tile is clicked; service_detail.html?serviceId={service_id} when a featured service card is clicked; project_detail.html?projectId={project_id} when a featured project is clicked; and contact.html?inquiry_type=design_build_project when the 'Start a Design-Build Project' quick action is clicked. The Home page itself does not read or modify these parameters; it only constructs them for navigation.

components.functionality: Provides top-level navigation links to the main sections of the site and a direct way to return to the Home page. It does not depend on dynamic data and primarily manages navigation to other pages.
event_handlers: onClickHome: Navigates to index.html or the site root URL without parameters.
onClickContact: Navigates to contact.html without additional parameters (for generic contact entry).

components.functionality: Centralized controller that, on page initialization, requests featured service categories and featured projects using the assigned interfaces, stores their results in page state, and exposes that state to child components. It also manages loading and error flags for each data source.
data_binding: getHomeFeaturedServiceCategories (interface response, an array of featured service category objects)
getHomeFeaturedProjects (interface response, an array of featured project objects)
event_handlers: onInit: Invoked when the Home page is loaded; invokes getHomeFeaturedServiceCategories and getHomeFeaturedProjects in parallel or sequence, and sets internal state like { serviceCategoriesLoading, projectListLoading, serviceCategoriesError, projectListError } based on the results.

onServiceCategoriesLoaded: Receives the array returned from getHomeFeaturedServiceCategories and stores it in state (e.g., homeState.serviceCategories), then clears the loading flag and error for that section.
onProjectsLoaded: Receives the array returned from getHomeFeaturedProjects and stores it in state (e.g., homeState.projects), then clears the loading flag and error for that section.

onServiceCategoriesError: Sets serviceCategoriesError to a non-null value and serviceCategoriesLoading to false, so dependent components can render an appropriate fallback (e.g., hide the section or show a non-data-bound message).
onProjectsError: Sets projectListError to a non-null value and projectListLoading to false, so dependent components can hide or fallback their UI.
onRetryServiceCategories: Re-invokes getHomeFeaturedServiceCategories and resets relevant loading/error state.
onRetryProjects: Re-invokes getHomeFeaturedProjects and resets relevant loading/error state.

components.functionality: Displays the list of featured service categories and their associated featured services on the Home page. Each category is shown as a selectable tile bound to a category_code; each featured service within that tile is shown as a clickable card bound to a service_id. Users can navigate to the Services list filtered by category or directly to a specific Service Detail page. The component reacts to loading and error flags provided by home-data-loader.
data_binding: Array from getHomeFeaturedServiceCategories exposed via home-data-loader, where each item includes category_code, category_name, category_description, and a featured_services array.

Each featured_services item’s fields (service_id, service_name, service_slug, typical_budget_min, typical_budget_max, typical_timeline_summary) are bound for display and for constructing navigation URLs.
event_handlers: onRender: Reads homeState.serviceCategories, serviceCategoriesLoading, and serviceCategoriesError from home-data-loader. If loading is true, renders a loading state; if error is present or the array is empty, hides or minimizes this section; otherwise renders the category tiles and service cards.

onCategoryClick(categoryItem): Uses categoryItem.category_code from the bound data to navigate to services.html?category={categoryItem.category_code}. The handler uses the exact category_code from the API response without hardcoding values.
onServiceClick(serviceItem): Uses serviceItem.service_id from the bound data to navigate to service_detail.html?serviceId={serviceItem.service_id}.
onExploreServicesClick: Optional CTA within this component that navigates to services.html without parameters, for users who want to browse all services.
onKeyboardActivateCategory (for accessibility): Keyboard-activated equivalent of onCategoryClick, triggered when users interact with a category tile via keyboard controls and the tile is focused.
onKeyboardActivateService (for accessibility): Keyboard-activated equivalent of onServiceClick for a focused service card.

components.functionality: Renders the list of featured projects returned by getHomeFeaturedProjects as a carousel or list on the Home page, allowing users to cycle through and open detailed views or navigate to the full Projects listing. It handles cases where the data is still loading, errored, or empty.
data_binding: Array from getHomeFeaturedProjects exposed via home-data-loader. Each project item provides project_id, name, sector, project_type, location_city, location_state, completion_year, budget, thumbnail_url, and is_featured for display and for navigation.
Internal UI state for carousel position (e.g., currentIndex) is stored in memory within this component and is not represented in URL parameters.
event_handlers: onRender: Reads homeState.projects, projectListLoading, and projectListError from home-data-loader. If loading is true, shows a loading indicator; if an error occurred or the list is empty, hides or minimizes this entire section while optionally leaving a generic 'View all projects' navigation CTA available.
onProjectClick(projectItem): Uses projectItem.project_id to navigate to project_detail.html?projectId={projectItem.project_id}.

onNext: If there are multiple projects, updates the internal carousel index to show the next project(s). If at the end of the list, may loop to the beginning or disable the next control. This state is purely internal and not reflected in the URL.
onPrevious: Updates the internal carousel index to show the previous project(s). If at the beginning of the list, may loop to the end or disable the previous control.
onSelectSlide(index): Directly sets the internal carousel index when a user clicks a pagination control or dot corresponding to a specific project index.
onViewAllProjectsClick: Navigates to projects.html without additional parameters.
onKeyboardActivateProject (for accessibility): Keyboard-activated equivalent of onProjectClick for a focused project card.

components.functionality: Provides a set of prominent, non-data-driven navigation tiles/buttons on the Home page that route users directly into key flows: equipment rental, branch finder, training courses, cost calculator, and contact (pre-configured for design-build project inquiries). It does not depend on any API data and acts solely as a navigation hub.
event_handlers: onClickEquipmentRental: Navigates to equipment_rental.html, allowing users to start exploring equipment rental options.
onClickFindBranch: Navigates to locations.html, where users can search for branches or offices.
onClickTraining: Navigates to training.html, where users can browse available training and certification courses.
onClickCostCalculator: Navigates to cost_calculator.html, where users can generate cost estimates using the Cost Calculator workflow.
onClickDesignBuildContact: Navigates to contact.html?inquiry_type=design_build_project, pre-configuring the Contact page for a design-build project inquiry type using the inquiry_type URL parameter.
onKeyboardActivateQuickAction (for accessibility): Handles keyboard activation of any focused quick-action tile, invoking the corresponding click handler.

components.functionality: Coordinates the placement and visibility of Home page components based on the current loading/error state of data and ensures that the page remains functional even if one or both data interfaces fail or return no results. It does not fetch data by itself but observes the state from home-data-loader and directs which sections should be rendered.
data_binding: Derived state from home-data-loader, including serviceCategoriesLoading, projectListLoading, serviceCategoriesError, projectListError, and the presence or absence of populated arrays from getHomeFeaturedServiceCategories and getHomeFeaturedProjects.
event_handlers: onStateChange: Triggered whenever home-data-loader updates its state. The layout-controller evaluates which sections (featured-service-category-list, featured-projects-carousel, quick-actions-strip) should be visible or hidden based on loading and error conditions and updates the rendered component tree accordingly.

onSectionErrorAcknowledged: When an error state is acknowledged by the user (if an error message is displayed), this handler hides the errored section while keeping other sections and navigation active.
onRetryFromLayout: If a global retry control is provided (e.g., 'Retry loading featured content'), this handler delegates to home-data-loader’s onRetryServiceCategories and/or onRetryProjects.

components.functionality: Provides bottom-of-page navigation shortcuts to the same key sections available in the header and quick-actions-strip, ensuring that users who scroll to the bottom can still easily access other flows. It uses simple link navigation and no dynamic data.
event_handlers: onClickFooterContact: Navigates to contact.html (generic contact entry).

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a stacked, top-to-bottom banded structure: a full-width global header/navigation component at the top (internally split into light grey utility bar, black brand header, and dark grey nav band), followed by a white main content area with a single centered column. Inside the content column, place the featured projects carousel as the main hero at the top, followed by a horizontal quick-actions strip, then the featured service category grid. End the page with a full-width footer navigation band. Non-visual controller/loader components sit logically near the top of the main content but do not occupy visible space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a stacked, top-to-bottom banded structure: a full-width global header/navigation component at the top (internally split into light grey utility bar, black brand header, and dark grey nav band), followed by a white main content area with a single centered column. Inside the content column, place the featured projects carousel as the main hero at the top, followed by a horizontal quick-actions strip, then the featured service category grid. End the page with a full-width footer navigation band. Non-visual controller/loader components sit logically near the top of the main content but do not occupy visible space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve roughly 20–25% of the viewport height for the stacked header/nav structure at typical desktop heights, with each internal header band kept relatively compact vertically. Allocate the next ~35–40% of the viewport to the featured projects carousel as a dominant hero element with generous vertical space. The quick-actions strip and service category grid share the remaining main content area with 32–48px vertical gaps between sections. The footer occupies a modest but clear full-width band at the bottom. Horizontally, all main content (hero, quick actions, grid) sits in a centered column that spans about 70–80% of the viewport width up to a max width (e.g., ~1200px), leaving consistent side margins for breathing room.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the header and navigation bands relatively dense: compact vertical height, tight grouping of logo and contact info, and closely spaced nav items, reflecting a serious, information-forward corporate feel. In contrast, the main content area is more airy: the hero carousel image is large with strong margins above and below, the quick-actions tiles are clearly separated but not cramped, and the service category grid uses 24–32px gutters between cards with 32–48px vertical separation between sections, maintaining clarity and visual breathing room.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F/Z-pattern. The eye starts top-left at the logo in the black header band, moves right across to contact information, then drops slightly to the dark grey nav and scans left-to-right across menu items, with the active 'Home' item highlighted in red. From that highlighted nav item, the eye naturally moves down to the central hero projects carousel. The strong horizontal edge of the carousel leads the eye across, then down into the quick-actions strip (a smaller but visually strong band of tiles), and finally into the service category cards. Red accents (active nav, key tiles, important category tags) and the black/dark grey bands anchor attention at interaction points while the white content area guides smooth downward scanning.

layout.overall_description: The page is composed of a sequence of horizontal bands that feel solid and corporate. At the very top, a compact light grey utility bar spans edge to edge, immediately followed by a taller, bold black band containing the logo on the left and key contact details on the right. Directly beneath, a dark grey navigation band stretches across, with main menu items arranged horizontally and the current 'Home' item highlighted in red. Below this stacked header, the background switches to white and a centered content column emerges. The first thing inside is a wide, landscape featured projects carousel that dominates the center, framed by generous left and right margins. Just beneath it, a lower-contrast but clearly separated quick-actions strip displays a row of rectangular tiles, each leading to critical flows like equipment rental or contact. Further down, the featured service categories are presented in a clean, multi-column grid of sturdy rectangular cards, evenly spaced and aligned to the same central column. The entire layout ends with a full-width, dark footer band that visually balances the header, containing secondary navigation and company details. The overall effect is structured and slightly compact at the top, opening into a more spacious, image-led main area, all governed by a clear 12-column centered grid and a strong black/red/white palette.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-header-nav component occupies the full width of the viewport at the very top and is composed of three stacked internal bands that together take up roughly 20–25% of the initial viewport height on desktop. The topmost internal band is a light grey utility bar approximately 40–48px high, running edge-to-edge with a centered max-width content container (about 1120–1200px). Within this bar, utility items (such as small text links or icons) are aligned to the right, with about 16–24px padding above and below and 24–32px padding from the right edge of the container; the left side remains mostly open for a spacious feel. Directly below, the main brand bar is a solid black band around 80–96px high. Inside its centered max-width container, the logo sits on the left, occupying roughly 30–40% of the horizontal space. It is vertically centered and given 16–24px of padding on all sides. On the right side of this band, filling the remaining 60–70%, are contact blocks (phone number, address, possibly email), laid out horizontally or in tight vertical stacks aligned right; they are slightly dense to convey seriousness but maintain at least 8–12px padding between text lines. Underneath the black bar, a dark grey navigation band about 56–64px high spans full-width. Within the same centered container, primary nav items are arranged horizontally from left to right, starting flush with or slightly inset from the left edge of the content column. Each nav item has about 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, with a compact vertical height. The active 'Home' nav item is rendered with a red background or underline, making it the strongest accent in this band and visually anchoring the transition into the main content. Overall, the header/nav stack appears as a solid, clearly layered block, tightly occupying the upper fifth of the screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed at the top of the document flow (not sticky by default), occupying the natural top position. All internal bands are static and scroll away together with the page as the user moves down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-data-loader component lives just below the global-header-nav in the DOM hierarchy but is effectively non-visual in the standard state. Visually, it occupies no dedicated height and does not disrupt the white background of the main content area. When an explicit loading state is needed, it can manifest as a thin red or dark grey progress bar immediately beneath the dark grey navigation band, stretching horizontally across the same centered content width (around 1120–1200px) with 4–8px height. This optional bar aligns flush to the top of the white content area so that it feels like an extension of the header system, not a separate block. Outside of loading, the surrounding layout closes up seamlessly, with the featured projects carousel appearing directly under the nav.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and non-visual by default. When active, it overlays or occupies a very thin strip at the top of the main content without pushing major components significantly, behaving like a transient status indicator.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-projects-carousel sits at the top of the white main content area, directly beneath the header/nav stack (and the optional loader bar if visible). It is centered within the main content column, which spans about 70–80% of the viewport width up to a max width of roughly 1120–1200px, leaving broad equal margins on left and right. The carousel’s primary image area uses a wide landscape aspect ratio (approximately 16:9 or slightly narrower), typically occupying 35–40% of the initial viewport height on desktop, making it the dominant visual on the page once the user scrolls past the header. The image extends almost the full width of the content column, with 24–32px top margin from the navigation band and 24–32px bottom margin before the next section. Navigation arrows or controls are positioned near the left and right edges of the image area, vertically centered; small dot indicators or slide labels may be centered below the image within the same block. Below the image, a compact caption and a short descriptive line can sit left-aligned within the carousel’s width, maintaining 16–24px padding inside a simple rectangular container. The carousel’s overall block appears as a strong, single-column hero that anchors the beginning of the main content, with crisp edges and no rounded corners, in keeping with the firm, rectangular visual style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow. It scales responsively with viewport width while preserving its aspect ratio and centered positioning in the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-actions-strip is positioned immediately below the featured-projects-carousel, separated from it by approximately 24–32px of vertical white space to maintain a clear but connected relationship. It spans the same centered content width (about 70–80% of the viewport up to ~1120–1200px) but can sit on a subtly differentiated background: either a very light grey band or white with a thin top border in red or dark grey, visually forming a shallow horizontal strip within the white content area. Within this strip, 3–6 rectangular tiles are arranged in a single horizontal row on desktop, each tile occupying roughly 1/4 of the content width when four tiles are present (i.e., about 3 columns each in a 12-column grid). Tiles are evenly spaced with 16–24px gaps between them. Each tile is a simple flat rectangle with sharp corners, about 96–128px tall, with an icon centered near the top and a label beneath, both center-aligned. The tiles use high contrast: either white backgrounds with black text and a red accent strip at the top, or alternating tiles with solid red backgrounds and white text for key actions like ‘Get a Quote’ or ‘Request Equipment’. The strip as a whole takes up a compact vertical footprint, roughly 120–160px including padding, and feels denser than the large hero image but more open than the navigation bar above. It acts as a visual bridge between the hero carousel and the more detailed content below, offering quick, scannable entry points into key flows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main content. On narrower screens it reflows into two rows of tiles while maintaining center alignment and consistent horizontal and vertical gaps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-service-category-list appears below the quick-actions-strip as the main structured content section on the home page. It starts after a larger vertical gap of about 32–48px to clearly distinguish it as a new section, still within the white main content background and centered on the same max-width column. At the top of this section, a simple left-aligned heading (e.g., ‘Our Services’ or ‘Service Areas’) sits with 8–16px of space below it before the grid. The grid itself follows a 12-column logic: on desktop, service category cards are typically arranged in three columns, each card spanning approximately 4 grid columns (around 30–32% of the content width) with 24–32px gutters between them. Cards stack into multiple rows as needed, with 24–32px vertical spacing between rows, and the entire grid is centered within the content column so that left and right margins align with the carousel above. Each service category card is a flat, sharp-edged rectangle with a subtle light grey border or shadow, 200–260px tall depending on content. Inside each card, a small category icon or thumbnail sits at the top, followed by a bold black category title and 1–2 lines of secondary description, all left-aligned with consistent 16–24px internal padding. A red accent—such as a thin top border, small tag, or CTA text—reinforces the brand. The section extends downward as needed but retains generous bottom margin (32–48px) before the footer, ensuring the content feels structured and readable rather than cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and responsive. On tablet, the grid reflows to two cards per row; on mobile, cards become a single centered column while preserving vertical spacing and alignment with the overall content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-layout-controller component sits conceptually at the root of the home page’s main content area, just after the global-header-nav in the structure but without any direct visual manifestation. It defines how the main column is centered and how components reflow across breakpoints. In practice, it establishes the max-width container (around 1120–1200px) and the horizontal alignment of the featured-projects-carousel, quick-actions-strip, and featured-service-category-list. It additionally governs vertical spacing between these sections in multiples of the 8px base unit (e.g., 24px, 32px, 48px), ensuring that each block sits in a clean vertical rhythm. Visually, it appears as a consistent alignment: all major content blocks share the same left and right boundaries and feel anchored to a single invisible vertical spine down the center of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, structural controller. It affects the responsive behavior and grid alignment of child components but does not occupy its own visible space; instead, it shapes the container within which other components are positioned.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-footer-nav component spans the full width of the viewport at the very bottom of the page and visually mirrors the strength of the header, helping to frame the layout. It appears as a dark grey or black horizontal band approximately 120–200px high, depending on content. Inside this band, a centered max-width container (matching the header’s ~1120–1200px width) holds the footer content. The layout within is more relaxed than the header: links and information can be arranged in 2–3 vertical columns aligned to the left side of the container while the right side can hold secondary information like a small logo, address, or certifications, all left-aligned within their columns. Text is white or light grey for high contrast against the dark background, with 16–24px vertical padding from the top and bottom edges. Link groups are stacked with 8–12px vertical spacing, keeping the footer moderately dense but clearly legible. A thin red line or subtle red accent icon can run along the top edge of the footer band or appear next to key links to tie into the brand color. The footer sits directly below the last content section, separated by about 32–48px of white space so that it reads as a distinct, final band rather than part of the main content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the document flow, expanding in height as needed based on its internal content. It always spans the full viewport width and aligns its internal content with the same centered column as the header and main content.

name: Services Overview
title: Services
description: Page that lists all service categories and their services, lets users focus on a specific category via URL, and navigate to detailed service, maintenance, training, and contact pages.

core_features: Fetch all service categories and their services using getServicesOverview and render them in a structured grid.
Allow users to focus on a specific service category via the optional `category` URL parameter or by selecting a category on the page.
Filter out inactive services so that only services marked as active in the getServicesOverview response are shown.
Enable navigation from service cards to service_detail.html with the selected service ID in the URL.
Provide category-level CTAs (e.g., for maintenance and training related categories) to navigate to maintenance_programs.html and training.html.
Provide global CTAs to navigate to contact.html with an appropriate inquiry_type parameter for design-build or custom service requests.
user_workflows: Browse all services: 1) User opens services.html without URL parameters. 2) Page calls getServicesOverview and shows all returned categories and their active services. 3) User scrolls through categories and their service cards. 4) User clicks a service card and is navigated to service_detail.html?serviceId={service_id}.

Arrive focused on a specific category from another page: 1) User clicks a link on index.html or service_detail.html that points to services.html?category={category_code}. 2) On load, the page reads the `category` URL parameter and sets this as the active category state. 3) Page calls getServicesOverview and renders all categories but auto-focuses and visually emphasizes the matching category section. 4) Only the active category’s section is shown or other categories are deemphasized according to the filter logic. 5) User can clear the focus or switch to another category via category controls, which update the `category` URL parameter.

Navigate to a service detail from a category: 1) User identifies the desired service in a category section. 2) User clicks the service card. 3) Page reads the service’s service_id from the bound data. 4) Browser navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={service_id}.

Navigate to Maintenance Programs from a relevant category: 1) User locates a category associated with maintenance services. 2) Within that category’s section, user clicks a category-level CTA such as 'View Maintenance Programs'. 3) The click handler determines the appropriate facility_type value if available from current page state or configuration; if available, it navigates to maintenance_programs.html?facility_type={facility_type}, otherwise it navigates to maintenance_programs.html without the parameter or with a neutral value accepted by the target page.

Navigate to Training from a relevant category: 1) User views a category associated with training or sees a training-related CTA. 2) User clicks 'View Training & Certifications' or equivalent category-level CTA. 3) Browser navigates directly to training.html.

Request a custom quote or talk to a project specialist: 1) From any point on the Services page, user finds the global CTA panel. 2) User clicks 'Talk to a project specialist' or 'Request Custom Quote'. 3) Browser navigates to contact.html?inquiry_type=design_build_project.
interactions: On page load, the page reads the `category` URL parameter (if present) and stores it as the initial active category code.
On page load, the page triggers getServicesOverview once and stores the returned list of categories and services in page state.
When a user clicks on a category control (e.g., tab, dropdown item, or list item), the active category state is updated and the `category` URL parameter is updated via navigation or history API.
When a user clicks a service card, the click handler reads the bound service_id and navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={service_id}.

When a user clicks a maintenance-specific CTA in a category section, the click handler determines any available facility_type context and navigates to maintenance_programs.html with or without the facility_type parameter based on what is available.
When a user clicks a training-related CTA, the click handler navigates directly to training.html.
When a user clicks a global CTA for contacting a specialist or requesting a custom quote, the click handler navigates to contact.html?inquiry_type=design_build_project.

If getServicesOverview fails or returns an empty set, the page enters an error or empty state where category and service grids show appropriate fallback messages while still keeping navigation elements functional where possible.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the `category` URL parameter and the response from getServicesOverview. On initial load, the page reads `category` from the URL (if present) and stores it as activeCategoryCode. The page then invokes getServicesOverview and stores the returned array as categoriesData. Once data is loaded, the visible category sections are determined as follows: if activeCategoryCode is null, empty, or does not match any category_code from categoriesData, all categories are displayed in their default order. If activeCategoryCode matches one or more category_code values in categoriesData, the UI marks those matching category sections as active; other categories may be hidden or visually secondary depending on the chosen filter behavior. Whenever the user changes the active category via UI controls, activeCategoryCode is updated and the browser URL is updated to services.html?category={new_category_code} (or services.html with no query when clearing the filter). This means the active category is fully recoverable via URL without any reliance on localStorage. The page also maintains transient states for data loading (e.g., loading, loaded, error). These states depend solely on the lifecycle of a single getServicesOverview request and are not persisted in the URL; they only influence what is currently rendered (loading indicators vs. data vs. error message). Navigation to other pages uses query parameters derived from currently bound data (for serviceId) or from explicit constants defined by the domain model (for inquiry_type) but does not introduce any additional page-level state beyond what is encoded in the target URLs.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getServicesOverview to retrieve all service categories and their services; manages loading and error states and exposes the resulting data to child components.
data_binding: Service (via mapping: each item in getServicesOverview[].services corresponds to a Service entity)
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered once when the page loads; executes getServicesOverview and populates categoriesData, loadingState, and errorState.
onReload: Can be triggered by user or system to re-call getServicesOverview and refresh categoriesData when needed.

components.functionality: Manages the currently active service category based on the `category` URL parameter and user interactions; exposes activeCategoryCode to other components and updates the URL when the active category changes.
data_binding: getServicesOverview[].category_code (set of valid codes loaded from the API)
event_handlers: onInit: Reads the `category` parameter from the URL, validates it against the list of category_code values (when data is available), and sets activeCategoryCode or clears it if invalid.

onCategoryChange: Invoked when a user selects a category in the UI; updates activeCategoryCode and updates the URL query string to reflect the new `category` value (or removes it when clearing the focus).

components.functionality: Displays a list, tabs, or dropdown of all service categories loaded from getServicesOverview and allows the user to select or clear an active category filter.
event_handlers: onCategoryClick: When the user clicks a category item, calls category-state-manager.onCategoryChange with the selected category_code.
onClearFilter: When the user chooses to view all categories, calls category-state-manager.onCategoryChange with a null or empty value to clear the activeCategoryCode.

components.functionality: Renders the main grid or stacked sections of service categories. Each category section includes its name, description, category-specific CTAs, and a list of service cards. The grid respects the active category filter so that only the relevant category (or all categories) is displayed according to activeCategoryCode.
event_handlers: onCategorySectionRender: Triggered for each category when rendering; uses activeCategoryCode to determine whether the section should be visible, emphasized, or hidden.
onCategorySectionCtaClick: Delegates category-level CTA clicks to the category-cta-panel component.

components.functionality: Within each visible category section, iterates over the services array and renders an individual service card for each service that is marked as active. Inactive services from the getServicesOverview response are filtered out.
event_handlers: onRenderServiceItem: For each service, decides whether to display it based on is_active; only active services are rendered.
onServiceCardClick: When a service card is clicked, reads the bound service_id and navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={service_id}.

components.functionality: Represents a single service card inside a category, showing the service name and key attributes (description, budget range, timeline summary) and providing a clickable region that leads to the service detail page.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={bound service_id} when the card (or its primary action) is clicked.

components.functionality: Displays category-specific call-to-action buttons within a category section. For categories associated with maintenance services, it offers navigation to maintenance_programs.html (optionally including a facility_type parameter when such context is available). For categories associated with training services, it offers navigation to training.html. For other categories, it can display generic navigation or no category-specific CTAs, depending on configuration.
event_handlers: onMaintenanceCtaClick: When the user clicks a maintenance-related CTA, determines if a facility_type selection or mapping is available from current state or configuration; if a facility_type value is available, navigates to maintenance_programs.html?facility_type={facility_type}, otherwise navigates to maintenance_programs.html without specifying facility_type or with a neutral value expected by the target page.
onTrainingCtaClick: When the user clicks a training-related CTA, navigates directly to training.html.

onGenericCtaClick: For other category-specific CTAs (if configured), navigates to appropriate target URLs using only parameters derived from the currently bound category data or from domain-defined constants, without introducing static service data.

components.functionality: Provides general page-level CTAs that allow users to contact the company for custom needs or design-build projects. CTAs navigate to the generic contact page with an appropriate inquiry_type parameter.
data_binding: Inquiry.inquiry_type (domain enumeration to construct the `inquiry_type` query parameter)
event_handlers: onTalkToSpecialistClick: Navigates to contact.html?inquiry_type=design_build_project using the domain-defined inquiry_type value.

onRequestCustomQuoteClick: Navigates to contact.html?inquiry_type=design_build_project using the same inquiry_type, allowing the contact page to distinguish design-build and custom service inquiries from other inquiry types.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing utility bar, black header, and dark grey navigation, the Services Overview page continues the mockup’s pattern with a white full-width content band. Inside this band, all components are arranged in a single centered column. The order from top to bottom is: (1) invisible data/state components, (2) a compact filter controls row, (3) a visually prominent services category grid, (4) a vertically stacked list of service cards for the selected category, (5) a category-specific CTA panel, and finally (6) a strong, full-width global CTA band at the bottom. All components align to the same max-width centered container, except the global CTA which echoes the full-width header bands.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing utility bar, black header, and dark grey navigation, the Services Overview page continues the mockup’s pattern with a white full-width content band. Inside this band, all components are arranged in a single centered column. The order from top to bottom is: (1) invisible data/state components, (2) a compact filter controls row, (3) a visually prominent services category grid, (4) a vertically stacked list of service cards for the selected category, (5) a category-specific CTA panel, and finally (6) a strong, full-width global CTA band at the bottom. All components align to the same max-width centered container, except the global CTA which echoes the full-width header bands.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The stacked header still occupies the top ~20–25% of the viewport. The remaining ~75–80% is a white content area where the category grid and service list dominate. Horizontally, content sits within a centered container that spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop (e.g., a max-width around 1100–1200px) with generous left/right margins. Vertically, the filter controls occupy a compact band near the top of the white area, the category grid and service list take the bulk of vertical space, and the two CTA panels are distinct bands near the lower third of the page, with the global CTA reclaiming a full-width horizontal bar similar to the header.
layout_strategies.content_density: Mirroring the mockup, density is higher in functional, information-heavy zones and more open around major content blocks. The filter controls are moderately dense and compact in height; the category grid is structured but allows clear breathing room between tiles. The service card list has medium density: each card is information-rich but separated with predictable vertical rhythm (24–32px). The category and global CTA panels use slightly more padding and whitespace to stand out as focused decision points, with clear separation from adjacent content (32–48px gaps) in the white area.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows an F/Z pattern: from the top-left logo and navigation (outside this scope), down into the white content area where it first catches the top-left of the filter controls, then scans right across any category selector elements. From there, attention drops to the category grid, scanning across category tiles left-to-right, then down through rows. Once a category is understood/selected, the eye moves downward into the vertically stacked service cards. Finally, the flow culminates in the category-specific CTA panel and then terminates at the bold global CTA band at the bottom. Red accents mark active filters, selected categories, and primary buttons, anchoring key interaction points along this vertical journey.

layout.overall_description: Below the dark, compact header stack, the page opens into a wide white content area with a centered column, giving a calm, structured feel. At the top of this white band, a slim, horizontally oriented filter section lets visitors choose or refine service categories without dominating the view. Directly beneath, a structured grid of service categories fills the central portion of the page, arranged in two or three columns depending on screen width, each tile framed cleanly with black/grey lines and red highlights for active or hovered items. Scrolling down, the layout transitions into a single, centered column of service cards for the selected category, each card a solid rectangular block with left-aligned text and right- or bottom-aligned red calls to action, separated by consistent white space. After the list, a darker category-specific CTA panel spans the same content width, using a dark grey or black background and a red button to stand out against the white area. The page finishes with a full-width global CTA band that stretches edge-to-edge across the viewport in solid black or deep red, echoing the header’s strength and visually framing the content between strong brand-colored bars at the top and bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is conceptually positioned at the very top of the white content band, directly below the navigation bar but within the same centered max-width container as the rest of the page content. Under normal conditions it occupies no visible height and does not disrupt the vertical flow. When loading, it presents a semi-opaque white overlay confined to the main content column (roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, aligned centrally), dimming the category grid, service list, and CTAs beneath. A simple, flat, black or dark grey spinner or loading icon is centered both horizontally and vertically within the current viewport portion of the content area, maintaining the clean, serious aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural layer; when active, behaves as an overlay fixed within the bounds of the central content column, covering all interactive components below it without affecting overall layout dimensions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category state manager wraps the filter controls, category grid, service list, and category CTA within the white content area but remains completely invisible. It does not create its own band or spacing; instead, it sits logically at the same horizontal position as the other content, spanning the full width of the central container. All visible category-related components appear to the user as a continuous vertical stack, with state transitions (such as changing the selected category) updating the content in place without shifting the surrounding layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logical wrapper; occupies the full width of the centered column but adds no extra padding or margins and scrolls with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category filter controls sit at the top of the white content area, just below any page title/intro text, aligned to the same centered max-width container. They form a low-profile horizontal bar spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, centered, with 16–24px of vertical padding and minimal height compared to the main content blocks. Controls such as dropdowns, category pills, or tabs are arranged left-to-right, starting from the left edge of the content column and possibly extending toward the center, following the F-pattern. The background remains white or a very light grey, with a subtle bottom border or faint grey outline to distinguish the control strip from the content below. There is about 24–32px of vertical space beneath this bar before the category grid begins, reinforcing it as a distinct but compact utility zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column; it scrolls off the screen normally with the rest of the content, maintaining the classic, non-sticky feel of the design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services category grid is the first major content block under the filter controls and occupies the visual center of the upper portion of the white content area. It is contained within the same centered max-width column and spans nearly the full width of that column (90–100% of the container width), with generous left and right margins to the viewport edges. On desktop, categories are arranged in a 3-column grid (each tile about one-third of the container width minus gutters), with 16–24px horizontal and vertical gaps between tiles. On narrower screens, the grid collapses to two columns and eventually a single column while staying centered. Each category tile is a sharp-edged rectangle with flat colors: a white or light grey background, a subtle dark grey border, and a bold red accent (e.g., a top bar or left strip) indicating selection or hover. The grid block has 32–40px of top margin from the filter controls and approximately 32–48px of bottom margin before the service card list, allowing it to feel substantial and easy to scan without crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive grid within the fixed-width central container; tiles reflow to fewer columns on smaller viewports but remain aligned to the same vertical stack, scrolling as a normal content block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service card list sits directly beneath the category grid within the white content area, aligned to the same centered column. A simple section heading (if present) would be left-aligned at the top of this block, with the list itself spanning roughly 80–90% of the content column’s width, leaving slim side gutters for breathing room. The list is a vertical stack of service cards, each card occupying nearly the full width of this inner band so they read as a clean single column. There is approximately 32–40px of vertical space above the first card to clearly separate the list from the grid, and 24–32px of space between each subsequent card. The bottom of the list is set 32–48px above the category CTA panel to preserve the page’s structured rhythm. The overall density is moderate: enough cards can be visible on-screen at once to suggest the full scope of services while maintaining clear visual separation between each item.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list; scrolls naturally within the central column and expands vertically as more service cards are included, pushing the CTA panels further down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each service card is a rectangular, flat block within the service-card-list’s single column. It spans approximately 100% of the list’s inner width (about 80–90% of the main content column), with 16–24px of inner padding on all sides. The background is solid white, separated from the page background by a 1px light grey border, in keeping with the slightly classic aesthetic; shadows are minimal or absent. Text content is left-aligned: the service title appears at the top in a bold, slightly larger font, followed by a short description and key details arranged in simple stacked lines. A primary CTA (e.g., “View Details” or “Request Service”) is anchored either at the lower right of the card or as a full-width button at the bottom, using a solid red background with white text and sharp rectangular edges. There is 16–24px of vertical spacing between cards, ensuring that each card feels like a distinct, scannable unit without excessive whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level items within the list; they expand vertically with content but always maintain full-width alignment inside the list container and scroll with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category CTA panel is positioned immediately below the service-card-list, still inside the white content area and aligned to the central max-width container. It reads as a distinct band within that container: a horizontally oriented panel spanning roughly the full width of the content column with 24–32px of inner padding. To visually differentiate it from the cards above, the background is a dark grey or near-black, while the surrounding page background remains white. The primary text (e.g., a prompt to inquire about the selected category or schedule maintenance) is left-aligned, occupying the left two-thirds of the panel on desktop. On the right third, a prominent red rectangular button anchors the call-to-action, vertically centered within the panel. On smaller screens, the text and button stack vertically and remain centered. The panel is separated from the service cards above by about 32–40px of vertical space and from the global CTA band below by another 32–48px gap, giving it room to stand out as the category-specific decision point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static panel within the central column; it does not stick or float, but behaves as a normal block-level section that scrolls into view after the service list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global CTA panel is the final major element in the vertical stack, positioned at the bottom of the page content just above the site footer. Unlike the other components, it breaks out of the constrained white content area and stretches full-width across the viewport, echoing the header’s bold bands. The background is solid black or brand red, creating a strong horizontal bar that visually anchors the end of the page. Inside, content is centered within the same max-width container as above: a concise headline and supporting line are center- or slightly left-aligned, with one or two prominent red or white buttons (depending on background color) placed to the right of the text or centered beneath it for a balanced layout. The vertical height of this band is moderate, around 120–160px on desktop, with 24–32px padding top and bottom. There is a clear 32–48px white gap between the category CTA panel and this global CTA band, allowing it to read as a separate, site-wide invitation rather than part of the category-specific flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-width band fixed in the document flow; it scrolls into view at the bottom of the page and remains just above the footer, maintaining the design’s stacked-band hierarchy.

name: Service Detail
title: Service Detail
description: Displays details for a selected construction or engineering service and lets users submit a structured quote request tied to that service.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a specific service based on the serviceId URL parameter using getServiceDetail.
Show service-specific metrics such as typical budget range, timeline summary, and floor range when available.
Display selectable completion timeline options for the service using the timeline_options returned by getServiceDetail.
Provide a structured 'Request a Quote' form that captures project details and contact information, and submits via submitServiceQuoteRequest.
Show submission feedback (success or error) after a quote request is submitted, and keep this state in sync with URL parameters for back/forward navigation.
List related projects or case studies for the service using related_projects from getServiceDetail, each linking to a project detail page.
Provide navigation links to view all projects in the service’s sector and to estimate costs for this service type in the cost calculator, passing appropriate URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Viewing a service detail:
1) User navigates to service_detail.html with a serviceId parameter in the URL.
2) The page reads serviceId from the URL and calls getServiceDetail(serviceId).
3) While data is loading, a loading state is shown; if getServiceDetail returns an error or no service, an error state is displayed.
4) When data loads successfully, the page displays the service name, description, typical budget information, typical timeline summary, and any floor range.
5) If the service is marked as inactive (is_active is false), the page indicates that the service is not currently available and disables the quote form, while still allowing users to view historical information and related projects.

Selecting a timeline option for the project:
1) After service data loads, the timeline selector displays all timeline_options for the service.
2) If a timelineOptionId is present in the URL, that option is preselected; otherwise, the option where is_default is true is selected (if any), or the first available option is selected by fallback logic.
3) When the user changes the selected timeline option, the selected timelineOptionId and its label are stored in the form state.
4) Optionally, the selected timelineOptionId is written to the URL parameters so that reloading or sharing the link preserves the selection.

Submitting a service quote request:
1) User reviews the service information and scrolls to the 'Request a Quote' form.
2) The form is prefilled with the current serviceId and the currently selected timelineOptionId (if any) from the timeline selector.
3) User enters project_name, project_location, estimated_budget, number_of_floors (where applicable), and any additional_details, plus their contact_full_name, contact_email, and contact_phone.
4) On submit, client-side validation checks that required fields are filled and that numeric fields are parseable as numbers.
5) If validation fails, inline error messages are shown and submission is blocked.
6) If validation passes, the page calls submitServiceQuoteRequest with the current serviceId, selected timelineOptionId (if any), and all entered form data, including the label of the selected timeline option as desired_completion_timeline_text.
7) While the request is in progress, the submit button is disabled and a pending state is shown.
8) On successful response, the page reads success, quote_request_id, status, and message from the response, shows a success banner, clears or locks the form as desired, and updates the URL parameters (e.g., adding quote_request_id and quote_status) so that the success state is preserved on reload.
9) On error response (success is false or network failure), the page shows an error banner and keeps the form data so the user can retry.

Exploring related projects and sector projects:
1) In the related projects section, the user sees a list derived from related_projects returned by getServiceDetail.
2) When the user clicks a related project tile, the browser navigates to project_detail.html with projectId set to the clicked project’s project_id in the URL.
3) A contextual link allows the user to view all projects in the service’s sector. When clicked, it navigates to projects.html, adding a sector URL parameter derived from the service’s category via a configurable mapping.
4) These navigations rely entirely on URL parameters; no local storage is used.

Estimating this service type in the cost calculator:
1) The user clicks an 'Estimate this type of project' call-to-action associated with the current service.
2) The page derives a cost calculator project_type value based on the current service’s category or other service attributes via a configurable mapping (not hardcoded in this page).
3) The browser navigates to cost_calculator.html with the project_type parameter set accordingly so that the calculator can preconfigure itself for this service type.
interactions:
Initial data load:
- Interaction: Page load or URL change.
- Behaviour: The page reads serviceId (and optionally timelineOptionId and quote_status/quote_request_id) from the URL. If serviceId is present, it triggers getServiceDetail(serviceId). If serviceId is missing or invalid, an error message is shown and no API call is made.
- Result: Service details, timeline_options, and related_projects are populated into page state.

Timeline option selection:
- Interaction: User selects a timeline option (e.g., clicking a radio button or selecting from a dropdown).
- Behaviour: The selected timeline_option_id and its label are stored in the shared state used by the quote form. Optionally, the timelineOptionId parameter in the URL is updated to match the new selection.
- Result: The desired_completion_timeline_text field in the quote request payload is updated to the label of the selected timeline option.

Quote form input changes:
- Interaction: User types into project_name, project_location, estimated_budget, number_of_floors, contact fields, or additional_details.
- Behaviour: Each input change updates the in-memory ServiceQuoteRequest draft state. Simple validation (e.g., required fields, numeric fields) runs on blur or on submit.
- Result: The form always reflects the latest user input and is ready to be submitted via submitServiceQuoteRequest.

Quote form submission:
- Interaction: User clicks the form submit button.
- Behaviour: Client-side validation runs. If valid and the service is active, the component calls submitServiceQuoteRequest with the current field values and serviceId. If the service is inactive, the submission is blocked and an explanatory message is shown.
- Result: On success, a success state and message are shown, and the URL is updated with submission-related parameters. On failure, an error state is shown and the form remains editable.

Quote feedback banner dismissal:
- Interaction: User clicks a close/dismiss control on the success or error banner.
- Behaviour: The banner is hidden in local component state. Optionally, any quote_status parameter in the URL is removed or updated to reflect the dismissal.
- Result: The user can continue interacting with the page without persistent banners.

Related project navigation:
- Interaction: User clicks a related project tile.
- Behaviour: The page navigates to project_detail.html, appending projectId with the clicked project’s project_id.
- Result: The user is taken to that project’s detail page with state driven by the projectId URL parameter on the destination page.

Sector projects navigation:
- Interaction: User clicks the 'View all projects in this sector' link.
- Behaviour: The page determines the appropriate sector string for the projects.html page based on the current service’s category using a configurable mapping, then navigates to projects.html?sector={derivedSector}.
- Result: The user is taken to the projects listing filtered by that sector.

Cost calculator navigation:
- Interaction: User clicks 'Estimate this type of project' CTA.
- Behaviour: The page derives an appropriate project_type for the cost calculator based on the current service’s attributes using a configurable mapping, then navigates to cost_calculator.html?project_type={derivedProjectType}.
- Result: The user is taken to the cost calculator page, preconfigured for this type of service.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary page state is driven by URL parameters and API responses.

- Required URL parameter:
  - serviceId: Identifies which Service to display. On any change to serviceId, the page re-invokes getServiceDetail(serviceId) and re-renders. If serviceId is missing or invalid, the page shows a 'service not found' state instead of calling the API.

- Optional URL parameters (used for state persistence and deep-linking):
  - timelineOptionId: If present, after getServiceDetail returns timeline_options, the timeline selector attempts to match this value to a timeline_option_id and preselects it. If it does not match any option, the selector falls back to the default option logic.
  - quote_request_id: If present, the page treats the quote as recently submitted (for this service only) and may show a success or status banner. The value is typically set from the quote_request_id returned by submitServiceQuoteRequest.
  - quote_status: If present, indicates the last known status returned by submitServiceQuoteRequest (e.g., submitted or in_review). The page uses this to determine whether to show success, informational, or error messaging. This parameter is updated after each successful or failed submission.

The page state is the combination of:
  - Service data (Service) and related collections (ServiceTimelineOption, ProjectCaseStudy) from getServiceDetail.
  - Form draft state for ServiceQuoteRequest fields (not persisted in URL).
  - Submission result state (success/failure, message) persisted both in local component state and, when successful, in URL parameters (quote_request_id and quote_status) to support reload and deep linking.

No localStorage or other persistent client storage is used. All navigable or shareable state relies on URL parameters, and dynamic content relies solely on the response data from getServiceDetail and submitServiceQuoteRequest.

components.functionality: Reads serviceId from the URL, invokes getServiceDetail, manages loading and error states, and provides service, timeline_options, and related_projects data to child components.
Displays key information about the service, including its name, description, typical timeline summary, and any applicable floor range. Shows an inactive notice if service.is_active is false.

Shows service-specific metrics extracted from the Service model, such as typical budget minimum and maximum and timeline summary. Handles cases where some metrics may be missing by adjusting the display accordingly.

Displays available completion timeline options for the current service and allows the user to select one. Preselects an option based on URL parameter timelineOptionId or the option with is_default set to true. Exposes the selected timeline_option_id and its label to the quote form.

Collects and validates quote request information (project details and contact information) for the current service and submits it via submitServiceQuoteRequest. Integrates the timeline selection from timeline-selector and controls submission state.

Displays success or error messages after a quote submission based on the result of submitServiceQuoteRequest or URL parameters (quote_request_id and quote_status). Allows the user to dismiss the banner and optionally syncs that dismissal to the URL.

Renders the list of related projects or case studies returned as related_projects from getServiceDetail. Each item shows basic project information and is clickable to navigate to the corresponding project detail page.

Provides a link to view all projects in the sector associated with the current service. When clicked, derives a sector value from the Service category using a configuration mapping and navigates to projects.html with sector set in the URL.

Provides a call-to-action for users to estimate costs for this type of project. On click, derives a project_type suitable for the cost calculator from the current Service attributes using a configuration mapping and navigates to cost_calculator.html with that project_type parameter.

Keeps key interaction state synchronized with URL parameters, including serviceId, timelineOptionId, quote_request_id, and quote_status, so that the page can be reloaded or shared while preserving context.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: The page continues the stacked-band pattern from the mockup: the existing light grey utility bar, black header bar, and dark grey navigation strip sit at the top. Immediately beneath the nav, a white content band holds a single, centered content column. Within this column, components are arranged in a linear, top-to-bottom order: service header → metrics panel → timeline selector → quote feedback banner → quote form → related projects → bottom navigation links. Horizontal alignment remains mostly centered, with occasional left alignment for copy and right alignment for primary CTAs to preserve the F/Z eye pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page continues the stacked-band pattern from the mockup: the existing light grey utility bar, black header bar, and dark grey navigation strip sit at the top. Immediately beneath the nav, a white content band holds a single, centered content column. Within this column, components are arranged in a linear, top-to-bottom order: service header → metrics panel → timeline selector → quote feedback banner → quote form → related projects → bottom navigation links. Horizontal alignment remains mostly centered, with occasional left alignment for copy and right alignment for primary CTAs to preserve the F/Z eye pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header stack occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport as in the mockup. The main white content band fills the remaining 75–80%, with a max-width centered column (about 960–1120px) and generous side margins. Vertically, the top of the content band is fairly tight (24–32px padding before the first component) to keep the page feeling connected to the header. Inside the white area, key sections (header, metrics, form, related projects) are separated by 32–48px vertical gaps, with denser packing within each component (8–16px between sub-elements). The quote form and related projects occupy the bulk of the vertical space in the content area.
layout_strategies.content_density: Following the mockup, the informational elements near the top (service header, metrics, timeline selector) are relatively compact and efficient, minimizing vertical spread while remaining readable. The central interaction area—the quote form—is more spacious, with clear grouping, comfortable line spacing, and generous field spacing. The related projects section uses moderate density with card-like items separated by consistent gaps. Small utility components like the timeline selector and navigation links are compact, using 8–12px vertical padding. The overall sensation is structured and slightly compact above, opening into a more airy middle and lower section.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z-pattern: users enter at the top-left of the white content band with the service title, scan rightward across any header meta details, then drop their gaze down to the metrics panel and horizontally across the key stats. From there, the eye moves slightly down to the timeline selector options, then directly to the quote form, which is the main focal block. Any active quote feedback banner creates a strong horizontal bar just above the form, drawing attention before fields. Below the form, the flow continues downward to the related projects, scanning left-to-right across project cards, and finally to the bottom navigation links where the primary red cost-calculator CTA sits toward the right edge, reinforcing the diagonal movement toward the lower-right of the content area.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing header and navigation bands, a white content area spans full width but is visually anchored by a centered content column using an implicit 12‑column grid. At the top of this column, the service header presents the service title and brief summary in a single, compact block aligned mostly to the left, with ~32px of white space above and below. Immediately beneath, a metrics panel stretches across much of the column, forming a low, horizontal band of key figures that echoes the stacked strips of the site header but within the white field. The timeline selector sits just below the metrics, a compact horizontal control centered in the column. If present, a quote feedback banner forms a bold, low-height stripe extending across the column, clearly separated by small vertical gaps and visually bridging the informational top portion with the interaction-heavy middle. The quote form then dominates the central vertical space, a structured, rectangular block with subtle borders and clear left-aligned labels, occupying most of the column width while leaving comfortable side margins. Below the form, the layout opens again into the related projects list, laid out as a row or grid of project tiles centered in the column with even spacing. At the very bottom, two navigation links—one more subdued (sector projects) and one more prominent in red (cost calculator)—sit in a low-height horizontal area, aligned along the bottom of the content column, subtly guiding attention toward the right-hand primary CTA. Throughout, the page feels like a continuation of the header’s strong horizontal bands and high contrast, but within a calm, centered single-column structure suited to reading and form completion.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual most of the time. Structurally, it sits at the very top of the white content band, just inside the main content container and above the service header in the DOM. When active (e.g., initial load or refetch), it overlays the white content area with a semi-opaque light overlay that covers the entire centered content column from just below the dark grey navigation bar to just above the page footer. A simple centered spinner or progress indicator appears in the middle of the viewport, aligned to the center of the column both vertically and horizontally. The overlay respects the page’s max-width—darkening only the main content column, not the full browser width, to maintain the sense of a central panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-column overlay positioned over all other main content elements when active (fixed within the scrolling viewport or absolutely positioned within the content area), otherwise occupies no visual space and does not affect layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service header is the first visible component in the white content band beneath the dark grey navigation. It is centered within the main content column, spanning roughly 80–90% of the column width (about 9–11 of the 12 implied grid columns), with even left and right margins. The top of the service header sits about 32px below the navigation bar edge. Within this block, the service title is left-aligned and occupies the full width of the header area, with a slightly larger font size that visually anchors the start of the content. Any subtitle or short description sits directly under the title with 8–12px vertical spacing, maintaining a compact vertical footprint. Additional meta information (like service category or ID) can run on a single line beneath or to the right, but the overall height of this section remains modest, approximately 100–160px tall depending on text length. A vertical gap of about 24–32px separates the bottom of this header from the top of the service-metrics-panel, maintaining a clear but tight connection between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element participating in normal document flow; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The metrics panel sits immediately below the service header, aligned to the same centered content column. It forms a low, wide strip spanning roughly the full content column width (around 10–12 grid columns), visually akin to a mini-band inside the white area. The panel has a light grey or slightly tinted background to subtly distinguish it from the white page and echo the utility bar’s tone, with 16–24px of internal vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding on both sides. Inside, key metrics (e.g., average project duration, typical budget, safety rating) are arranged horizontally in 2–4 evenly spaced columns. Each metric block is vertically oriented with a small label above a larger value, both left-aligned within their column. The overall height of the panel is compact—roughly 120–180px—emphasizing breadth over depth. A vertical gap of around 24px separates the bottom of this panel from the timeline-selector below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal flow. On narrower viewports, its internal columns stack vertically while maintaining the same outer block width and centered alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The timeline selector is positioned directly below the metrics panel, still within the same centered column, and appears as a compact horizontal control. It is narrower than the full content width, centered so that it occupies roughly 60–70% of the column (about 7–8 grid columns), leaving visible white space on both sides. There is about 16–24px of vertical separation from the metrics panel above.
