Its options (e.g., '3 months', '6 months', '12 months') are arranged in a single row of buttons or tabs, each with about 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, following the compact button pattern from the mockup. The active option uses the brand red as a bottom border or filled background, echoing the red active nav item. The control’s overall height is low, and there is a 24–32px vertical gap below it before the quote-feedback-banner or quote form, preserving the sense that this selector is a light-touch filter before the heavier form area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static component in the main flow, optionally becoming horizontally scrollable on very narrow screens while remaining centered within the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service quote form is the dominant interactive block in the main content area and sits below the timeline selector and any quote-feedback-banner. Structurally, it is a rectangular panel centered within the content column, spanning about 80–90% of the column width (around 9–11 grid columns), with clear, straight edges and a subtle border or light grey outline consistent with the classic, no-frills aesthetic. The top of the form sits approximately 24–32px below the element above (timeline-selector or quote-feedback-banner). Inside, form fields are arranged in one or two columns depending on screen width: on desktop, key fields can be organized into two balanced vertical columns (each ~5–6 grid columns wide) with 16–24px horizontal spacing between them; on smaller screens, they stack into a single column. Labels are left-aligned above fields with 4–8px gaps, and vertical spacing between fields is about 16–24px to avoid a cramped feeling. Grouped sections (e.g., contact details, project details, budget info) are separated by 24–32px and potentially a subtle divider line. The submit button area is aligned toward the bottom-right of the form, with the primary submit button styled in brand red and padded (10–12px vertical, 20–24px horizontal), echoing navigation button sizing. There is about 32–48px of vertical white space below the form before the related-projects-list to visually separate the completion action from follow-up content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the normal layout. Its internal fields reflow responsively, but the outer form panel remains centered and non-sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote feedback banner occupies a shallow, full-width strip within the main content column and appears directly above the service-quote-form when visible. It aligns with the same horizontal boundaries as the form, spanning roughly 80–90% of the content column and maintaining a centered alignment. The top of the banner sits about 16–24px below the timeline-selector, and the form’s top edge is 16–24px beneath the banner, creating a tight but distinct stack. Visually, it uses a high-contrast colored background—red for errors, green or dark grey for success or info—with white or very light text, mirroring the strong band-like color treatments in the header. The banner height remains compact (around 56–80px), with 12–16px vertical padding and text aligned left inside the band. An icon may appear to the far left, while an optional close/dismiss control is aligned to the right edge, preserving the horizontal scan pattern. When hidden, it occupies no vertical space, allowing the form to move up.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in place within the normal flow; no fixed or sticky behavior. Its presence dynamically shifts the vertical position of the quote form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related projects list sits below the quote form in the same centered content column, beginning about 40–48px beneath the form’s bottom edge to create a clear separation between the primary conversion area and exploratory content. A simple left-aligned section heading (e.g., 'Related Projects') anchors the top of this block, with 8–12px spacing between the heading and the first row of projects. Project items are arranged horizontally in a 2–3 column grid depending on available width: on large viewports, three project cards span the width of the column (each ~3–4 grid columns wide with 16–24px gaps between them), while on narrower screens they drop to two or one per row. Each project card is a simple rectangle with a thumbnail image on top and short text below, maintaining the flat, rectangular, no-frills look. The list’s overall width matches the main column (about 10–12 grid columns), and its vertical height can extend significantly if there are multiple rows, but a consistent vertical gap of 24–32px separates the last row of projects from the bottom navigation links beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block grid within the normal flow; cards wrap to new lines responsively while the section stays centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sector projects link is placed at the bottom of the main content column, directly beneath the related-projects-list. It shares a horizontal row with the cost-calculator-cta, forming a low-height utility strip that visually closes the content area. The strip begins about 24–32px below the related-projects-list. Within this strip, the sector-projects-link is left-aligned, appearing as a text-based navigation link or understated button with a dark grey or black label and perhaps a subtle underline or right-pointing chevron. It occupies the left third of the content column, leaving central space mostly empty and creating visual tension that draws the eye diagonally toward the more prominent CTA on the right. Vertical padding for this link area is minimal (8–12px), keeping it compact and in line with the mockup’s utility elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline/block element within the bottom navigation row; it does not stick or float and simply aligns left in its row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cost calculator CTA shares the same bottom row as the sector-projects-link but is aligned to the far right edge of the centered content column, reinforcing the F/Z-pattern by placing a strong action at the lower-right. It appears as a button-style link in the brand red, with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, matching the prominence of the main site’s primary buttons. The button sits on the same baseline as the sector-projects-link, with enough horizontal spacing between them (the middle third of the column) to avoid visual crowding. This CTA’s width is determined by its label text, but visually it occupies the rightmost 2–3 grid columns. A small amount of white space (24–32px) is left below this row before the beginning of any global footer, preserving a clean edge at the bottom of the content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned block within the bottom navigation row. It remains in normal flow and does not exhibit sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is invisible in the UI and sits logically near the main interactive elements—conceptually tied to the timeline-selector, quote-feedback-banner, and service-quote-form—but it does not render any visible block. It ensures that selections (like chosen timeline options or form submission states) are reflected in the URL, but from a layout perspective, it occupies no width or height and does not alter the visual structure of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely behavioral, not visual. It has no spatial behavior and does not affect document flow or positioning.

name: Projects & Case Studies
title: Projects & Case Studies
description: Browse, filter, search, sort, and compare completed construction and engineering projects and case studies, with deep-linkable filter states via URL parameters.

core_features: Load dynamic filter options (sectors, states, completion year range, budget range) using getProjectFilterOptions and render them in a filter sidebar.
Initialize page filters, search term, and sort order from URL parameters (sector, location_state, min_completion_year, max_completion_year, min_budget, max_budget, sort_by, search_query).
Search and filter project case studies using searchProjectCaseStudies based on the current filter state, search term, and sort option.
Display search results as project cards showing key information and linking to project_detail.html with projectId in the URL.
Allow users to add or remove projects from a comparison list using addProjectToComparison and removeProjectFromComparison, and display the current list using getProjectComparisonList.
Provide a comparison bar/panel so users can see selected projects side-by-side and navigate to each project’s detail page.
Support deep-linking by reflecting the current filter, search, and sort state in the URL, so the same view can be restored or shared.
user_workflows: Workflow: Landing on a pre-filtered projects view from another page. 1) User clicks a link to projects.html from another page (e.g., services.html) with URL parameters such as sector and location_state. 2) On load, the page parses URL parameters and initializes internal state (selected sector, state, completion year range, budget range, search_query, sort_by). 3) The page calls getProjectFilterOptions to populate filter controls and align their initial values with the parsed URL state. 4) The page calls searchProjectCaseStudies with the URL-derived filter and sort parameters. 5) When results are returned, project cards are rendered in the results grid, and the filter controls show the pre-applied filters.

Workflow: User adjusts filters and refreshes the project list. 1) User modifies one or more filters in the filter sidebar (e.g., changes sector or adjusts completion year/budget ranges). 2) User clicks an 'Apply filters' action in the filter sidebar. 3) The page’s state manager updates URL parameters to reflect the new filters (adding or updating sector, location_state, min_completion_year, max_completion_year, min_budget, max_budget as needed; removing parameters for cleared filters). 4) The page calls searchProjectCaseStudies again with the updated parameters. 5) The results grid updates to show the filtered project list, and if no results are returned, an appropriate empty-state message component displays.

Workflow: User searches by keyword and changes sorting. 1) User enters a keyword into the search input in the search bar. 2) User submits the search form. 3) The state manager updates the search_query URL parameter (or removes it if the query is cleared). 4) The page calls searchProjectCaseStudies with the current filters, sort_by, and search_query. 5) When the user changes the sort option in the sort control, the state manager updates the sort_by URL parameter. 6) The page calls searchProjectCaseStudies again with the updated sort_by while keeping current filters and search_query. 7) Results in the grid reorder according to the new sort option.

Workflow: User opens a project detail from the list. 1) User scans the project results grid and clicks on a project card (any non-comparison interactive area of the card). 2) The click handler reads the project_id associated with that card. 3) The browser navigates to project_detail.html with projectId set in the query string (e.g., project_detail.html?projectId=<project_id>). 4) The project detail page then handles loading full details for that project.

Workflow: User builds and manages a comparison list. 1) On page load, the page calls getProjectComparisonList to retrieve any existing comparison list for the user/session and displays it in the comparison bar/panel. 2) In the results grid, each project card shows a comparison toggle control. 3) When the user clicks the toggle for a project not yet in the list, the page calls addProjectToComparison with the projectId. 4) On a successful response, the comparison bar and card states update using the returned projects array (e.g., the toggle appears active for projects in the list). 5) If the backend responds with success=false and a message (e.g., due to a limit), that message is displayed non-disruptively and the UI does not mark the project as selected. 6) When the user clicks the comparison toggle for a project already in the list, the page calls removeProjectFromComparison with that projectId. 7) On success, the comparison bar updates to remove that project and card toggle resets. 8) At any time, the user can open a comparison panel from the comparison bar to view a side-by-side comparison of all selected projects using fields returned by getProjectComparisonList (name, sector, project_type, location, completion_year, budget).

Workflow: User navigates to related services from the projects page (if available). 1) In a sidebar or CTA area, the user sees one or more 'View related services' links. 2) Each link is associated with a serviceId provided by server-side rendering or page configuration (not fetched via this page’s interfaces). 3) When the user clicks a link, the browser navigates to service_detail.html with serviceId in the URL (e.g., service_detail.html?serviceId=<serviceId>).
interactions: Changing a sector filter option triggers an internal filter state update in the filter sidebar and enables the 'Apply filters' action without immediately calling searchProjectCaseStudies until the user explicitly applies.

Adjusting completion year or budget range controls updates the local filter state; when 'Apply filters' is triggered, URL parameters for min_completion_year, max_completion_year, min_budget, and max_budget are updated and searchProjectCaseStudies is called.

Clicking 'Reset filters' in the filter sidebar clears all filter controls to their default values, removes corresponding URL query parameters, and triggers searchProjectCaseStudies with only remaining (if any) URL-derived search_query and sort_by.
Typing in the search input updates a local search term; submitting the search form sets or clears the search_query URL parameter and calls searchProjectCaseStudies with current filters and sort_by.
Choosing a new sort option in the sort control updates the sort_by URL parameter and triggers a new searchProjectCaseStudies call to reorder results.
Clicking anywhere on a project card’s main body (excluding the comparison toggle) triggers navigation to project_detail.html with the card’s project_id in the projectId URL parameter.

Toggling the comparison control on a project card calls addProjectToComparison or removeProjectFromComparison depending on the card’s current comparison state; on success, the comparison bar and card UI states refresh based on the returned projects from the interface response or from a follow-up getProjectComparisonList call.

Opening the comparison panel from the comparison bar expands a detailed comparison view of all projects currently in the comparison list; closing it collapses the view without altering the comparison list.
If searchProjectCaseStudies returns an empty array, the results grid hides project cards and shows an empty-state message with an option to clear filters or adjust search criteria.

Using browser back/forward navigation changes the URL parameters; the page listens for URL changes, re-parses parameters, updates filter/search/sort UI, and re-calls searchProjectCaseStudies to keep the view consistent with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The URL query string is the authoritative representation of filter, search, and sort state for this page, except for the comparison list which is owned by the backend via getProjectComparisonList/addProjectToComparison/removeProjectFromComparison. On initial load, the page reads URL parameters sector, location_state, min_completion_year, max_completion_year, min_budget, max_budget, sort_by, and search_query. These values initialize the internal state and the UI of filter controls, search input, and sort dropdown. The page then calls getProjectFilterOptions to retrieve dynamic ranges and option lists, and adjusts control bounds and labels accordingly, preserving selected values if they fall within the returned ranges. After filters are initialized, the page calls searchProjectCaseStudies using the current in-memory state derived from URL parameters; any parameter that is not present in the URL is passed as undefined to the interface. When the user changes filters, search term, or sort order and applies changes (e.g., via 'Apply filters' or search submit), the state manager updates the URL query string by adding or updating the relevant parameters; if a value is cleared, its parameter is removed from the URL. After each URL update, searchProjectCaseStudies is called with arguments that reflect the new URL state. Browser navigation events (back/forward) are handled by listening to URL changes; on such changes, filters/search/sort UI are re-synced from the URL and searchProjectCaseStudies is re-run. The comparison list state is not stored in the URL; it is fetched on load via getProjectComparisonList and updated solely through addProjectToComparison and removeProjectFromComparison responses.

components.functionality: Parses URL query parameters on load and on history changes, initializes filter/search/sort state, keeps the URL synchronized with user actions, and orchestrates calls to searchProjectCaseStudies when state changes.

Renders sector and state selectors plus completion year and budget range controls using dynamic options from getProjectFilterOptions. Collects user filter selections, exposes them to the state manager, and provides 'Apply filters' and 'Reset filters' actions.

Provides a free-text search input and submit/clear actions. On submit, notifies the state manager to update the search_query URL parameter and re-run searchProjectCaseStudies with the current filter and sort state.

Displays a set of sort options for project results (e.g., by completion date or budget as defined in configuration). When the user selects an option, it updates the local sort state and informs the state manager to set the sort_by URL parameter and trigger searchProjectCaseStudies.

Displays the list of projects returned by searchProjectCaseStudies as a grid or list of project-card components. Handles loading state, empty-state messaging, and passes project data and comparison status to each card.

Represents a single project result with core attributes from searchProjectCaseStudies (project_id, name, sector, project_type, location_city, location_state, completion_year, budget, thumbnail_url). The main card click navigates to project_detail.html with projectId; a separate comparison toggle reflects whether the project is currently in the comparison list and triggers add/remove actions.

Shows a summary of projects currently in the comparison list using data from getProjectComparisonList (e.g., project names and key stats). Provides controls to open the full comparison panel, remove individual projects from comparison, and optionally navigate directly to a project from the bar.

Displays a detailed side-by-side comparison of all projects in the comparison list, using fields provided by getProjectComparisonList such as name, sector, project_type, location_city, location_state, completion_year, and budget. Allows removing projects from comparison and navigating to project_detail.html for each compared project.

Listens to the results from searchProjectCaseStudies and, when the returned array is empty, hides the project grid and displays a contextual message encouraging the user to adjust filters or search. Provides a shortcut action to clear filters and notify the state manager to update the URL and re-run the search.

Optionally renders one or more links that navigate to service_detail.html for services related to the currently visible projects or page context. Each link includes a serviceId provided by server-side configuration so that clicking it takes the user to service_detail.html with serviceId in the query string.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Retain the stacked, full-width band structure from the mockup (utility bar → main header → navigation → content). The Projects & Case Studies page lives in the white content band below the dark navigation bar. Inside this white band, use a centered max-width container aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. Within that container, arrange content in a top‑to‑bottom flow: page title and controls (search + sort) at the top, then a two-column layout where a left filter sidebar sits beside a right project results area. Comparison UI sits near the bottom of the viewport/content flow, and a related services CTA closes the page.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Retain the stacked, full-width band structure from the mockup (utility bar → main header → navigation → content). The Projects & Case Studies page lives in the white content band below the dark navigation bar. Inside this white band, use a centered max-width container aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. Within that container, arrange content in a top‑to‑bottom flow: page title and controls (search + sort) at the top, then a two-column layout where a left filter sidebar sits beside a right project results area. Comparison UI sits near the bottom of the viewport/content flow, and a related services CTA closes the page.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the familiar header stack occupying the top ~20–25% of the viewport. The Projects page content occupies the remaining ~75–80% with a generous white background. Within the main content, the top control area (title, search, sort) uses about the top 15–20% of the content band, the filter sidebar takes roughly 25–30% of the content width on the left, and the project results grid occupies the remaining 70–75% on the right. The comparison bar is a low‑height horizontal band near the bottom of the viewport or just above the footer, spanning the full content width. When opened, the comparison panel uses roughly the lower 35–45% of the viewport. Vertical spacing between major groups (controls, filters+results, comparison, related CTA) follows the 32–48px rhythm from the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Mirror the mockup’s pattern: relatively dense and information‑rich near the top controls (title, search, sort, filter labels are closely stacked with 8–16px gaps), transitioning to more spacious presentation in the main results grid (cards with clear separation and white space between them). The filter sidebar is moderately dense but legible, using compact vertical spacing to fit multiple filters. The comparison bar is compact and utility‑focused; the comparison panel is more airy, giving each compared project enough room to be scanned horizontally. The related services CTA at the bottom is more open, with larger vertical padding and clear separation from preceding content.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F/Z pattern from the mockup. After scanning the global header and nav, the eye lands on the page title at the top-left of the white content band, then moves right across the search bar and sort control. From there, the flow drops diagonally down to the left filter sidebar, then sweeps to the right into the project results grid where cards are scanned in rows from left to right. Selected items draw attention toward the comparison bar at the bottom, which in turn leads the eye into the comparison panel when opened. The related services CTA at the very bottom acts as a closing visual anchor. Red highlights and black text are used sparingly to emphasize active filters, selected comparison items, and primary CTAs.

layout.overall_description: Below the dark, full-width navigation band, the page opens into a clean white content area centered within the viewport. A max-width container, aligned to a 12-column grid, holds all Projects & Case Studies content. At the very top of this white band, a left-aligned page title and supporting text establish context, while a broad search bar stretches across the upper center and a compact sort dropdown sits to the upper right, echoing the horizontal emphasis of the mockup’s header. Directly beneath these controls, the layout resolves into a stable two-column structure: a tall, medium-width filter sidebar on the left and a wider results area on the right. The results area presents project cards in clear rows, with comfortable white margins around the grid, similar to the large central image in the original mockup. As users select projects to compare, a discreet horizontal comparison bar appears near the bottom of the viewport, spanning the same centered width as the main content. When activated, a comparison panel slides up from the bottom, overlaying the lower portion of the page while still respecting the strong horizontal banding. The page ends with a generously spaced related services CTA block, centered and clearly separated from the results above, maintaining the classic, high-contrast corporate aesthetic throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager does not occupy any visible space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits behind the entire page, tying together the search bar, sort control, filter sidebar, results grid, and comparison components. It updates and reads URL parameters whenever the user interacts with filters, search, sort, or comparison features, but it never appears as a visual element or shifts any spatial relationships.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, non-layout component; it is logically global to the page and has no rendered footprint, positioning, or size.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project filter sidebar appears in the main white content band, starting just below the search/sort control row. It is anchored to the left side of the centered content container, occupying approximately 3–4 out of 12 grid columns (about 25–30% of the content width). Vertically, its top aligns with the top of the project results grid on the right, creating a unified horizontal starting line. The sidebar stretches downward as needed with filter groups stacked vertically, using compact 8–16px spacing between group labels and controls to keep it efficient but readable. There is a consistent 32–40px padding at the top and sides within the sidebar’s white area to keep content from touching edges, and a clear right-hand gutter (16–24px) separates it from the project results grid. On taller pages, the filter sidebar can extend beyond the initial viewport height; its content scrolls naturally with the page. On larger screens, the sidebar may use a sticky behavior so that its top remains visible as the user scrolls the results, mirroring the mockup’s preference for keeping critical utilities near the top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column, with optional sticky top offset so that the upper part of the sidebar remains in view as the user scrolls the results area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project search bar is located at the top of the white content area, just below the dark navigation band and page title. It is center-aligned within the max-width container but visually anchored to the left to support the F-pattern: the left edge of the search input aligns with the left edge of the filter sidebar and main content text. The search bar spans roughly 60–70% of the content width, occupying about 7–8 of the 12 grid columns. Its vertical height is modest but substantial enough to feel like a primary control, with 12–16px internal vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. There is ~16–24px of vertical space above it (from the page title) and ~16px below it before the sort control row aligns to its right. The search input is a simple horizontal field with a subtle border, echoing the flat, rectangular visual style, and may include a search icon on the right side for clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top of the main content band; it scrolls away with the content and does not stick once the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project sort control is positioned on the same horizontal row as the search bar, aligned to the far right of the centered content container. It occupies roughly 2–3 of the 12 grid columns (about 15–25% of the content width) and sits flush with the top edge of the search bar so that their baselines align, creating a single cohesive control band. There is a narrow horizontal gap (16–24px) between the right end of the search bar and the left edge of the sort control on larger screens. The control itself is a compact dropdown or segmented selector with about 8–12px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding, matching the moderate density of header utilities from the mockup. Vertically, there is approximately 24–32px space beneath this control row before the two-column filter-and-results section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored to the top-right of the main content container; it scrolls away with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project results grid sits to the right of the filter sidebar within the same white content band. It begins directly below the search/sort controls and horizontally aligns so that its top edge matches the top of the filter sidebar. The grid occupies approximately 8–9 of the 12 grid columns (about 70–75% of the content width), leaving a consistent vertical gutter (16–24px) between it and the sidebar. Within this area, project cards are arranged in a regular grid, typically 2 cards per row on standard desktop widths, with each card taking about half of the results area’s width minus gutter space. There is about 24–32px vertical spacing between rows of cards and 16–24px horizontal spacing between columns, creating a structured yet moderately airy feel similar to the large central image separation in the original mockup. The results grid can extend well below the initial viewport, forming the main scrolling area of the page. At the bottom of the grid, there is a clear 32–48px vertical gap before any pagination controls and the related services CTA, maintaining distinct visual sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primary scrolling region of the page, expanding vertically as more projects are listed; it remains right-aligned to the main content container and maintains its column relation with the filter sidebar.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each project card is a rectangular tile inside the project results grid, echoing the classic, sharp-edged aesthetic from the mockup. A card typically occupies about 45–48% of the results grid width in a two-column layout, allowing for horizontal gutters between cards. Vertically, cards are moderately tall, with a landscape thumbnail image occupying the top 40–50% of the card height, similar in proportion to the large central image in the mockup but scaled down. Below the image, project title, location, key metrics, and a short description stack in a single column with 8–16px vertical spacing. Internal padding around the card’s content is about 16–24px on all sides. Cards sit on the plain white content background with a subtle border or shadow to separate them from each other while staying consistent with the flat corporate style. Selected-for-comparison cards use a red or dark accent (border or corner tag) to visually tie into the brand and clearly signal their state. Cards are clickable as whole blocks, with hover states that slightly increase contrast or border strength without breaking the minimal style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Repeating unit within the results grid; cards are static in the flow, maintaining equal height rows as much as content allows, and reflow responsively into fewer columns on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison bar appears as a low-height, full-width band within the white content area once at least one project is marked for comparison. It is horizontally centered with the main content container, aligning exactly with the edges of the filter sidebar and results grid. By default, it sits near the bottom of the viewport, overlapping the lower portion of the content when sticky, or just above the page footer when scrolled to the end. Its height is modest—roughly 56–72px—matching the compact utility band feel seen in the mockup’s top bars. Inside, there is 16–24px horizontal padding, with selected project thumbnails or titles left-aligned in a tight row and a primary red “Compare” button aligned to the right. The bar uses a darker grey background (or a light grey with a strong top border) to create a distinct horizontal stripe, akin to the navigation band, making it visually separate from the main white content while still fitting the brand.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the bottom edge of the viewport when active, staying visible as the user scrolls through the project results. When the user reaches the bottom of the page, it rests just above the footer, maintaining the same centered width as the content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison panel is a larger overlay that appears when the user activates the Compare action from the comparison bar. It slides up from the bottom of the viewport as a full-width panel but remains horizontally constrained to the same max-width content container as the rest of the page, centered in the viewport. The panel occupies roughly the lower 35–45% of the viewport height, creating a strong horizontal band reminiscent of the header structure but in the lower portion of the screen. Its background is white with a clear top border in black or red to visually anchor it. Inside, compared project cards or columns are laid out side-by-side in a tight grid, each column using around 25–33% of the panel’s width depending on how many items are compared. There is about 24–32px of padding around the interior edges and 16–24px between comparison columns. A close button is aligned to the top-right inside the panel, and a summary title/label is aligned top-left. The underlying page content remains partially visible above and behind the panel, but dimming is minimal to maintain the straightforward, corporate feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed, slide-up overlay anchored to the bottom of the viewport when open. It does not scroll with the page; instead, its internal content can scroll vertically if needed. Closing the panel returns focus to the underlying page at the same scroll position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state message appears in the main results area when no projects match the current filters or search criteria. It replaces the project results grid content on the right while the filter sidebar remains visible on the left. The message is centered within the results column both horizontally and, where possible, vertically within the visible portion of the white content area. It occupies roughly 50–60% of the results column width, leaving generous white space to the left and right. The message includes an icon or simple graphic at the top, followed by a brief headline and explanatory text stacked with 8–16px spacing. There is about 32–48px of clear space above and below the message block to emphasize its importance and keep the area from feeling cluttered. A link or button to reset filters or broaden the search appears below the text, aligned center, tying back to the brand’s red accent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block that appears in place of the results grid items; it scrolls with the main page content, maintaining its centered position within the results column until dismissed by changing filters/search.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related services CTA sits at the bottom of the main white content band, beneath the project results grid (or empty state) and any pagination. There is a clear 48–64px vertical gap between the last row of project cards and this CTA block, signaling a transition from browsing to next-step navigation. The CTA is centered within the same max-width content container, spanning roughly 70–80% of the width with ample left and right margins. Inside, a short heading and supporting sentence are stacked vertically at the top, left-aligned, with a row of prominent link-style buttons or pill-shaped items below, each representing a related service or key offering. These buttons are arranged in a horizontal row that can wrap to multiple lines, with 16–24px horizontal spacing and 12–16px vertical spacing between them. The block has slightly more vertical padding (24–32px top and bottom) than the surrounding content, echoing the mockup’s more open treatment for footer-adjacent content. A subtle top border or light grey background band can be used to distinguish it from the project list while preserving the overall flat, corporate aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section anchored at the bottom of the main content flow; it scrolls into view after the user reaches the end of the project results and remains just above the site footer.

name: Project Detail
title: Project Detail
description: Displays full details for a single project case study, allows users to add or remove projects from a session-based comparison list, and surfaces similar projects for further exploration.

core_features: Load and display full details for a specific project case study based on the projectId URL parameter using getProjectCaseStudyDetail.
Show project narrative, key metrics (location, sector, project type, budget, size, completion timing, client), and associated imagery.
Allow the user to add or remove the current project from a session-based comparison list using addProjectToComparison and removeProjectFromComparison.
Show current comparison list status (including count and which projects are already in the list) using getProjectComparisonList.
Allow comparison list membership to be managed for the current project and for similar projects (add/remove via comparison actions).
Provide navigation to similar projects returned by getProjectCaseStudyDetail.similar_projects by linking to project_detail.html with the appropriate projectId.
Provide navigation back to the main projects listing (projects.html).
Provide navigation to the cost calculator page (cost_calculator.html) pre-filtered with a project_type parameter derived from the current project.
user_workflows:
View a project detail from a listing:
1. User clicks a project link on projects.html, index.html, or service_detail.html; browser navigates to project_detail.html?projectId={id}.
2. On page load, the project-detail-loader component reads projectId from the URL and calls getProjectCaseStudyDetail(projectId).
3. When data is returned, project header, metrics, description, gallery, and similar projects components bind to the project and similar_projects data.
4. In parallel or immediately after, project-comparison-context calls getProjectComparisonList() to populate comparison status (including whether the current project is in the list and how many projects are currently selected).

Add the current project to the comparison list:
1. User clicks the "Add to comparison" control in the project-comparison-toolbar.
2. The toolbar triggers addProjectToComparison(projectId).
3. On success, the component updates: (a) local flag indicating the current project is in the comparison list, using response.success and/or updated list; (b) comparison count and list via the returned projects array; (c) button label / state (e.g., toggled to a remove action).
4. If the interface returns a message, it is displayed in a non-blocking status area (e.g., below the button).

Remove the current project from the comparison list:
1. User clicks the "Remove from comparison" control in the project-comparison-toolbar (visible only when the current project is already in the list).
2. The toolbar calls removeProjectFromComparison(projectId).
3. On success, the component updates comparison list state (removing this project) and toggles the control back to an "Add to comparison" state.
4. Any message from the API is displayed to the user.

Manage comparison list items from the comparison status panel:
1. User opens or views the comparison-status-panel showing projects in the current comparison list, sourced from getProjectComparisonList or from the latest add/remove response.
2. For any listed project, user can click a remove control next to it, which calls removeProjectFromComparison(listItem.project_id).
3. On success, the panel updates the list and count; if the removed project is the one currently displayed in detail, the project-comparison-toolbar also reflects that change (no longer marked as in comparison).

Add or remove a similar project to/from comparison:
1. In the similar-projects-list, each project card includes a comparison toggle control.
2. On click, the component determines if that project_id is already present in the current comparison state (from getProjectComparisonList or the last API response).
3. If not present, it calls addProjectToComparison(similarProject.project_id); on success, it adds that project to the local comparison state and updates its toggle state.
4. If present, it calls removeProjectFromComparison(similarProject.project_id); on success, it removes that project from the local comparison state and updates its toggle state.

Navigate to another project via similar projects:
1. User clicks the name, image, or main clickable area of a similar project.
2. Browser navigates to project_detail.html?projectId={similar_project.project_id} (either via full page load or client-side routing with pushState).
3. The new page (or updated route) re-runs the project-detail-loader workflow using the new projectId.

Navigate to cost calculator for a similar project type:
1. User clicks the "Estimate a similar project" CTA in the estimate-similar-cta component.
2. The component derives a cost_calculator project_type parameter from the current project.project_type using a configuration-based mapping function (no hardcoded values in the page logic) and generates a URL cost_calculator.html?project_type={mapped_value}.
3. Browser navigates to the generated cost_calculator.html URL.

Return to all projects:
1. User clicks the "Back to all projects" link in the project-main-header.
2. Browser navigates to projects.html (no additional parameters needed).
interactions: On initial load or when the projectId URL parameter changes, project-detail-loader calls getProjectCaseStudyDetail(projectId) and updates all bound components with the returned project and similar_projects data.
On initial load, project-comparison-context calls getProjectComparisonList() to populate the current comparison list and propagates this state to comparison-related components.
Clicking the "Add to comparison" action for the current project triggers addProjectToComparison(currentProject.id); on success, the UI updates button state, comparison count, and list.
Clicking the "Remove from comparison" action for the current project triggers removeProjectFromComparison(currentProject.id); on success, the UI updates button state and comparison list.

Clicking a comparison toggle action on a similar project card triggers either addProjectToComparison or removeProjectFromComparison for that similar project’s ID, depending on whether it is currently in the comparison list.
Clicking the name or image of a similar project triggers navigation to project_detail.html?projectId={that_project_id}.
Clicking a project listed in the comparison-status-panel (if configured as navigable) triggers navigation to project_detail.html?projectId={that_project_id}.

Clicking the "Estimate a similar project" CTA constructs a URL to cost_calculator.html with a project_type query parameter derived from the current project.project_type and navigates the browser to that URL.
Clicking the "Back to all projects" link navigates the browser to projects.html.

If getProjectCaseStudyDetail or any comparison interface call returns an error state, the corresponding component displays the returned message or a generic error message and may disable affected controls until the user retries or navigates away.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the projectId URL parameter. When the browser loads project_detail.html, the project-detail-loader reads projectId from the query string (for example, project_detail.html?projectId={id}). If projectId is missing or empty, the page can show a validation error and a link back to projects.html instead of attempting to call getProjectCaseStudyDetail. When projectId is present, the page calls getProjectCaseStudyDetail(projectId). The response populates the main project entity and its similar_projects. The response field is_in_comparison_list is used for the current project’s initial comparison state. Separately, getProjectComparisonList() retrieves the full current comparison list for the session; this list is authoritative for determining whether any project ID (current or similar) is included in comparison. UI components that manage comparison state subscribe to a shared comparison context based on this list. When a user performs addProjectToComparison or removeProjectFromComparison, the returned projects array replaces the comparison context list and updates any dependent UI (toolbar, status panel, similar project toggles). No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used; comparison state across pages is maintained on the server side and accessed exclusively through getProjectComparisonList, addProjectToComparison, and removeProjectFromComparison. When the user navigates between projects (either through similar project links or by direct deep link), the only required state transition is updating the projectId in the URL; a full reload or client-side route handler re-executes getProjectCaseStudyDetail and getProjectComparisonList for the new project. When navigating to cost_calculator.html, the project_type parameter is appended to the URL based on the current project.project_type via a configurable mapping, preserving state solely through query parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the projectId from the URL query string, calling getProjectCaseStudyDetail(projectId) to retrieve the main project detail and similar_projects, and triggering an initial load of the comparison list via getProjectComparisonList(). Distributes the loaded data to other components via a shared state or props.

Displays high-level project identifiers such as name, sector, project_type, and location. Provides a "Back to all projects" navigation link and optional secondary meta labels (e.g., completion year). Uses project data supplied by project-detail-loader.

Shows key project metrics including budget, size_sq_ft, completion_year/completion_date, and client_name if available. All values are read directly from the loaded ProjectCaseStudy entity and formatted by generic formatting utilities without hardcoded numbers.

Displays the project image_urls as a gallery or slideshow. Allows users to navigate between images. If no images are provided, the component can render a generic placeholder text state without referencing any specific static image.

Displays the project.description narrative text and may include additional derived content such as formatted location (from location_city, location_state, location_country) within the same section. Content binds solely to ProjectCaseStudy fields.

Maintains the current comparison list state for the session. On initialization, calls getProjectComparisonList() to populate the list. Exposes methods to update state after addProjectToComparison and removeProjectFromComparison calls, and provides derived helpers (e.g., isInComparison(projectId), getComparisonCount()). Broadcasts state changes to comparison-related components.

Provides comparison actions specific to the current project: shows current inclusion status (in/out of comparison list), displays the current comparison count, and exposes buttons to add or remove the displayed project from comparison. Uses project.id and is_in_comparison_list from getProjectCaseStudyDetail, reconciled with the global comparison state from project-comparison-context.

Displays a compact list or count of all projects currently in the comparison list. Each item shows minimal identifiers (such as name and location) and may include a remove control. Optionally, clicking on a project navigates to its detail page via project_detail.html?projectId={id}. All list content is driven by the ProjectComparisonList state; no items are statically defined.

Renders the similar_projects collection returned from getProjectCaseStudyDetail. Each list item shows basic project information (name, sector, project_type, location, budget, completion_year) and includes: (1) a click target that navigates to project_detail.html?projectId={project_id}, and (2) a comparison toggle that uses the global comparison state to show whether that project is in the comparison list and to add/remove it via the comparison interfaces.

Provides a call-to-action for estimating a similar project using the cost calculator. Reads project.project_type from the current ProjectCaseStudy and passes it into a configurable mapping function (defined outside this page) to obtain the appropriate cost_calculator project_type value. Constructs a navigation URL cost_calculator.html?project_type={mapped_type} and redirects the browser when clicked.

Handles and displays errors related to loading project details or updating the comparison list. Listens for error events from project-detail-loader and comparison actions. Shows error messages returned by interfaces where available, and can expose a retry action that re-calls the respective interface (getProjectCaseStudyDetail, getProjectComparisonList, addProjectToComparison, or removeProjectFromComparison as appropriate).

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing stacked utility/header/navigation bands, the Project Detail page uses a single, centered content column within a full-width white content band. Components are stacked vertically in a linear, top-to-bottom flow. The order within the white band is: project-main-header at the very top of the content column, followed by the large project-image-gallery as the main visual anchor, then the project-metrics-panel, the project-description-section, comparison-related elements (comparison-status-panel and comparison-toolbar), and finally the similar-projects-list with the estimate-similar-cta integrated into its header row. The project-detail-loader and project-error-state appear as overlays or replacements of this central column rather than separate bands, and the project-comparison-context is non-visual, simply governing state.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing stacked utility/header/navigation bands, the Project Detail page uses a single, centered content column within a full-width white content band. Components are stacked vertically in a linear, top-to-bottom flow. The order within the white band is: project-main-header at the very top of the content column, followed by the large project-image-gallery as the main visual anchor, then the project-metrics-panel, the project-description-section, comparison-related elements (comparison-status-panel and comparison-toolbar), and finally the similar-projects-list with the estimate-similar-cta integrated into its header row. The project-detail-loader and project-error-state appear as overlays or replacements of this central column rather than separate bands, and the project-comparison-context is non-visual, simply governing state.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header stack (utility bar, black brand bar, dark grey nav) occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport. The remaining 75–80% is the white content band. Within this band, the centered content column spans about 70–80% of the viewport width, capped at a max width around 1100–1200px, with generous left and right margins of white space. Above the fold, the project-main-header and project-image-gallery together take the majority of vertical space, with the gallery itself occupying roughly 40–50% of the visible content area. The project-metrics-panel and project-description-section occupy the middle of the page, with clear 32–48px vertical separation from adjacent blocks. The similar-projects-list takes the lower portion of the page, with enough vertical room to display at least one full row of project cards without feeling cramped. The comparison-toolbar, when visible, occupies a low-profile horizontal strip at the bottom of the viewport, overlaying the white content band but not the global header.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is compact at the very top (header and nav bands), while the main content is more spacious. The project-main-header is moderately compact, with tightly grouped title and meta information but comfortable padding inside the column. The project-image-gallery is spacious, with the image allowed to breathe horizontally and vertically. The project-metrics-panel is denser internally, using a card-like treatment that groups several data points with tighter internal spacing but maintains 24–32px outer padding. The project-description-section is text-heavy but uses generous line height and 32–48px vertical margins from surrounding blocks. Comparison controls (comparison-status-panel and comparison-toolbar) are compact, horizontal strips that pack information and CTA buttons into a relatively small vertical footprint. The similar-projects-list balances density and clarity by using a structured grid of cards with consistent gaps, feeling ordered rather than crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the user’s eye traverses the global header and navigation (F/Z pattern across logo, contact info, and nav), it drops into the white content band at the top-left where the project-main-header title sits. From there, the eye moves downward to the large, centered project-image-gallery, which serves as the primary focal point. Below the image, the eye moves across the project-metrics-panel in a left-to-right scan of key stats, then continues down into the project-description-section for deeper reading. As the user scrolls further, attention is drawn to the comparison-status-panel, which summarizes the comparison state near the lower-middle of the page, and then to the sticky comparison-toolbar at the bottom of the viewport, whose dark background and red CTAs echo the nav and brand colors. Finally, attention flows to the similar-projects-list near the page bottom, with its left-aligned section heading and a right-aligned red estimate-similar-cta, forming a secondary Z-pattern across the section header row before scanning down through the project cards.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing light grey utility bar, black brand header, and dark grey navigation, the page opens into a full-width white content band. In the center of this band sits a constrained content column, roughly 70–80% of the viewport width and capped at about 1100–1200px, with generous white margins on either side. At the top of this column, the project-main-header presents the project title and key identifiers in a compact block, visually tied to the navigation above through black typography and red accent lines or tags. Immediately beneath it, a wide, landscape-oriented project-image-gallery spans almost the full width of the content column and dominates the above-the-fold area as the primary visual, mirroring the large central image behavior from the mockup. A clearly separated project-metrics-panel sits just below the gallery as a horizontally oriented, card-like band that succinctly displays core project metrics. Further down, the project-description-section fills the column with narrative text, diagrams, or lists, with comfortable line height and generous white space above and below, giving the middle of the page an open, readable feel. As the user scrolls, a subtle comparison-status-panel appears within the flow just above the similar projects, providing a compact, slightly shaded summary of how many projects are in comparison. At the very bottom of the viewport, a dark, sticky comparison-toolbar hugs the lower edge, stretching across the main content area and echoing the dark grey of the navigation, with a bold red compare CTA on the right. Near the lower portion of the page, the similar-projects-list introduces a new section with a left-aligned heading and a right-aligned red estimate-similar-cta in the same row, followed by a grid of project cards arranged across the central column with consistent gaps. In error or loading states, a centered loader or error panel overlays this white content band, keeping the user’s focus anchored in the middle of the page. Overall, the layout feels structured and corporate, with strong horizontal bands, a clear vertical reading path, and black/red accents anchoring key actions and summaries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears within the white content band, centered both horizontally within the main content column and vertically within the visible portion of the content area. It overlays all other project-detail components with a semi-transparent white scrim that extends to the full width of the content column and at least the height of the initial viewport within the white band. The loading indicator itself (spinner and short status text) is placed at the geometric center of this overlay, maintaining at least 48px of clear space around it so it does not feel cramped. When active, it temporarily hides or dimly obscures the project-main-header, image gallery, and other sections beneath, keeping the user’s focus in the middle of the page rather than at the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay relative to the main content column; positioned absolutely or fixed within the white content band, centered, and only visible while data is loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-main-header is the first visible block inside the white content band, starting approximately 32px below the bottom edge of the dark grey navigation bar. It is centered horizontally within the main content column, spanning the full width of that column. Vertically, it occupies a relatively shallow height, roughly 10–15% of the main content area above the fold, with 24–32px of padding above and below its internal elements. The project title is left-aligned at the top of this block, aligning with the left edge of the content column, while secondary information (such as project category, location, or breadcrumbs) is positioned beneath the title or subtly aligned to the right within the same block, following an F-pattern across the top of the content. The background remains white to blend into the main content band, but strong black typography and red accent lines or tags anchor it visually to the corporate brand. A 32–40px vertical gap separates this header from the project-image-gallery below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in normal document flow, pinned to the top of the main content column below the global navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-metrics-panel sits directly beneath the project-image-gallery, separated by approximately 32px of white space. It spans the full width of the main content column and reads as a horizontally oriented card or band, about 15–20% of the visible content height on a typical desktop viewport. Inside, it uses a slightly tinted light grey or subtle boxed style to distinguish it from the plain white background while maintaining the corporate, flat aesthetic. Metrics are arranged in 2–4 columns within this panel, starting from the left edge and flowing right, encouraging a left-to-right scan that continues the Z-pattern established by the header and gallery. Each metric cluster has tighter internal spacing (8–16px between label and value) but the panel as a whole uses 24–32px padding on all sides to avoid feeling cramped. A 32–40px vertical gap separates this panel from the project-description-section below, ensuring the metrics read as their own summary block rather than part of the narrative text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in normal document flow, visually grouped as a single card-like band below the image gallery.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-image-gallery is positioned immediately below the project-main-header, with a vertical gap of approximately 32px. It is the dominant visual element within the white content band and spans nearly the entire width of the main content column, leaving just standard internal padding if needed for arrows or thumbnails. The main hero image area is landscape-oriented, occupying roughly 40–50% of the first viewport height for typical desktop screens, ensuring it feels large and central, similar to the news image in the mockup. The image is centered within the column; if there are thumbnails or navigation controls, they are compactly placed below or overlaid at the edges without significantly increasing the vertical footprint. Top and bottom white space around the gallery (24–32px) helps isolate it visually from the header above and metrics panel below, creating a strong, uninterrupted focal zone in the center of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in normal document flow, occupying a large, continuous horizontal slice of the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-description-section is placed directly beneath the project-metrics-panel, separated by 32–40px of vertical space. It extends across the full width of the main content column and can occupy a substantial vertical height, often 30–40% of the total page height or more, depending on content length. The section begins with a left-aligned heading or label that aligns with the left edge of the column, followed by paragraphs, bullet lists, or subheadings arranged in a single column of text. Line length is controlled by the content column width, keeping text comfortable to read, and line height is generous for a corporate site, with 16–24px spacing between lines. There is at least 32px of padding above the first line of text and 32–48px below the last paragraph before the next component, providing a clear sense of separation from the comparison and similar projects sections that follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in normal document flow, expanding vertically as needed based on content, while maintaining fixed column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-comparison-context functions as an underlying state manager and is not intended to have a prominent visual representation. If any visual footprint is needed (for example, to expose subtle messaging or hidden meta), it appears as a very minimal, single-line status or hidden container directly above or around the comparison-status-panel, occupying no more than a thin strip of vertical space (8–16px). It aligns with the left edge of the main content column and inherits the white background to avoid drawing attention away from primary content. In most cases, it remains invisible to the user and simply coordinates the behavior of the comparison-status-panel and comparison-toolbar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual or minimally visual state container that does not significantly affect layout; effectively exists in the DOM with negligible spatial impact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-comparison-toolbar appears as a low-profile, full-width horizontal strip anchored to the bottom of the viewport when there is at least one project in the comparison list. It spans the same max width as the main content column and is centered horizontally on the page, with the bar itself often stretching edge-to-edge within that column. The background is a dark grey or near-black, visually echoing the site’s navigation bar, with white text and a prominent red compare button positioned on the right side of the toolbar. Informational text and secondary actions are left-aligned within the bar, following a left-to-right reading order. Vertically, the toolbar’s height is compact (approximately 48–64px), leaving adequate screen real estate for the content above. There is a subtle drop shadow or clear separation line at its top edge to distinguish it from the white content behind as the user scrolls. On smaller screens, this bar may overlay the bottom portion of content, but always remains above the browser chrome, ensuring that the primary action is consistently visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed within the viewport at the bottom edge when active, overlaying the content band and remaining visible while the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison-status-panel is positioned within the normal content flow, typically just below the project-description-section and above the similar-projects-list, separated from each by about 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the main content column but has a relatively shallow height, roughly 10–15% of the viewport height or 48–96px, depending on content. This panel uses a very light grey background or a subtle border to distinguish it from the white page while remaining less visually heavy than the dark comparison-toolbar. Text is left-aligned, summarizing how many projects are currently in the comparison list and possibly naming them, with a small red link or button for adjusting the selection on the right. The panel serves as an informational checkpoint before users encounter the similar projects and the persistent bottom toolbar. It maintains 16–24px internal padding and keeps typography modest so it reads as a supporting element rather than a main call to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in normal document flow, acting as a compact, inline summary strip above the similar projects section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The similar-projects-list occupies the lower portion of the white content band, directly beneath the comparison-status-panel with a 32–40px vertical gap. It starts with a section heading row that spans the full width of the main content column: a left-aligned heading such as "Similar Projects" and, sharing the same horizontal row, the estimate-similar-cta aligned to the right (on smaller screens, the CTA stacks beneath the heading). Below this heading row, the list displays project cards in a grid-like layout within the confines of the single main column. On wider screens, cards are arranged in 2–3 columns per row, each card occupying roughly 30–45% of the column width with 24–32px gutters between them; on narrower screens, cards stack into a single column. Vertically, the similar-projects-list can occupy 30–40% of the main page height, depending on how many items are shown, with a comfortable 32–48px margin at the bottom separating it from the page footer or any subsequent content. Each card is rectangular with flat colors, simple iconography, and black text, maintaining the corporate feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in normal document flow, with internal responsive grid behavior for its child cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate-similar-cta is a prominent red button that shares the top row of the similar-projects-list header. Horizontally, it is aligned to the far right edge of the main content column, directly opposite the left-aligned "Similar Projects" heading, creating a balanced Z-pattern across this section header. The button’s width is modest (roughly 15–25% of the column width, depending on text length) and its height aligns with the established button sizing from the header and navigation (about 32–40px tall), using 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. There is at least 8–16px of space between the button and the heading row’s top and bottom edges, ensuring it feels comfortably placed, not cramped. On smaller screens, the CTA shifts to a new line directly under the heading, center- or left-aligned, with 16–24px of vertical spacing separating it from the heading text. The red fill and white text of the button echo the brand accent used elsewhere, making this CTA visually distinct in the lower part of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline-block element within the similar-projects-list header row, right-aligned on wide screens and stacking below the heading on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-error-state replaces or overlays the main content column within the white content band when an error occurs (such as a failed data load). It is centered horizontally within the same max-width content column and vertically within the visible portion of the content area. The error panel itself is a rectangular block occupying roughly 30–40% of the viewport height, with 32–48px of internal padding and a clear, high-contrast message in black text on a white or very light grey background, possibly framed by a thin red border or red error icon to align with the brand palette. Surrounding the error panel, there is generous whitespace on all sides (at least 48px top and bottom within the white band) so it feels intentionally placed and not crowded by other elements. During this state, underlying components like the project-main-header, image gallery, and metrics are either not rendered or visually suppressed behind a solid white background, ensuring the error message is the singular focal point within the content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay or replacement block centered within the white content band, occupying the main content column and temporarily removing other project-detail content from view.

name: Locations & Branch Finder
title: Locations & Branch Finder
description: Page for searching company branches by postal code, viewing nearby results sorted by distance, and navigating to a selected branch detail page to schedule a site visit.

core_features: Postal code search form that calls the searchBranchesByPostalCode interface with postal_code, country, max_distance_km, and sort_by parameters.
Automatic pre-population of the search form and optional auto-search based on URL parameters postal_code, country, max_distance_km, and sort_by.
Results list of branches returned from searchBranchesByPostalCode, including distance and key contact details, with client-side filtering to show only active branches.
Sorting controls that adjust the sort_by parameter (e.g., distance ascending) and re-trigger the searchBranchesByPostalCode interface.
Radius control to adjust max_distance_km, update the URL parameter, and re-run the search.
Clickable branch result cards that navigate to branch_detail.html with the selected branchId via URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Load the page with no URL parameters
1. User navigates to locations.html without query parameters.
2. The page reads URL parameters; none of postal_code, country, max_distance_km, sort_by are found.
3. The search form displays empty fields with default values (e.g., no postal code, default country and radius if defined in UI configuration).
4. No request to searchBranchesByPostalCode is made yet.
5. Page shows an idle state prompting the user to enter a postal code and submit the search.

Workflow: Load the page with a pre-defined search (deep link)
1. User opens locations.html with one or more URL parameters, for example: postal_code, and optionally country, max_distance_km, sort_by.
2. The controller component parses the query string and populates the search form fields with these values.
3. If postal_code is present, the controller automatically calls searchBranchesByPostalCode using postal_code, country, max_distance_km, and sort_by from the URL.
4. While the request is in progress, a loading state appears in the results area.
5. When the response returns, the results list component displays the branches (filtered to is_active = true) sorted as specified.
6. The user may then adjust filters, sort order, or begin a new search, which updates the URL and re-runs the interface call.

Workflow: User performs a new branch search from the form
1. User enters or edits the postal_code in the search form.
2. User optionally chooses or changes the country and max_distance_km (radius) in the form.
3. User clicks the 'Find Branches' button (form submit).
4. The form submit handler validates that a postal_code is present and prevents default page reload behavior.
5. The handler updates the page URL query string with the current form values for postal_code, country, max_distance_km, and resets or sets sort_by to a default (e.g., distance ascending) if not already defined.
6. The URL change triggers the controller component to call searchBranchesByPostalCode with the updated parameters.
7. While awaiting the response, a loading indicator is shown and prior results are either hidden or visibly marked as stale.
8. On success, the results list displays the returned active branches; on failure, an error message appears and no new results are shown.

Workflow: User adjusts sort order and radius from the results view
1. After a search, the user sees current results along with sort and radius controls.
2. User changes the sort option (e.g., selects distance ascending/descending) via a dropdown.
3. The sort control's change handler updates the sort_by URL parameter while preserving current postal_code, country, and max_distance_km.
4. This URL change triggers a new call to searchBranchesByPostalCode using the updated sort_by.
5. The results list re-renders using the newly ordered data.
6. If the user changes the radius (max_distance_km), the handler updates max_distance_km in the URL and re-calls searchBranchesByPostalCode, potentially returning a different set of branches.
7. The results header and list update to reflect the new search radius and results.

Workflow: User handles empty or error results
1. After submitting a search, the page calls searchBranchesByPostalCode.
2. If the interface returns an empty array or only branches where is_active is false after filtering, the results list component shows a 'no branches found' state.
3. The user can adjust postal_code, country, max_distance_km, or sort_by and resubmit the search.
4. If the interface call fails (e.g., network or service error), the status component shows an error message and provides an option (e.g., button) to retry.
5. Clicking retry repeats the last search using the same URL parameters.

Workflow: User navigates to a branch detail page from results
1. A set of branches is visible in the results list.
2. User clicks anywhere within a specific branch result card (or a dedicated 'View Details' action on that card).
3. The click handler reads the branch_id field from that card's data.
4. The handler navigates the browser to branch_detail.html with a query parameter branchId set to the selected branch_id (e.g., branch_detail.html?branchId={branch_id}).
5. The branch_detail page, which is outside the scope of this page, will then handle loading that branch and providing site visit scheduling.

Workflow: User clears or modifies an existing search
1. After a search has been performed and results are shown, the user decides to clear the current search.
2. User either clicks a 'Clear search' control (if provided) or manually deletes the postal_code input and submits an empty form.
3. The handler removes postal_code and related parameters (country, max_distance_km, sort_by) from the URL, or sets them to empty values according to the clearing logic.
4. The controller component detects that postal_code is now missing and does not call searchBranchesByPostalCode.
5. The results list is cleared or replaced with an idle state prompting the user to run a new search.
6. If the user instead modifies the postal_code or other fields and submits, the usual search workflow runs with the new parameters.
interactions: Input: User types into the postal_code field; onChange updates local component state but does not immediately call searchBranchesByPostalCode until the form is submitted.
Input: User selects or changes the country value in a dropdown; onChange updates local state and can optionally trigger an immediate URL update only when the search is submitted.
Input: User selects a radius (max_distance_km) via a dropdown or numeric input; onChange updates local state, and on form submit or control blur, the URL is updated and the search is re-run.

Action: User clicks 'Find Branches' (form submit); onSubmit validates input, updates URL parameters (postal_code, country, max_distance_km, sort_by), and triggers searchBranchesByPostalCode via the controller.

Action: User interacts with sort control; onChange updates the sort_by URL parameter (e.g., to distance_asc or distance_desc) and triggers a new searchBranchesByPostalCode call using all current query parameters.
Action: User clicks a branch result card; onClick reads that card's branch_id and changes the page location to branch_detail.html?branchId={branch_id}.
Action: User clicks a 'Retry' button after an error; onClick calls searchBranchesByPostalCode again with the same parameters derived from the current URL.

Navigation: Any change to the relevant URL query parameters (postal_code, country, max_distance_km, sort_by) triggers the main controller component's onURLChange handler, which decides whether to call searchBranchesByPostalCode and then updates child components with loading, success, or error states.

State: When a search is in progress, the results list is disabled from new navigation actions (or clearly indicates loading), and user interactions that would alter parameters either queue or immediately cancel/restart the current search depending on implementation.
State: Only branches with is_active set to true in the searchBranchesByPostalCode response are rendered as clickable cards; inactive branches are filtered out before display.
page_functionality.state_logic: Page state is driven entirely by URL query parameters and the response from the searchBranchesByPostalCode interface. On initial load and on every URL change, the controller component reads postal_code, country, max_distance_km, and sort_by from the current URL. If postal_code is present (non-empty), the controller calls searchBranchesByPostalCode with these values. If postal_code is absent, no search is executed and the page remains in an idle state. When the user submits the search form, the handler constructs a new URL query string: it sets postal_code to the entered value, country to the selected country (if any), max_distance_km to the selected radius (if any), and sort_by to the chosen sort order (or a default if not yet chosen). It then updates window.location or the history state, which triggers the controller's onURLChange logic to run a new search with the new parameters. When the user changes the sort order using the sort control, the sort_by parameter in the URL is updated while postal_code, country, and max_distance_km are preserved; this URL change triggers a fresh call to searchBranchesByPostalCode.
