Similarly, adjusting the radius updates max_distance_km in the URL and re-runs the search. Error and loading states are derived solely from the lifecycle of the searchBranchesByPostalCode call for the current URL parameters. Clearing the search removes postal_code (and optionally related parameters) from the URL, which prevents the controller from calling searchBranchesByPostalCode and resets the visible state to an idle prompt. All branch data shown in the UI is taken from the latest searchBranchesByPostalCode response that corresponds to the current URL parameters; no static or cached data outside this interface is used.

components.functionality: Central controller for the Locations & Branch Finder page. It parses URL parameters, manages the current search state (idle, loading, success, error), invokes the searchBranchesByPostalCode interface when appropriate, and passes data and state down to child components (search form, controls, results list, and status indicator).

Provides inputs for postal_code, country, and optional max_distance_km along with a submit action to initiate a branch search. On submit, it validates required fields, emits an event to update the URL parameters via the controller, and does not directly call searchBranchesByPostalCode.

Displays and manages search-related controls that apply after or alongside a search, including the sort_by selector (e.g., distance order) and the radius selector for max_distance_km. It reads the current values from the controller, updates them based on user interaction, and triggers URL parameter changes that cause new searches.

Shows the current status of a branch search: idle, loading, success with count of results, empty results, or error. It receives state and counts from the controller and does not initiate searches itself, but can emit a retry event when the user clicks a retry action.

Renders a list of branch result cards based on the array returned from searchBranchesByPostalCode. It filters out branches where is_active is false before rendering. It handles empty arrays by delegating to the status indicator or showing an inline 'no results' state. It does not call searchBranchesByPostalCode directly.

Represents a single branch in the results list. Displays branch name, full address, postal code, phone_number, email, and distance_km from the search result object. When clicked, it triggers navigation to branch_detail.html with the appropriate branchId URL parameter.

Displays a contextual message when searchBranchesByPostalCode returns no active branches for the current query. Suggests that the user adjust postal_code, country, or max_distance_km and resubmit. It appears conditionally based on controller state and result length.

Displays error information when the searchBranchesByPostalCode call fails for the current URL parameters. It offers a 'Retry' action that triggers the controller's onRetry event with the same parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing stacked header (light grey utility bar, black brand bar, dark grey navigation), the Locations & Branch Finder page uses a single centered content column inside a full-width white band. Components are arranged linearly from top to bottom: first any critical feedback (error), then the location search container, which holds the postal code search form at the top, followed by a compact horizontal controls/status bar, then the results region that either shows a no‑results message or a vertical list of branch cards. All elements are center-aligned within the content column, following the mockup’s linear, stacked-band structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing stacked header (light grey utility bar, black brand bar, dark grey navigation), the Locations & Branch Finder page uses a single centered content column inside a full-width white band. Components are arranged linearly from top to bottom: first any critical feedback (error), then the location search container, which holds the postal code search form at the top, followed by a compact horizontal controls/status bar, then the results region that either shows a no‑results message or a vertical list of branch cards. All elements are center-aligned within the content column, following the mockup’s linear, stacked-band structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header bands occupy the top ~20–25% of the viewport as in the mockup. The white content band beneath takes the remaining ~75–80%. Within this content area, the locations-search-controller occupies the central vertical column, spanning roughly 70–80% of the page width on desktop with generous left/right margins. The postal-code-search-form receives the most vertical emphasis near the top of the content band, roughly the top 20–25% of the content area. The search-controls-bar and status indicator sit in a relatively compact band beneath the form (~10–15% of the content area), while the branch-results-list area is allowed to grow and scroll, occupying the bulk of the remaining vertical space. Vertical spacing between major blocks follows the 32–48px rhythm, with tighter spacing inside individual components.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate at the top of the content area, mirroring the header: the search form fields and primary actions are grouped tightly but with enough breathing room to stay legible. The controls bar and status indicator are compact, echoing the navigation band’s denser feel. The results region is more spacious, similar to the mockup’s hero image area: cards fill the width of the column but are separated vertically by comfortable gaps to avoid clutter. Text and controls use flat, rectangular treatments with 8–16px internal padding, respecting the classic, slightly compact corporate style without crowding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye enters the content area at the top-left of the white band, first encountering any error message, then the search title/label and postal code field. It moves horizontally across the search form (from label to input to primary search button), then drops down to the search-controls-bar where it scans left-to-right across filter/sort options, ending on the right-aligned search-status-indicator. From there, the eye naturally moves down into the branch-results-list, scanning down each branch-result-card. Strong horizontal separations (form band, control/status band, results region) and selective red highlights on primary actions and key states reinforce an F/Z pattern similar to the mockup’s navigation and hero image transition.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s existing light grey utility bar, black brand header, and dark grey navigation, the Locations & Branch Finder content sits in a full-width white band that feels like an opened-up continuation of the main layout. In the center of this white area, a single, moderately wide column (about 70–80% of the page width on desktop) anchors all functional elements. At the very top of this column, any critical error message appears first, spanning the full column width. Immediately beneath, the postal code search form functions as the primary focal block, aligned centrally with a clear left-to-right reading order for labels, input, and the red primary search button. A short distance below, a dark grey–accented controls/status strip echoes the navigation band: filters and sort controls line up on the left while the search status (e.g., number of branches, current radius) is right-aligned in the same horizontal band. Below this bar, the column opens into the main results area, which either shows a centered no-results message or a vertically stacked list of branch cards that stretch the column width but are separated by generous vertical gaps, similar in presence to the mockup’s large central image. The overall effect is a structured, corporate layout: denser bands at the top guiding attention, then a spacious, easy-to-scan results region, all framed by consistent side margins and flat, rectangular elements with black, white, and red brand accents.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The locations-search-controller acts as the main content container within the white content band beneath the navigation. It is centered horizontally on the page, spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop, with equal left and right margins creating generous white space on larger screens. Its top edge sits approximately 32–48px below the bottom of the dark grey navigation bar, visually separated by the background color change rather than a large gap. Inside this controller, the components are stacked vertically in a strict top-to-bottom order: first any error-message-panel at the very top of the controller, then the postal-code-search-form, followed directly by the search-controls-bar (which visually houses the search-status-indicator on its right), and finally the results region where either no-results-message or branch-results-list appears. The container maintains a consistent internal vertical rhythm with ~24–32px gaps between these major sections, echoing the structured spacing of the mockup’s header and hero image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container in normal document flow, centered within the main content band with a max-width constraint (e.g., 960–1200px) to preserve the single-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The postal-code-search-form is positioned near the top of the locations-search-controller and serves as the initial focal element of the page’s main content. It is centered within the controller and spans approximately 60–70% of the controller’s width, leaving visible white space on both sides. Vertically, it sits about 16–24px below any visible error-message-panel, or directly beneath the controller’s top padding if no error is present. The form is structured in a single horizontal row on desktop: a left-aligned form label or optional heading, followed by the postal code input field occupying roughly half of the form’s width, and a prominent red rectangular “Search” button on the right. Internal padding of about 16px vertically and 24px horizontally ensures the form feels like a compact, self-contained block without being cramped. On narrower viewports, the form elements stack into 2–3 compact rows while staying center-aligned within the same column, but the dominant perception remains a central, rectangular search module at the top of the white content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered component within the locations-search-controller; it adapts responsively from a single horizontal row to a stacked layout on smaller screens while maintaining its central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-controls-bar sits directly beneath the postal-code-search-form, separated by approximately 16–24px of vertical space to keep the relationship tight, similar to the proximity of the header and navigation in the mockup. It spans the full width of the locations-search-controller (about 70–80% of the page width) and reads visually as a slim horizontal band or strip echoing the navigation bar’s density. Within this bar, filter toggles (e.g., radius selector, service type) and sort options are aligned left, starting from the left edge of the controller’s content area. On the right side of this same bar, the search-status-indicator is visually anchored, creating a left-right balance that supports the F-pattern scan: controls first, then status. The bar itself has a subtle background differentiation (e.g., very light grey) and internal padding of about 8–12px vertically and 16–24px horizontally, giving it a compact, utility feel while clearly separating it from both the form above and the results below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in normal flow by default, forming a continuous horizontal band across the controller; it can optionally become sticky at the top of the content column during long result scrolls, but its baseline behavior is non-sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-status-indicator shares the same horizontal row as the search-controls-bar content but is visually anchored to the right edge of the locations-search-controller. It appears as a concise text block or pill-like label (e.g., “12 branches within 25 km”), aligned vertically centered within the height of the controls bar. It does not span the full width; instead, it occupies roughly the rightmost 25–30% of the bar. There is a clear visual separation from the left-aligned controls, maintained by internal spacing (at least 16–24px) and potentially a slight typographic emphasis (bold or red accent for key numbers) that draws the eye at the end of the horizontal scan. The indicator remains on this right side even as viewport width changes, maintaining its role as the right terminus of the F/Z pattern in this band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logically grouped with the search-controls-bar but laid out as a right-aligned inline/side-by-side element within the same horizontal band; remains in normal flow and does not float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The branch-results-list occupies the entire content area beneath the search-controls-bar (and search-status-indicator). It begins approximately 24–32px below the bottom edge of the controls bar, providing a clear visual break similar to the gap between the navigation band and the hero image in the mockup. The list spans the full width of the locations-search-controller and is structured as a single vertical column of branch-result-card components. Each card is aligned flush with the left and right edges of the controller’s content area, creating strong, consistent vertical edges. Between each card, a vertical gap of about 24–32px maintains visual separation and a comfortable scanning rhythm, echoing the spacious feel of the mockup’s large central image area. The list grows vertically as results increase, becoming the primary scrollable content in the page while preserving the centered column layout and generous side margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically growing list in normal document flow; it forms the main scrollable region of the page’s content area, with cards stacked top-to-bottom within the centered column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each branch-result-card is a rectangular, full-width card within the branch-results-list, aligned to the left and right edges of the locations-search-controller’s content column. Visually, each card is a flat panel with a white or very light grey background, a subtle 1px border in a neutral grey, and internal padding of approximately 16–24px on all sides, echoing the sharp, rectangular shapes of the mockup. Content within the card follows a left-to-right hierarchy: the branch name and key identifiers appear at the top-left, followed by address and distance information below or to the right, with the primary action (e.g., “View Branch” or “Schedule Site Visit”) aligned towards the right side, potentially as a red rectangular button. The card height is variable based on content, but all cards maintain consistent typographic styling and spacing, with internal vertical gaps of 8–16px between text rows. A vertical margin of about 24–32px separates each card from the next, allowing the eye to scan down the column easily without feeling crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level items within the branch-results-list; they do not float or overlap and maintain consistent full-width alignment inside the centered column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-results-message appears in place of the branch-results-list when a search returns zero branches. It is positioned in the same vertical slot where the results list would begin: approximately 24–32px below the search-controls-bar. The message is center-aligned horizontally within the locations-search-controller and constrained to about 60% of the controller’s width to keep the text easily readable. Vertically, it is padded with 24–32px of space above and below, forming a self-contained block that feels similar in visual weight to a single, simplified result card. A concise headline (e.g., “No branches found near this postal code”) is centered, with optional secondary text below in a slightly smaller size, and a subtle prompt or button to adjust the search (such as “Expand search radius”) aligned centrally beneath the text. The overall impression is a clear yet understated message, preserving the clean corporate aesthetic without adding unnecessary visual noise.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static component that conditionally replaces the branch-results-list area; it occupies a single block in the normal flow, centered within the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-message-panel, when present, is the topmost element inside the locations-search-controller and appears directly below the bottom of the global navigation band. It spans the full width of the controller, aligning with the same left and right edges used by the search form and results, making it impossible to miss without overshadowing the header. The panel is a rectangular band with a high-contrast background (e.g., red or dark red with white text) that aligns with the brand’s red accent, using 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding to keep the content compact but readable. It sits about 16–24px above the postal-code-search-form, preserving a tight connection to the form it typically refers to. The message text is left-aligned within the panel, matching the corporate, straightforward tone, and may include a small icon on the far left. Once the error is resolved or dismissed, the content below shifts upward, reclaiming the space in the normal stacked flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static alert band within the locations-search-controller; it appears and disappears in the normal document flow without overlaying content, pushing the search form and subsequent components down when visible.

name: Branch Detail & Site Visit Booking
title: Branch Detail & Site Visit Booking
description: Displays information for a specific branch and allows users to schedule a site visit by selecting project details, preferred date, and an available time slot.

core_features: Display detailed information for a specific branch based on the branchId URL parameter using getBranchDetail.
Show branch contact information (address, phone, email) and a map based on latitude/longitude from getBranchDetail.
Provide a clear 'Schedule Site Visit' action that reveals or scrolls to an embedded booking form.
Prefill booking form fields (preferred date and project type) from optional URL parameters date and project_type when present.
Allow users to select a preferred site visit date and dynamically load available time slots for that date via getBranchAvailableTimeSlots.
Allow users to filter available time slots by morning-only or all-day using the morning_only parameter in getBranchAvailableTimeSlots.
Collect project information (project type, project address) and contact details (name, email, phone, optional notes) in a booking form.
Submit a site visit appointment to the backend via submitSiteVisitAppointment and receive confirmation data (status, scheduled start/end).
Display a clear confirmation message and key appointment details after successful submission, based on submitSiteVisitAppointment response.
Provide navigation back to the branch finder (locations.html) via a dedicated link.
user_workflows:
View branch details and open booking form:
1. User accesses branch_detail.html with a branchId URL parameter (and optionally date and project_type).
2. On page load, the page uses getBranchDetail(branchId) to fetch the branch data.
3. Branch name, address, contact details, and map are populated from the Branch data.
4. User reviews branch information and clicks the 'Schedule Site Visit' action.
5. The booking form section becomes visible (or the page scrolls to it), using any prefilled values from URL parameters.

Book a site visit with preselected date and project type from Locations:
1. User chooses a branch and preferred date/project type on locations.html and clicks a link to branch_detail.html, which includes branchId, date, and project_type in the URL.
2. On load, getBranchDetail(branchId) populates branch details.
3. The booking form initializes with project_type set from the URL parameter, and the preferred date initialized from the date URL parameter.
4. The page immediately calls getBranchAvailableTimeSlots(branchId, preferred_date, project_type, morning_only=false) to load all available time slots for that date.
5. User reviews the list of available time slots and selects one.
6. User completes project address and contact fields and submits the form.
7. The page calls submitSiteVisitAppointment with the selected values.
8. On success, confirmation details are shown in the confirmation panel.

Book a site visit by picking date and filtering time slots:
1. User opens branch_detail.html with a branchId URL parameter but without date or project_type.
2. getBranchDetail(branchId) loads branch data.
3. User opens the booking form and selects a project type from the project_type options.
4. User selects a preferred date in the date picker.
5. On date or project_type change, the page calls getBranchAvailableTimeSlots(branchId, selected_date, selected_project_type, morning_only flag) and updates the time slot list.
6. User can toggle a 'morning-only' filter; the toggle triggers another call to getBranchAvailableTimeSlots with morning_only=true or false.
7. User picks a time slot from the updated list, fills out project address and contact information, and submits.
8. The form calls submitSiteVisitAppointment, and upon success, the confirmation panel displays the appointment details and status.

Handle missing or unavailable time slots:
1. User selects a date and project type.
2. getBranchAvailableTimeSlots returns either an empty array or only unavailable slots.
3. The time slot list displays a message indicating that no slots are available for the selected criteria and prompts the user to try a different date or toggle filters.
4. User changes the date and/or project type, triggering another getBranchAvailableTimeSlots call.
5. Once a date with available slots is selected, user chooses a slot and completes booking as in other workflows.

Navigate back to branch finder:
1. While viewing branch_detail.html, user clicks the 'Back to Branch Finder' link.
2. The page navigates to locations.html without additional logic on this page.
3. Any state required by locations.html is managed by that page (outside the scope of this design).
interactions: On page load with branchId: Trigger getBranchDetail to load and bind branch data to header, contact block, and map components.
On missing or invalid branchId: Display an error state in the branch detail area and disable or hide the booking form until a valid branchId is provided in the URL.
Click 'Back to Branch Finder': Navigate the browser to locations.html.
Click 'Schedule Site Visit' CTA: Toggle the visibility of the booking form section (and optionally scroll it into view). Optionally update the URL with a state parameter (e.g., showBooking=1).

Change project type in booking form: Update internal form state; if a preferred date is already selected, call getBranchAvailableTimeSlots with updated project_type and refresh the time slot list. Optionally update project_type in URL.
Change preferred date in booking form: Update form state, update date in URL, and call getBranchAvailableTimeSlots with the new date and current project_type, refreshing the time slot list.
Toggle 'morning-only' filter: Update morning_only flag in local state (and optionally URL), call getBranchAvailableTimeSlots with updated morning_only, and refresh the time slot list.
Select a time slot: Mark the selected time slot in the UI and bind the corresponding time_slot_id to the form state; only one slot can be selected at a time.
Fill project address: Bind text input to SiteVisitAppointment.project_address field in form state.
Fill contact details (name, email, phone): Bind inputs to SiteVisitAppointment.contact_name, contact_email, and contact_phone fields in form state.
Enter optional notes: Bind textarea to SiteVisitAppointment.notes field in form state.

Submit booking form: Validate required fields (project_type, project_address, preferred_date, selected timeSlotId, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone). If valid, call submitSiteVisitAppointment with current form state. While waiting for response, disable submit button and show a loading indicator.

Handle successful appointment submission: On success=true, display confirmation panel with appointment_id, status, scheduled_start_datetime, scheduled_end_datetime, and message. Optionally disable the booking form or show it as read-only.
Handle failed appointment submission: If success=false or an error occurs, display an error message in the booking form area, keep the user's inputs, and allow them to retry submission.

Update URL state on key interactions: When project_type or preferred date changes, update corresponding URL parameters using the browser history API so that the page can be reloaded or shared with those selections prepopulated.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the URL. On initialization, the page reads branchId, date, and project_type from the URL query string. If branchId is present, getBranchDetail(branchId) is called and its result populates all branch-related components. If branchId is missing or invalid, branch details and the booking form show an error state and no calls to getBranchAvailableTimeSlots or submitSiteVisitAppointment are made until a valid branchId is available. If date is present in the URL, it initializes the booking form's preferred date field and is passed as the preferred_date parameter in getBranchAvailableTimeSlots calls. If date is absent, the booking form may default to a system-derived date (for example, the current date) without hardcoding a specific value. If project_type is present in the URL, it initializes the project type selector in the booking form and is passed to getBranchAvailableTimeSlots and submitSiteVisitAppointment. If project_type is absent, the project type remains unselected until the user chooses one. When the user changes project_type or preferred date, the page updates the URL query parameters accordingly (e.g., using history.replaceState) so that the current state can be restored on reload or share. The morning_only filter is stored in internal UI state and may optionally be mirrored in a URL parameter to make the time slot filtering state sharable. Time slot availability is always derived from the most recent getBranchAvailableTimeSlots call using the current branchId, preferred date, project_type, and morning_only values, ensuring no hardcoded or static schedule data. Appointment confirmation state (success/failure, scheduled times, appointment_id) is derived solely from the latest submitSiteVisitAppointment response; it is not persisted in localStorage and may optionally be reflected in the URL with a nonrequired parameter (such as appointmentStatus) if needed by other systems, but the page design does not depend on that parameter for core behavior.

components.functionality: Initializes the Branch Detail & Site Visit Booking page by reading URL parameters, fetching branch details, prepopulating the booking form, and loading initial time slots if sufficient information is provided.
Displays the branch name and a basic status indicator (active/inactive) based on Branch.is_active. If the branch is inactive, booking-related actions may be disabled or hidden.
Shows branch contact details including full address, phone number, email, and service coverage description using data from the Branch entity returned by getBranchDetail.
Displays a map centered on the branch's latitude and longitude. If coordinates are missing, shows a fallback text message rather than a map.
Provides a link that navigates the user back to locations.html (the branch finder) when clicked.
Primary action that reveals or focuses the site visit booking form when clicked. May update URL state (e.g., to indicate that the booking form should be visible).

Main form for collecting all information required to schedule a site visit, including project type, project address, preferred date, time slot selection, and contact details. Coordinates calls to getBranchAvailableTimeSlots and submitSiteVisitAppointment based on user input.

Allows the user to select the project type for the site visit. The available options correspond to the SiteVisitAppointment.project_type enumeration, and the selected value is used by getBranchAvailableTimeSlots and submitSiteVisitAppointment.
Single-line or multi-line input where the user enters the project address. The value binds to SiteVisitAppointment.project_address and is required before submission.
Lets the user pick a preferred date for the site visit. Changes to this field update the URL date parameter and trigger getBranchAvailableTimeSlots when branchId and project_type are available.
Toggle switch or checkbox to limit displayed time slots to morning-only or show all available time slots. Updates the morning_only parameter used in getBranchAvailableTimeSlots.

Displays available time slots returned by getBranchAvailableTimeSlots for the selected date and project type. Allows the user to select exactly one time slot, binding the chosen time_slot_id into the booking form state.
Collects contact_name, contact_email, and contact_phone for the person requesting the site visit, binding them to the SiteVisitAppointment fields used in submitSiteVisitAppointment.
Optional free-text field where the user can provide notes or special instructions for the site visit. Binds to SiteVisitAppointment.notes.
Contains the submit button and possible secondary actions (e.g., reset or cancel). Handles submission of the booking form via submitSiteVisitAppointment.

Displays appointment confirmation details based on the response from submitSiteVisitAppointment, including success status, appointment_id, status, scheduled_start_datetime, scheduled_end_datetime, and any human-readable message.
Shows validation errors for missing or invalid required fields before submission, and displays API-level error messages if submitSiteVisitAppointment fails.
Ensures that the booking form and 'Schedule Site Visit' CTA are only enabled when Branch.is_active is true. If the branch is inactive, it shows a non-interactive message instead of the booking form.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing stacked utility/header/navigation bands, the branch detail page uses a single centered content column within a white background band. Content flows linearly from top to bottom: a slim back-navigation link, then a compact branch header with branch title and primary CTA, followed by branch contact info, then a large map hero, and finally a vertically stacked booking form and confirmation panel. All components align to the same central column, reinforcing the corporate, grid-based structure seen in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing stacked utility/header/navigation bands, the branch detail page uses a single centered content column within a white background band. Content flows linearly from top to bottom: a slim back-navigation link, then a compact branch header with branch title and primary CTA, followed by branch contact info, then a large map hero, and finally a vertically stacked booking form and confirmation panel. All components align to the same central column, reinforcing the corporate, grid-based structure seen in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header stack (utility bar, black brand bar, dark grey nav) still consumes the top ~20–25% of the viewport. The branch detail content occupies the remaining ~75–80%, with the map and booking form taking most of that space. Within the white content band, the map behaves like the hero image observed in the mockup, spanning most of the column width and taking a generous vertical height. The booking form below is slightly narrower than the map to create a card-like feel with breathing room. Vertical gaps between major sections (back link → header, header → contact, contact → map, map → form) are in the 24–48px range, with slightly larger spacing around the map and booking card.
layout_strategies.content_density: The top of the content area is relatively compact, mirroring the denser heading patterns from the mockup: the back link, branch header, and contact info sit fairly close together with minimal wasted vertical space. Below that, the density relaxes: the map has generous spacing around it and feels airy, similar to the large image block in the mockup. The booking form concentrates fields vertically but uses clear internal spacing between groups and fields to avoid clutter, creating a structured but not cramped form experience.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows a Z-pattern that echoes the mockup: starting at the top-left of the content area with the back link and branch name, moving horizontally to the right-aligned ‘Schedule Site Visit’ CTA, then dropping down through the contact details to the large map panel, which dominates central attention like the hero image. From the bottom of the map, the eye is pulled straight down to the booking form title/first field, then scans fields top-to-bottom. Within the form, date/time selection creates a secondary left-to-right scan (date on left, time filter on right, slots below), culminating at the primary submit action aligned to the lower right of the form, where the confirmation panel can then appear directly underneath as a clear next step.

layout.overall_description: Within the existing framework of stacked utility, header, and navigation bands, the branch detail page appears as a clean white band occupying the remainder of the viewport, with all content centered within a max-width column. At the very top of this column, a subtle back link sits left-aligned, immediately giving way to a compact branch header where the branch name anchors the left side and a bold red ‘Schedule Site Visit’ button sits on the right, echoing the strong left-right tension of the global header. Directly beneath, structured contact information stretches across the column in a low, dense band. Below that, a wide, landscape-oriented map fills most of the column width, acting as the visual hero and centering the user’s attention. Under the map, a slightly inset booking form card appears, narrower than the map so that generous side margins frame it; fields are stacked cleanly with clear groupings for project details, date and time, and visitor contact details. A row of time slots forms a horizontal focal band inside the form, with the submit actions anchored at the bottom-right in line with the site’s F/Z-pattern. If a booking is completed, a confirmation panel appears directly below the form, echoing its width and alignment. The overall effect is a structured, corporate layout: dense and information-rich near the top, relaxing into spacious, centered blocks as the user moves into the map and form content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component defines the page-level content area immediately below the dark grey navigation bar. It creates a full-width white background band that stretches from the bottom of the nav to the bottom of the viewport and beyond, with a centered content column. The column should span roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop, capped by a max-width (for example ~1120–1200px) and equal left/right gutters. All branch-specific components (back link, headers, map, form, confirmation) are aligned within this column. There is a top padding of about 32–40px from the bottom of the navigation bar to the first element (the back link), and at least 48–64px bottom padding under the latest content block to give the page a complete, grounded feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the static structural container for all in-page components; scrolls with the page and does not fix or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The branch header sits near the top of the white content band, directly below the back-to-branch-finder link and aligned to the same centered column. It occupies the full width of that column (roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, up to the max-width) and is about 80–120px tall depending on content. Inside, the layout is a horizontal split: on the left 60–70% of the width, the branch name and location stack vertically (name on top, location or branch type beneath), left-aligned. On the right 30–40%, the schedule-site-visit-cta is right-aligned and vertically centered; any branch-status-guard indicator appears near the branch name or adjacent to the CTA, depending on availability messaging. Top and bottom padding within this header band is around 16–24px. The overall impression is a compact, dark-text-on-white bar that clearly announces the branch and frames the primary action on the right, echoing the logo/phone split seen in the main site header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element that scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The branch contact info block is positioned directly below the branch-header with a vertical gap of about 16–24px. It spans the full width of the central content column and has a relatively low height, forming a compact information band. Inside, the content is organized into 2–3 vertical columns (for example: phone and email; address; opening hours) arranged in a simple grid. Each column occupies roughly one-third of the width on desktop, with 16–24px horizontal gaps between them. Text within each mini-column is left-aligned, with labels in a slightly bolder or darker style and values underneath in regular weight, separated by 4–8px. The block has about 16–24px internal padding on all sides, creating a neat rectangle that feels denser than the map below but still breathable, mirroring the information density of the header bands in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that sits in the main flow and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The map display appears as the dominant visual element in the main content area, positioned directly beneath the branch-contact-info with a larger vertical gap of around 32px to emphasize its importance. It is centered within the same content column and spans nearly its full width—about 90–100% of the column width—while maintaining generous left and right white space relative to the viewport edges. The map has a landscape orientation with a width-to-height ratio of roughly 2.5:1 to 3:1 (for example, 960px wide by 320–380px tall at the max-width breakpoint). It appears as a clean rectangular frame with a subtle border or shadow consistent with the brand’s slightly classic aesthetic, mimicking the large hero image in the mockup. There is about 24–32px of white space above and below the map before the next major content block (the booking form), helping it stand out as a central visual anchor on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static embedded element that maintains its aspect ratio and scrolls with the page; no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This link sits at the very top of the branch-specific content area, just inside the upper padding created by branch-detail-page-init. It is left-aligned within the centered content column and appears as a small, low-height text link with an accompanying left arrow icon. The link occupies only a narrow horizontal slice—about 20–30% of the column width—and has minimal vertical height, roughly one line of text plus 4–8px padding above and below. There is a modest gap (about 16px) below this link before the branch-header begins, creating a subtle but clear separation between navigation and the branch’s main content. Its styling is subdued (e.g., dark grey text with a red hover state) so it is visible but does not compete with the main header or CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static link that scrolls with the page; stays in the normal document flow beneath the global navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule-site-visit-cta is a primary red button placed within the branch-header on the right-hand side. It is right-aligned to the inner edge of the content column and vertically centered within the height of the header. The button width is determined by its label text plus horizontal padding of approximately 16–24px on each side, and vertical padding of about 8–12px, matching the button sizing pattern in the mockup. On desktop, it occupies roughly the rightmost 25–30% of the branch-header area, clearly separated from the branch title by at least 24–32px of horizontal space. Above and below, it has enough breathing room to feel clickable but remains visually compact, echoing the prominence and density of key CTAs in the black header band of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline-block element within the branch-header; moves with the header as the page scrolls, no stickiness.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking form is positioned below the branch-map-display, separated by a generous vertical gap of around 40–48px so it reads as a distinct section. It appears as a white card or panel centered within the content column but slightly narrower than the map—approximately 60–70% of the column width—creating clearly visible left and right margins that frame it. The card has a subtle border or shadow in line with the classic corporate aesthetic, with internal padding of 24–32px on all sides. Within the form, fields are arranged in a vertical stack following a logical flow: project details at the top, then date and time, then visitor contact details and notes, and finally the actions. Related fields may share horizontal rows (e.g., date picker and time-slot-filter-toggle) but overall the form preserves a single-column feel, consistent with the mockup’s central column pattern. Vertical spacing between individual fields is around 16–24px, while spacing between form sections (project, time, contact) is slightly larger at 24–32px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card-like block that scrolls with the page. It does not fix to the viewport but extends in height as needed based on content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-type-selector appears as the first form field within the site-visit-booking-form, near the top-left of the form card, immediately below any form title or the error banner if present. It spans the full internal width of the form card (minus standard side padding), giving it about 90–100% of the form’s usable width. The label is top-aligned above the dropdown with 4–8px of space, and the dropdown itself has standard input height (about 40–48px) with 8–12px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding. There is about 16–20px of vertical space below this field before the next field (project-address-input), establishing a clear top-down reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width form field within the booking form; scrolls within the natural page flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The project-address-input sits directly beneath the project-type-selector within the booking form, aligned to the same left and right internal edges of the form card and spanning its full usable width. Its label is positioned above the text input with a small 4–8px gap; the input itself is a single-line text field with a width matching the selector above and a height of roughly 40–48px. There is a similar 16–20px vertical gap below this field before the preferred-date-picker row begins, keeping the project details section compact but clearly segmented.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width text field; remains in standard vertical flow within the form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The preferred-date-picker is placed beneath the project-address-input and typically aligned in a horizontal row that shares space with the time-slot-filter-toggle. It is anchored on the left side of the form card, occupying roughly 60–70% of the form’s internal width on desktop. The label appears above the field with 4–8px spacing, and the date input/calendar control uses the same height as other inputs (about 40–48px). There is a modest 16px vertical separation above from the previous field and about 8–16px of horizontal space between the date-picker and the time-slot-filter-toggle to its right, creating a left-to-right reading flow in this row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form field that participates in a horizontal row with the time-slot-filter-toggle; wraps to its own line on narrower viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-slot-filter-toggle is inline with the preferred-date-picker, positioned to the right within the same horizontal row. It occupies roughly 30–40% of the form’s internal width and is right-aligned within that row, creating a visually balanced line: date on the left, filter toggle on the right. The control may appear as a labeled toggle switch or segmented control with a small label above or to the left, using a compact height similar to form inputs. It has 8–16px of horizontal spacing from the date picker to prevent crowding. This placement encourages a left-to-right scan: choose a date, then refine the time slot list via the toggle.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline control that shares a row with the date picker; falls below the date picker on small screens, maintaining standard scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-slot-list is positioned directly below the preferred-date-picker and time-slot-filter-toggle row, with a vertical gap of about 16–24px. It spans the full internal width of the form card, forming a horizontally oriented selection band that becomes one of the main focal zones inside the form. Time slots are arranged as a grid or rows of pill-shaped buttons, 3–5 per row depending on width, each with consistent padding (8–12px vertically, 16–20px horizontally). There is 8–12px of vertical spacing between rows of slots. The entire list has a modest top margin to visually separate it from the inputs above and an equivalent bottom margin (around 24px) before the contact-details-section. Selected slots may be highlighted in red or dark grey to align with the high-contrast brand palette.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid-like selector that scrolls within the form; its height expands or contracts based on the number of available slots.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-details-section appears beneath the time-slot-list, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space to mark a distinct phase in the booking flow. It occupies the full internal width of the form card and typically starts with a small section heading (e.g., ‘Your Details’) left-aligned and potentially bold, with 4–8px spacing below. Within this section, the various contact fields (not individually listed here) are laid out in a simple vertical stack, occasionally using two-column rows for shorter fields (like first/last name) but maintaining the single-column feel. The notes-input is placed at the end of this section. Overall section height is moderate to high, depending on how many contact fields are present, but internal field spacing mirrors the 16–24px vertical gaps used elsewhere.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grouping within the form; scrolls as part of the standard vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notes-input appears as the final field within the contact-details-section, positioned directly after the other contact fields with a 16–24px vertical gap. It is a multi-line textarea spanning the full internal width of the form card. The label is placed above with 4–8px spacing, and the textarea itself has a height equivalent to 3–5 lines of text (approximately 96–140px) with 8–12px vertical and 16px horizontal internal padding. There is around 24px of whitespace below the textarea before the booking-form-actions row begins, allowing the contact section to visually taper into the form’s footer actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static multi-line text field within the form, expanding vertically based on user input but remaining within the form’s card structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-form-actions row sits at the very bottom of the site-visit-booking-form card, directly beneath the notes-input with a vertical gap of roughly 24px. It spans the full internal width of the form, with content aligned along a horizontal axis. The primary submit button (‘Confirm Booking’ or similar) is right-aligned, matching the strong right-side emphasis of key CTAs in the mockup. Any secondary action (e.g., ‘Cancel’ or ‘Reset’) appears to the left of the primary button with 12–16px of horizontal spacing. The row has slightly less height than the main form fields but uses consistent button padding (8–12px vertically, 16–24px horizontally). A thin top border or subtle tonal separation can be used to define this row as the form’s footer, echoing the structured, banded look of the header area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static action row fixed to the bottom of the form card; scrolls with the rest of the form and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The appointment-confirmation-panel appears directly below the site-visit-booking-form card, with a vertical gap of about 32–40px to clearly separate it from the form. When visible, it is centered on the same column and matches the form’s width (about 60–70% of the content column), creating a visually cohesive pair of stacked cards. The panel has a white or very light background with a subtle border or shadow and 24–32px of internal padding, mirroring the form’s card style. Key confirmation details (date, time, branch, contact info) are laid out in a compact vertical stack, left-aligned, with short labels and bold values. The panel height is moderate—enough to present the confirmation clearly but not as tall as the full form—so that it feels like a succinct summary rather than another form. If the form is hidden after booking, this panel slides into its place in the vertical flow; if both are shown, the confirmation remains clearly below the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card further down the page; appears or disappears based on booking state but otherwise scrolls normally with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-form-error-banner is positioned within the top of the site-visit-booking-form card, occupying the full internal width. When visible, it appears directly beneath any optional form title and above the first field (project-type-selector), separated from the card’s top edge by about 8–12px. The banner has a conspicuous red background consistent with the brand’s alert styling, with white or very light text, and 12–16px of padding on all sides. Its height is relatively low—usually one or two lines of text—but stands out strongly against the white form. There is a small 8–12px gap beneath the banner before the first field begins so the error message feels connected yet visually distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline at the top of the form card when there are validation issues; scrolls with the form and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The branch-status-guard is a compact status indicator that integrates with the branch-header area. When the branch is unavailable for bookings, it appears near the right side of the header in close proximity to the schedule-site-visit-cta—either as a small pill-shaped label to the left of the CTA or directly under the branch title on the left, depending on messaging needs. It uses a condensed width (about 80–140px) and minimal height (a single text line with 4–8px vertical padding), and adopts a high-contrast background (e.g., red for closed, green/grey for open) with white text to make the status immediately recognizable. If bookings are disabled, the associated CTA may be visually muted or replaced; the status guard remains in the same horizontal band as the branch title and CTA, preserving the header’s compact yet information-rich presentation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline indicator within the branch-header that participates in the normal layout flow; scrolls with the header and does not float or stick.

name: Equipment Rental Catalog
title: Equipment Rental Catalog
description: Browse and filter construction equipment available for rental by category, technical attributes, price, rating, and availability, with deep-linkable search and navigation to equipment details and cart.

core_features: Category sidebar populated via getEquipmentCategories to allow users to browse equipment by category and select an active category.
Dynamic filter controls (operating weight, capacity, daily rate, rating) populated via getEquipmentFilterOptions(categoryId) and applied to searchEquipmentItems.
Keyword search field (seeded from the search_keyword URL parameter when present) that filters equipment via searchEquipmentItems.search_keyword.
Equipment type filter that constrains searchEquipmentItems.equipment_type based on dynamically available equipment types derived from current search results.
Availability toggle that controls the searchEquipmentItems.only_available parameter so users can restrict results to currently available equipment.
Sorting controls bound to the searchEquipmentItems.sort_by parameter (e.g., price ascending) to order the catalog results.
Equipment result grid using searchEquipmentItems to display equipment cards with key specs and daily rate.
Navigation from an equipment card to equipment_detail.html using the equipmentId URL parameter.
Navigation link or icon to cart.html so users can review their rental cart.
URL-driven state so that category selections, filters, search term, and sort options are encoded in the query string and can be shared, bookmarked, and restored on reload.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Open catalog with pre-selected category and keyword from another page
1. User clicks an 'Equipment Rental' link on index.html that includes URL parameters such as categoryId and search_keyword.
2. equipment_rental.html loads and the catalog-init-controller reads URL parameters (categoryId, search_keyword, equipment_type, min_operating_weight_tons, min_capacity_cu_ft, max_daily_rate, min_customer_rating, sort_by, and any internal-only parameters such as only_available).
3. The page calls getEquipmentCategories with no parameters to retrieve all active equipment categories.
4. The category-sidebar highlights the category matching categoryId from the URL (if any).
5. If a categoryId is present, the page calls getEquipmentFilterOptions(categoryId) to retrieve dynamic ranges and rating options for that category and initializes the filter controls accordingly.
6. The page calls searchEquipmentItems with the combined state from URL parameters (including categoryId, search_keyword, equipment_type, and other filters) to load the initial list of equipment.
7. The equipment-grid displays the resulting equipment cards, and the keyword-search-form shows the seeded search keyword from the URL.

Workflow 2: Refine search using filters and sort options
1. User adjusts one or more filters in the advanced-filter-panel (e.g., minimum operating weight, minimum capacity, maximum daily rate) and optionally adjusts the customer rating filter using options provided by getEquipmentFilterOptions.
2. When any filter control changes, the advanced-filter-panel notifies the catalog-init-controller via an onFilterChange event.
3. The catalog-init-controller updates the URL query string to reflect the new filter values (min_operating_weight_tons, min_capacity_cu_ft, max_daily_rate, min_customer_rating, and optionally equipment_type and only_available) without reloading the page.
4. The catalog-init-controller triggers a new call to searchEquipmentItems with the updated parameters.
5. The equipment-grid updates to show the filtered results.
6. If the user changes the sort selection, the sort-control updates the sort_by parameter in the URL and triggers another searchEquipmentItems call to reorder the results.

Workflow 3: Switch category and explore different equipment
1. User clicks a different category in the category-sidebar.
2. The category-sidebar emits an onCategorySelect event with the selected categoryId.
3. The catalog-init-controller updates the URL query parameter categoryId and clears or adjusts any filters that are category-specific as needed.
4. The catalog-init-controller calls getEquipmentFilterOptions(newCategoryId) to load the relevant dynamic ranges and rating options for the new category.
5. The advanced-filter-panel re-renders its controls using the new ranges and resets their current values according to URL parameters (if present) or neutral defaults.
6. The catalog-init-controller calls searchEquipmentItems with the new categoryId and current filter state.
7. The equipment-grid displays the equipment items for the selected category.

Workflow 4: View equipment details and go to cart
1. User identifies a suitable equipment item in the equipment-grid.
2. User clicks the equipment card or a 'View Details' action on that card.
3. The equipment-card component emits an onEquipmentCardClick event with the equipment_id returned by searchEquipmentItems.
4. The page navigates to equipment_detail.html?equipmentId={equipment_id}.
5. From equipment_rental.html header or catalog controls, the user can click a 'View Cart' link or cart icon.
6. The cart-link component emits an onViewCartClick event, and the page navigates to cart.html, where the cart page can optionally pass back a categoryId when returning to the catalog.
interactions: Category selection: Clicking a category in the category-sidebar updates the active category, writes categoryId to the URL, triggers getEquipmentFilterOptions with the new categoryId, and then calls searchEquipmentItems with the updated category and existing filters.

Keyword search: Typing into the keyword-search-form and submitting it updates the search_keyword URL parameter and triggers searchEquipmentItems with the new search_keyword along with current category and other filters.

Equipment type filter: Selecting an equipment type option in the advanced-filter-panel updates the equipment_type URL parameter and triggers searchEquipmentItems with that equipment_type; options are derived from distinct equipment_type values returned in the current searchEquipmentItems results.

Technical filters: Adjusting minimum operating weight, minimum capacity, or maximum daily rate in the advanced-filter-panel updates the corresponding URL parameters (min_operating_weight_tons, min_capacity_cu_ft, max_daily_rate) and triggers a new searchEquipmentItems call.

Rating filter: Selecting a minimum rating option in the advanced-filter-panel updates the min_customer_rating URL parameter based on the selected rating_options entry from getEquipmentFilterOptions and triggers a new searchEquipmentItems call.

Availability toggle: Toggling the availability control updates an only_available URL parameter (e.g., only_available=1 or only_available=0) and triggers searchEquipmentItems with only_available set accordingly.
Sorting: Selecting a sorting option in the sort-control updates the sort_by URL parameter and triggers searchEquipmentItems with that sort_by value to reorder current results.
Result navigation: Clicking an equipment card in the equipment-grid navigates to equipment_detail.html, passing the card's equipment_id in the equipmentId URL parameter.
Cart navigation: Clicking the cart-link in the header navigates directly to cart.html.

Browser navigation: Using the browser back/forward buttons changes the URL query string; the catalog-init-controller listens for URL changes, re-parses the parameters, re-applies UI state, and re-calls getEquipmentFilterOptions (if category changed) and searchEquipmentItems so the catalog state matches the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page parses the query string for categoryId, search_keyword, equipment_type, min_operating_weight_tons, min_capacity_cu_ft, max_daily_rate, min_customer_rating, sort_by, and internal parameters such as only_available and page if used. These parameters establish the initial UI state and the initial searchEquipmentItems call. If categoryId is present, getEquipmentFilterOptions(categoryId) is called and its response shapes the ranges and rating options in the advanced-filter-panel; if categoryId is not present, the page can either defer loading these category-specific filter metadata or call getEquipmentFilterOptions with a null/empty category, depending on backend behavior. getEquipmentCategories is always called on load to populate the category-sidebar; the sidebar highlights the category matching the categoryId URL parameter if found. Every user interaction that changes search state (category change, keyword change, filter change, availability toggle, sort change, and optionally page change for client-side pagination) updates the URL query string via history.pushState or history.replaceState so that the current catalog configuration is always encoded in the URL. After each such change, the catalog-init-controller recomputes the parameters passed to searchEquipmentItems and calls it; when categoryId changes, it also calls getEquipmentFilterOptions(categoryId) before re-rendering filters. When the browser URL changes due to back/forward navigation, the page re-parses the URL, applies the parameters to UI controls, and re-issues the necessary getEquipmentFilterOptions and searchEquipmentItems calls to restore the catalog view. No localStorage or other client-side storage is used for state persistence; the URL is the single source of truth for catalog state.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and ongoing synchronization between URL parameters, UI state, and backend calls. On page load and on URL changes, it parses the query string, determines the active category and filter values, calls getEquipmentCategories, conditionally calls getEquipmentFilterOptions(categoryId), and calls searchEquipmentItems with the merged state. It also listens to events from child components (category-sidebar, keyword-search-form, advanced-filter-panel, sort-control, availability-toggle, pagination-control) and updates the URL and API calls accordingly.

Displays a list of equipment categories returned by getEquipmentCategories and allows the user to select one active category. It highlights the active category based on the categoryId parsed from the URL and notifies the catalog-init-controller when a new category is selected.

Provides a text input for searching equipment by keyword (e.g., equipment name or type). On initial render, it is prefilled with the search_keyword URL parameter if present. Submitting the form updates the search_keyword parameter in the URL and triggers a new searchEquipmentItems call through the catalog-init-controller.

Renders technical and commercial filter controls based on the metadata from getEquipmentFilterOptions for the currently selected category: range controls for operating_weight_range_tons, capacity_range_cu_ft, and daily_rate_range, plus rating filter options from rating_options. It also renders an equipment type selection derived dynamically from current searchEquipmentItems results (distinct equipment_type values). Adjustments to any control emit an onFilterChange event with the new filter values so the catalog-init-controller can update URL parameters and call searchEquipmentItems.

Allows the user to restrict results to equipment that is currently available. The toggle state is initialized from an only_available URL parameter when present. Changing the toggle updates only_available in the URL and triggers a new searchEquipmentItems call via the catalog-init-controller.

Provides a dropdown or set of options for ordering results (e.g., by price or rating). It reads the current sort_by value from the URL on initialization to set the active option. When the user selects a different sort option, it updates the sort_by URL parameter and triggers searchEquipmentItems with the chosen sort_by value.

Displays a summary of the current search state, such as the number of items returned by the latest searchEquipmentItems call and a concise representation of active filters (derived from URL parameters). It updates whenever the search results or filter URL parameters change.

Displays the list of equipment items returned from the latest searchEquipmentItems call as a grid or list of equipment-card components. It responds to updated search results, client-side pagination changes, and selection events from cards.

Represents a single equipment item within the equipment-grid. It displays key properties from searchEquipmentItems (name, category_name, equipment_type, daily_rate, operating_weight_tons, capacity_cu_ft, customer_rating, rating_count, image_url, and availability status). Clicking the card emits an onEquipmentCardClick event with the equipment_id so the parent can navigate to equipment_detail.html.

Manages client-side pagination over the array of equipment items returned by searchEquipmentItems. It uses URL parameters such as page and per_page (if defined) to control which slice of results is shown in the equipment-grid. Changing the page updates the URL and instructs the equipment-grid to show the appropriate subset of results.
Provides a 'View Cart' link or icon in the catalog header. When activated, it navigates to cart.html without modifying any equipment-related state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: The page continues the stacked full-width band pattern from the mockup: light grey utility bar, black header, dark grey navigation, then a white content band for the catalog. Inside the white band, content is centered within a max-width container (~1100–1200px) aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. Within this content column, the layout becomes a two-column structure: a narrower left sidebar (about 3/12 of the width) for categories and advanced filters, and a wider right column (about 9/12) for search, sorting, summary, results grid, and pagination. The flow is still predominantly top-to-bottom, with key controls clustered in horizontal bars that echo the compact header and nav bands.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page continues the stacked full-width band pattern from the mockup: light grey utility bar, black header, dark grey navigation, then a white content band for the catalog. Inside the white band, content is centered within a max-width container (~1100–1200px) aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. Within this content column, the layout becomes a two-column structure: a narrower left sidebar (about 3/12 of the width) for categories and advanced filters, and a wider right column (about 9/12) for search, sorting, summary, results grid, and pagination. The flow is still predominantly top-to-bottom, with key controls clustered in horizontal bars that echo the compact header and nav bands.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport is reserved for the global utility, header, and navigation bars, as in the mockup. The remaining 75–80% is the main white content area. Within this content area, the first 15–20% of vertical space is used for the central keyword search and the results control bar (summary, availability toggle, sort, cart). The left filter sidebar occupies the full usable height of the content band but uses a narrower width (~25%) and a slightly more compact vertical rhythm. The right results area uses the majority of the width (~75%) and vertical height for the equipment grid, with generous white margins on left and right and 32–48px vertical spacing between the major sections (search, controls, grid, pagination).
layout_strategies.content_density: Controls at the top of the content band (keyword search, summary, sort, availability, cart) follow a moderately dense pattern similar to the mockup’s navigation bar: elements are packed efficiently into a thin band but with sufficient padding (8–12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal) to feel usable and professional. The left sidebar is also relatively dense, with stacked filters and minimal vertical gaps between headings and controls. In contrast, the results area is more spacious: equipment cards are separated by clear gaps (~24–32px), with comfortable padding inside each card and ample white space around the grid and pagination. Overall, the top of the content band feels more compact and information-rich, while the grid area feels more open and scannable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z-pattern. After passing the global header and nav, the eye lands on the prominent, centered keyword search near the top of the white content area. From there, the eye moves horizontally across the results control bar: first to the left summary text, then across to the availability toggle, sort control, and finally to the cart link on the far right, which echoes the header’s right-aligned contact info. From this top-right cluster, the eye drops diagonally down into the equipment grid, scanning cards left-to-right and then downwards. The left sidebar provides a vertical anchor for refinement, drawing the eye back to the left when users want to adjust filters, then returning to the central/right area where changes are reflected in the results.

layout.overall_description: Below the dark navigation bar, the page opens into a white content band that feels centered and structured, with wide side margins and a max-width content container. At the very top of this band, a large, horizontally centered keyword search form anchors the page, spanning a comfortable majority of the width and framed by generous white space above and below. Directly beneath it, the layout splits into a two-column grid: on the left, a narrower, light‑toned sidebar stacks category selection and advanced filters from top to bottom; on the right, a wider main column begins with a slim control bar where a concise result summary sits on the left while availability, sorting, and a prominent cart link align to the right, echoing the mockup’s compact navigation strip. Below this band, the main column opens up into an airy equipment grid with 2–3 cards per row, each card framed by consistent padding and clear gaps so the content breathes. The pagination controls sit centered under the grid, providing a clean visual endpoint. Throughout, strong horizontal alignments and subtle separators create an orderly, corporate feel, while the red and black accents on controls, active states, and the cart link guide the eye along an F/Z path from top-left search and filters through to the top-right actions and down into the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The catalog-init-controller sits conceptually at the top of the white content band, spanning the full width of the centered content container but occupying no visible space. It is treated as an invisible layer that hooks into the entire catalog region (from the keyword search down through pagination), wiring URL parameters, filter state, and grid content together. In the DOM/order, it appears before all other catalog components within the main content area, ensuring that as soon as the user reaches the white band beneath the navigation, all interactions inside this area are coordinated by this controller.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; occupies zero layout height and width, but logically wraps or initializes all catalog-related components within the main white content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category sidebar is anchored to the left side of the white content band, beginning just below the keyword search region. Within the centered max-width content container, it occupies approximately 3 of 12 grid columns (about 25% of the inner width), leaving the remaining 9 columns for the main results area. The sidebar starts roughly 16–24px beneath the bottom edge of the keyword search form, aligning its top with the top edge of the result summary bar on the right. It runs vertically downward, extending at least to the height of the visible grid, and may continue further as filters require. The background is either white with a subtle light-grey right border or a very pale grey panel to distinguish it from the main results column, while internal padding on all sides is around 16–24px. Filter groups (categories, subcategories) are stacked with tight vertical spacing (8–16px between group title and options, 16–24px between groups), creating a compact, information-rich panel that feels denser than the main grid but still orderly. On wider screens, the right edge of the sidebar lines up on a clean vertical grid line, clearly separating it from the results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky on larger screens: the sidebar remains fixed relative to the viewport once the user has scrolled past the keyword search, staying visible along the left side of the results column with a top offset that clears the navigation and search region. On smaller screens, it collapses into a full-width block above the results, losing the sticky behavior and stacking inline with other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The keyword search form is the primary focal element at the top of the white content band. It is horizontally centered within the max-width content container and spans roughly 60–70% of that width, leaving substantial white space to its left and right for a balanced, anchored feel. It is positioned about 32–40px below the bottom edge of the dark navigation bar, and about 24–32px above the start of the two-column content (sidebar and result summary bar). The form itself is a single rectangular search field with a right‑aligned red search button, following the brand’s high-contrast style: a white input with a thin dark border, and a solid red button with white text, sized to around 40–48px in height. Internal padding follows the system (8–12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal), and the entire form reads as a large, horizontal control strip centered in the page, analogous to a hero element but functionally focused on search.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow on desktop; on smaller screens it expands to near full width of the container and remains the first interactive element in the content band, still centered but respecting smaller side padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The advanced filter panel is stacked directly beneath the category sidebar within the same left column, sharing its approximate width of 3/12 columns. It begins around 16–24px below the bottom-most element of the category sidebar, maintaining a clear but compact separation between basic category selection and more granular filters. Visually, it continues the same panel styling: either within the same pale-grey box as the category sidebar or as a clearly segmented subsection with a thin top border. Inside, filter controls (checkboxes, sliders, dropdowns for technical attributes, price, rating) are grouped into labeled sections with small headings, with 8–12px spacing between label and controls, and 16–24px between groups. Overall vertical density is moderate-to-high, aligning with a professional, data-heavy control panel feel, but the left margin and internal padding match the sidebar so the entire left column reads as one coherent filter zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Matches category-sidebar behavior: on larger screens, it participates in the sticky sidebar behavior (the combined sidebar and advanced panel scroll within a sticky container); on smaller screens it appears as a full-width block beneath the category controls and above the results, stacking vertically with standard document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability toggle sits within the horizontal results control region at the top of the right column, sharing a single thin band with the result summary, sort control, and cart link. Within this band, it is placed to the right of the result summary but to the left of the sort control, acting as a mid-right control in the F-pattern line. The control bar itself spans the full width of the right column (9/12 grid columns) and is about 48px tall. Inside it, the availability toggle is a compact, horizontally oriented switch labelled (e.g., ‘In stock only’) and aligned vertically centered in the bar. There is approximately 16–24px horizontal spacing between the summary text and the toggle, and another 16–24px between the toggle and the sort dropdown. The overall impression is of a concise, high-contrast control aligned within a tight band, visually linked to filters but residing with the result controls for quick access.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the result-summary bar on desktop. On narrow screens, it may wrap below the summary text, becoming part of a stacked control group where it appears above the sort control but still right-aligned within its row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is placed in the same horizontal control band as the result summary and availability toggle, on the right side of the right column. Within this band, it sits immediately to the left of the cart link, forming part of a right-aligned cluster. The bar spans the full width of the right column, but the right-aligned cluster (sort + cart) occupies roughly the rightmost 30–40% of that width. The sort control itself is a rectangular dropdown with a visible border and a small downward arrow, visually similar in styling to the search field but smaller in width (around 160–220px). It is vertically centered within the 48px-tall bar, with roughly 16px padding between its right edge and the cart link. Its alignment and shape echo the navigation's compact, link-like controls, reinforcing the orderly, corporate feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the control bar; on smaller screens, it wraps below the availability toggle and possibly below the summary, occupying a full-width row within the control area but maintaining right alignment to keep the F-pattern focus on the upper-right area of the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The result summary bar forms a thin horizontal band at the top of the right-hand content column, starting at the same vertical level as the top of the category sidebar on the left. It spans the entire width of the right column (approximately 9 of 12 grid columns), positioned about 16–24px below the keyword search form. The bar height is around 48px, with a white background and either a subtle bottom border or a very light grey stripe to distinguish it from the grid area below.
