On the left side of this bar, occupying roughly the left half, is a concise text summary (e.g., ‘120 items found’, current filters). On the right half, aligned flush to the right edge, sits a cluster containing the availability toggle, sort control, and cart link arranged in that order from left to right. This band feels slightly more compact and text-heavy than the airy grid below, echoing the mockup’s dark navigation bar in function and density, but rendered in a lighter visual style appropriate for the content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On large screens, the bar can become sticky within the right column: as the user scrolls past it, it pins to the top of the viewport (just below the global navigation) so that the summary, availability toggle, sort control, and cart link remain visible while scrolling the grid. On smaller screens, it scrolls normally, remaining part of the standard document flow without stickiness.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The equipment grid is the dominant content block in the right-hand column, positioned directly beneath the result summary bar with a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It uses the full width of the right column (about 9 of 12 grid columns within the centered container), leaving generous white space between the grid and the left sidebar. Cards are arranged in a multi-column layout: on large desktops, 3 cards per row (each roughly 3 of the 9 columns minus gutters), on medium screens 2 per row, and on narrow screens 1 per row. Horizontal and vertical gaps between cards are in the 24–32px range, matching the spacious feel of the main content area. The grid extends downward, taking up most of the vertical height of the main content band, and is visually framed by ample whitespace above (summary bar) and below (pagination), reinforcing an open, scannable catalog presentation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive, flowing grid: cards wrap to the next line based on available width, but the grid remains left-aligned within the right column and never encroaches into the sidebar. It scrolls normally beneath any sticky result-summary bar, with no fixed positioning of individual rows or cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each equipment card is a rectangular tile within the equipment grid, occupying one grid column slot (out of the 2–3 per row) with consistent width and height dictated by content. The card features a white background with a thin grey border and subtle shadow on hover to align with the high-contrast but somewhat classic aesthetic. At the top of the card, a landscape-oriented image area spans the full card width and occupies roughly the top 40% of the card’s height, mirroring the hero image behavior from the mockup but at a smaller scale. Below the image, the content is arranged in a vertical stack: equipment name in bold text near the top, a short line or two of key attributes, then price and rating aligned in a clear row, and finally a call-to-action area at the bottom with a red ‘View details’ or ‘Add to cart’ button aligned to the bottom right. Card padding (16–24px) and consistent spacing between text elements follow the base 8px system. There is a small bottom margin inside the card to ensure the CTA area is visually separated from the lower card edge. Together, these cards create a rhythm of solid, structured blocks, readable in both horizontal and vertical scanning passes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, clickable tiles within the grid. The entire card (or at minimum the image and title) may be treated as a clickable region leading to the equipment detail page, but the card itself does not change size or position beyond subtle hover feedback (shadow, border color) and responds to screen width solely by reflowing within the grid layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination control sits directly beneath the equipment grid, centered horizontally within the right column. It is separated from the last row of equipment cards by a vertical gap of about 24–32px, and from the bottom of the page (or any following content) by a slightly larger gap of 48–64px to provide a clean visual endpoint for the catalog view. The control itself is a compact horizontal row of rectangular page buttons and next/previous controls, with each button sized at roughly 32–40px in height and following the brand palette: neutral light grey or white backgrounds with dark borders, and a strong red fill for the active page. The entire pagination row is no wider than it needs to be for the number of pages shown, so it reads as a compact, centered control strip floating in the white space beneath the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow. On smaller screens, page buttons may wrap to a second line, but the block remains horizontally centered and positioned directly after the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart link is positioned at the far right end of the result summary bar, sharing the horizontal band with the result summary, availability toggle, and sort control. It is aligned flush with the right edge of the right column, with about 16px spacing between its left edge and the sort control. Visually, it appears as a concise text-and-icon navigation link (e.g., a cart icon followed by ‘Cart’ or ‘View Cart’), using the brand’s accent red for the text or icon to stand out against the otherwise neutral bar. Its size is slightly larger and bolder than a standard body link but smaller than the main navigation items, signaling importance without overpowering the primary content. This positioning at the top-right of the results column echoes the mockup’s emphasis on right-aligned, high-value info in the header, helping users quickly transition from browsing to checkout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the result-summary bar. On smaller screens where controls wrap, the cart link remains right-aligned in its row and is kept near the top of the control area (e.g., first row right side) to preserve its role as the primary destination for users ready to proceed.

name: Equipment Detail
title: Equipment Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a specific rental equipment item, lets users configure rental dates, quantity, and extras, and add the configured rental to the cart.

core_features: Load detailed equipment information and available rental extras using getEquipmentDetail based on the equipmentId URL parameter.
Display equipment specifications, images, pricing per day, rating, and availability status.
Provide a rental configuration form to select rental start and end dates, quantity, and optional extras.
Validate rental inputs (dates, duration, quantity) before allowing the user to add the equipment to the cart.
Calculate and display an estimated rental cost summary using equipment daily rate, selected extras, and rental duration.
Add the configured rental to the cart via addEquipmentToRentalCart and show feedback on success or failure.
Retrieve and display a mini summary of the current rental cart using getRentalCart.
Provide navigation to cart.html (View Cart) after adding an item and a breadcrumb/link back to equipment_rental.html with the appropriate categoryId when available.
user_workflows:
1. View equipment details
   - User arrives on equipment_detail.html with a URL that includes equipmentId.
   - On page load, the page reads equipmentId from the URL query string.
   - The equipment-detail data loader component calls getEquipmentDetail with equipmentId.
   - When the response arrives, equipment name, images, specs, pricing, rating, availability, and available extras are rendered.
   - If equipment data cannot be loaded or equipmentId is invalid, an error state is shown and the rental form is disabled.

2. Configure a rental (dates, quantity, extras)
   - Once equipment data is loaded, the rental configuration form becomes active.
   - If the URL contains optional rentalStart and rentalEnd parameters, these values are parsed and prefilled into the date fields (if valid).
   - The user selects a rental start date and a rental end date.
   - The form validates that the end date is after the start date and that the total rental days meet or exceed any min_rental_days defined for this equipment.
   - The user selects a quantity (with validation that it is at least 1) and optionally toggles extras from the list provided by available_extras.
   - After every change to dates, quantity, or extras, the price summary panel recalculates an estimated rental_days and estimated line_subtotal based on the equipment daily_rate and extras daily_price values.
   - The URL query string may be updated to reflect the selected rentalStart and rentalEnd so the configuration can be bookmarked or shared.

3. Add configured rental to cart
   - When the rental configuration is valid, the Add to Cart button becomes enabled.
   - User clicks Add to Cart.
   - The add-to-cart form component compiles the payload using the current equipmentId, rental_start_date, rental_end_date, quantity, and selected_extra_ids from the form state.
   - The component calls addEquipmentToRentalCart with these parameters.
   - While the request is in progress, the Add to Cart button is temporarily disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.
   - If the API call returns success = true, the component:
     • Shows a success banner with the message from the response.
     • Updates mini cart display using cart_summary from the response or by refreshing via getRentalCart.
     • Enables a View Cart button that navigates to cart.html.
   - If success = false, an error banner is shown with the returned message and the user can adjust inputs and retry.

4. Review cart summary and navigate to cart
   - On initial page load or after a successful add-to-cart operation, the mini cart summary loader calls getRentalCart.
   - The mini cart summary component displays the total item_count and total_estimated_cost from the cart.
   - The user can click View Cart in the mini summary or in the success banner; the browser navigates to cart.html (no additional parameters are required by this page).

5. Navigate back to equipment catalog
   - The breadcrumb/nav component retrieves the equipment’s categoryId from the loaded equipment data (when provided by the backend).
   - The component builds a link to equipment_rental.html with categoryId as a URL parameter.
   - User clicks Back to Equipment Catalog.
   - The browser navigates to equipment_rental.html?categoryId={categoryId}.
   - If categoryId is not available in the equipment data, the link falls back to equipment_rental.html without the categoryId parameter.
interactions: On page load, read equipmentId (and optionally rentalStart, rentalEnd) from the URL and trigger getEquipmentDetail (and optionally prefill form state).
If equipmentId is missing or invalid, show an error state and disable the rental configuration and Add to Cart button.
When getEquipmentDetail succeeds, populate equipment details, spec list, and extras list; enable the rental configuration form if equipment is_available is true.
If equipment is not available (is_available is false), show an availability notice and keep Add to Cart disabled while still allowing users to inspect specs.
When the user changes the rental start date field, update internal state, revalidate date range, recalculate rental_days and estimated cost, and optionally update the rentalStart URL parameter.

When the user changes the rental end date field, update internal state, revalidate date range and min_rental_days, recalculate rental_days and estimated cost, and optionally update the rentalEnd URL parameter.
When the user changes the quantity field, validate that it is a positive integer, update internal state, and recalculate estimated cost.
When the user selects or deselects a rental extra, update selected_extra_ids in state and recalculate extra_daily_total and line_subtotal estimates.

If validation fails (invalid dates, rental_end_date not after rental_start_date, rental_days less than min_rental_days, or invalid quantity), display inline validation messages and keep Add to Cart disabled.
When the user clicks the Add to Cart button with a valid configuration, call addEquipmentToRentalCart with equipmentId, rental_start_date, rental_end_date, quantity, and selected_extra_ids.
After addEquipmentToRentalCart returns, display a success or error message based on success and message fields; on success, update or refresh the mini cart summary.
When the user clicks View Cart from the success banner or mini cart summary, navigate to cart.html.
On initial load and after each successful cart update, call getRentalCart to show up-to-date item_count and total_estimated_cost in the mini cart summary.
When the user clicks Back to Equipment Catalog in the breadcrumb, navigate to equipment_rental.html with categoryId if available.
If the user modifies rental dates, the page can update the URL query parameters for rentalStart and rentalEnd so that reloading or sharing the URL preserves these selections.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver is the equipmentId URL parameter. On every page load, equipment_detail.html parses equipmentId from the query string. If equipmentId is present and valid, getEquipmentDetail is called with this value. The equipment and available_extras returned are stored in page state and bound to detail and configuration components. If equipmentId is missing or invalid, the page enters an error state where equipment details are not shown and rental actions are disabled.

The page may also use optional URL parameters rentalStart and rentalEnd (date strings). When present and parseable, these pre-populate the rental start and end date fields in the rental configuration form. If they are invalid (e.g., end before start, incorrect format), they are ignored for form initialization, and the form starts with empty dates. When the user updates the dates through the form, the page may update rentalStart and rentalEnd in the URL query string using the browser history API so the current configuration is preserved on refresh or when the URL is shared.

The current cart state is not stored in URL parameters; instead, it is obtained via getRentalCart, which returns the active cart_id and cart contents for the current session. After a successful call to addEquipmentToRentalCart, the returned cart_summary is used to update the mini cart summary immediately; an optional subsequent call to getRentalCart can ensure full synchronization. Navigation to cart.html does not require passing cartId via URL because getRentalCart is responsible for resolving the current session cart.

The Back to Equipment Catalog link uses categoryId from the loaded equipment data when available. The link target is equipment_rental.html?categoryId={categoryId}. If categoryId is not provided in the equipment detail data, the link defaults to equipment_rental.html without parameters. All other state (selected extras, quantity, validation errors) is maintained in page-level component state and not persisted in the URL.

components.functionality: Reads equipmentId (and optionally rentalStart and rentalEnd) from the URL query string, calls getEquipmentDetail to fetch equipment details and available extras, and initializes shared page state. Also triggers getRentalCart to populate the mini cart summary on load and after successful add-to-cart operations.
Displays a simple breadcrumb trail including a Back to Equipment Catalog link that navigates to equipment_rental.html with categoryId from the loaded equipment data when available.

Shows key equipment information such as name, equipment_type, primary pricing (daily_rate and any other non-null rates), customer_rating, rating_count, and high-level availability status (is_available).
Displays the equipment image_urls as a selectable gallery or carousel, allowing the user to browse through available images without affecting other page state.

Lists detailed specifications for the equipment using the structured fields (operating_weight_tons, capacity_cu_ft, power_source) and the specs array. Provides a structured view of technical details and can be expanded or collapsed if needed.

Provides inputs for rental_start_date, rental_end_date, quantity, and selection of available extras. Validates that dates form a valid range, enforce any min_rental_days from the equipment data, and ensure quantity is a positive integer. Maintains internal configuration state used both for validation and for building the addEquipmentToRentalCart request payload.

Calculates and displays an estimated rental summary based on the current rental configuration. Uses the difference between rental_start_date and rental_end_date to derive rental_days (when valid), multiplies by the equipment daily_rate and sums daily_price of selected extras to display an estimated line_subtotal and any extra_daily_total. Updates in real time as the user changes dates, quantity, or extras.

Handles the submission of the configured rental to the cart. On a valid configuration it calls addEquipmentToRentalCart with equipmentId, rental_start_date, rental_end_date, quantity, and selected_extra_ids. Manages loading state, processes the response (success or error), and triggers updates to the mini cart summary and status banner components.

Displays feedback messages after an add-to-cart attempt, including success or error messages from addEquipmentToRentalCart. When successful, it may show a summary of the added item configuration and provide a View Cart button that navigates to cart.html.

Shows a compact summary of the current rental cart, including total item_count and total_estimated_cost, using getRentalCart or the cart_summary returned from addEquipmentToRentalCart. Provides a View Cart button that navigates to cart.html for full cart review.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the site's existing stacked header (utility bar → black brand bar → dark grey nav), the Equipment Detail page continues the centered single-column pattern observed in the mockup. All components sit inside a max‑width content container (roughly 1080–1200px) centered on a white background. Content flows top‑to‑bottom in a linear sequence: breadcrumb at the top, then the equipment title header, then any status messaging, followed by a large, centered image gallery as the visual anchor. Beneath the gallery, a two‑column layout appears within the main content column: the left column holds configuration and specs, while the right column holds pricing and cart summary. On smaller screens, this two‑column block collapses back into a single stacked column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the site's existing stacked header (utility bar → black brand bar → dark grey nav), the Equipment Detail page continues the centered single-column pattern observed in the mockup. All components sit inside a max‑width content container (roughly 1080–1200px) centered on a white background. Content flows top‑to‑bottom in a linear sequence: breadcrumb at the top, then the equipment title header, then any status messaging, followed by a large, centered image gallery as the visual anchor. Beneath the gallery, a two‑column layout appears within the main content column: the left column holds configuration and specs, while the right column holds pricing and cart summary. On smaller screens, this two‑column block collapses back into a single stacked column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The stacked site header continues to occupy roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport. The main equipment content fills the remaining 75–80%, with the hero image gallery taking a dominant portion of the initial viewport height. Horizontal space is allocated to a central content column spanning about 70–80% of the viewport width on desktop, with generous white margins on both sides. Within the details section, the left column (configuration + specs) occupies approximately 60–65% of the content column width, while the right column (price summary + mini cart) uses the remaining 35–40%. Vertical spacing between major sections (breadcrumb, header, gallery, details block) follows the 32–48px scale, while internal padding within cards and panels uses 16–24px in line with the mockup’s compact header but more generous body spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page mirrors the mockup’s density pattern: relatively information‑dense at the top, more open and spacious around the main visual, then moderately dense again in the detailed configuration area. The breadcrumb and header compress critical information into a tight vertical space. The gallery is allowed to breathe with substantial white space above and below. The configuration, specs, and price summary present more data but are grouped into clearly delineated cards with adequate padding (16–24px) and 16px vertical gaps between form fields and content rows to avoid clutter. Text and UI controls are laid out in neat, rectangular blocks with no overlapping or floating elements, keeping the layout visually solid and structured.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow begins at the top-left of the white content area with the breadcrumb, then moves immediately to the bold equipment name in the main header. From there, the eye follows a horizontal sweep across any meta information on the right side of that header, echoing the F/Z pattern of the global header bands. The gaze then drops straight down to a full-width status banner (when present), and from there to the large central image gallery, which acts as the main focal point. Below the gallery, the eye first engages with the left-column configuration (starting top-left of the details zone), then sweeps right across to the price summary. From the price summary panel, the gaze naturally continues downward to the mini-cart summary. If users scroll further, the left column specs panel continues the vertical reading path. Red accents are reserved for key interactive elements (e.g., primary action, price highlight) and active states, maintaining a strong but controlled visual hierarchy.

layout.overall_description: Within the familiar stacked header of light grey, black, and dark grey bands, the Equipment Detail page opens into a centered white content area that feels structured yet spacious. At the very top of this area, a slim breadcrumb line anchors the user’s position in the site, immediately followed by a bold, black equipment title band that stretches across the main column, echoing the horizontal strength of the brand header. Any temporary status messaging appears directly below this header as a low-profile, full-width colored bar. The layout then releases into a broad, landscape image gallery that dominates the upper half of the content, framed by generous white space and subtle borders, mirroring the prominent hero image in the mockup. Beneath the gallery, a clearly defined ‘details’ region divides into a left and right column: the left side focuses on booking controls and technical specifications, while the right side consolidates rental pricing and a compact view of the current cart, all contained in simple, rectangular cards. This combination of stacked bands, strong horizontals, and a central content column preserves the mockup’s classic corporate feel while organizing complex rental information into an intuitive, linear reading experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader behaves as a full-surface overlay tied to the white content area beneath the site’s main header and navigation. When active, it stretches from the top edge of the content column (just below the dark grey nav band) down to the beginning of the footer, covering 100% of the width of the central content column and extending horizontally to match its max width (roughly 1080–1200px). It slightly dims the underlying components (breadcrumb, header, gallery, form, specs, price summary, mini cart) with a semi‑transparent white or black veil while displaying a compact spinner or progress indicator at the vertical and horizontal center of the content column. It does not reserve permanent height in the layout; instead, it sits above other elements via layering (higher z‑index) and disappears entirely when loading finishes, allowing the underlying components to retain their natural spacing and relationships.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as an absolute/overlay layer anchored to the content container; it covers and blocks interaction with all underlying components when active but otherwise does not affect document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation is the first visible element within the white content area, aligned with the left edge of the central content column. It sits approximately 32px below the bottom edge of the dark grey site navigation band, maintaining continuity with the mockup’s minimal separation between header bands and content. The breadcrumb text spans a single line or wraps into two lines at most, using about 80–90% of the content column width and leaving a comfortable 16px margin to the next component below. Its visual footprint is slim—roughly 24–32px tall—keeping the top of the page compact and allowing the eye to quickly travel from the breadcrumb to the bold equipment title immediately beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main document flow, no sticky or floating behavior; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly below the breadcrumb, separated by about 16px of vertical space, the main equipment info header occupies the full width of the central content column, forming a strong horizontal band reminiscent of the black header bar in the mockup but rendered on a white background. The block has 16–24px of padding on the top and bottom and 24px on the left and right, creating a rectangular card that is roughly 80–120px tall depending on title length and meta content. Inside, the content is arranged in a simple two-zone horizontal layout: the left 65–70% holds the equipment name in a large, bold, black type, with an optional smaller line beneath for category or model code; the right 30–35% aligns to the right edge and can show key meta like equipment ID, availability status, or location in a compact stacked or right-aligned format. This band acts as the primary text anchor before the image, clearly announcing what is being rented and framing subsequent information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in normal flow, spanning the entire width of the content column with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add‑to‑cart status banner sits directly beneath the equipment main info header, separated by a tight 8–12px vertical gap so it feels visually connected to the action of configuring and adding the equipment. When visible, it extends across the entire width of the central content column, forming a slim band approximately 40–56px high. The banner uses a high-contrast background tint (e.g., soft green for success, pale red or yellow for errors/warnings) with a thin, darker border along the top or left edge, echoing the mockup’s preference for clear horizontal separators. Content within is left-aligned: a small status icon on the far left, followed by concise text that may extend to about 80% of the column width. There is about 12–16px of internal padding on all sides. When not needed, this component collapses entirely, allowing the gallery to move up so the vertical rhythm remains compact at the top portion of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow. Appears and disappears dynamically without leaving extra whitespace, but does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The image gallery functions as the visual hero of the page and is placed immediately below the status banner (or the main header if no banner is present), with about 24–32px of vertical separation to keep it visually distinct. It spans the full width of the central content column up to a max width around 960–1040px and is centered horizontally within the white page background, leaving generous side margins on large screens. The main image area is a large landscape rectangle, roughly a 16:9 or 3:2 ratio, occupying about 40–50% of the initial visible content height on a typical desktop viewport. The image is framed by a subtle light grey 1px border and maybe a minimal drop shadow, reinforcing the mockup’s emphasis on simple, rectangular shapes. Directly below the main image, a single row of small thumbnail images or navigation dots is centered and horizontally aligned, with 8–16px gaps between thumbnails. The entire gallery block, including thumbnails, sits on white with 16–24px of internal padding, differentiating it as a clear, standalone section before the more densely packed configuration area below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section; it scrolls normally with the page and maintains its aspect ratio, shrinking to full-width on smaller screens while stacking thumbnails beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rental configuration form begins the ‘details’ region of the page and is positioned directly below the image gallery with about 32px of vertical spacing. This region is structured as a two-column layout within the central content column. The configuration form occupies the left column, which takes approximately 60–65% of the content width, starting flush with the left edge of the main column. The form itself is contained within a white rectangular panel that uses 24px padding on all sides and 16–24px vertical spacing between individual fields. At the top of this panel, a medium-weight heading (e.g., ‘Configure Rental’ or similar) anchors the section. Below, form controls are arranged in a clean vertical sequence: date range pickers may be presented in a single horizontal row (start date on the left, end date on the right) that spans the full left column, followed by quantity controls, optional extras (checkboxes or toggles) in aligned rows, and contact or notes fields if applicable. The primary ‘Add to cart’ button is placed at the bottom of the panel, either full-width or right-aligned within the left column, styled as a strong rectangular red button that spans roughly 40–60% of the column width. The entire form block typically appears above the specs panel and is tall enough to fill much of the vertical space opposite the price summary on the right, reinforcing its role as one of the primary interactive areas. On tablet and mobile, this form spans 100% of the content column width and appears before the price summary and mini cart, maintaining the page’s linear top-down flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; participates in a two-column layout on desktop and collapses into a full-width block stacked above pricing and specs on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The equipment specs panel sits in the same left column as the configuration form, directly beneath it with a clear 24–32px vertical gap to provide breathing room between interaction controls and informational content. It matches the left column width (about 60–65% of the main content column) and is rendered as a simple white rectangular block with 24px internal padding and possibly a thin grey border at the top to visually separate it from the form. At the top of the panel, a bold or semi-bold heading such as ‘Specifications’ spans the full left column width. Below this, specs are organized in either a two-column label/value grid or a vertical list of rows, each row using 8–12px vertical spacing. The panel can grow vertically to accommodate detailed technical information, extending downward past the bottom of the right column if needed. This preserves the mockup’s single-column reading experience within each column: users read down through the specs after they have configured or evaluated pricing, without visual clutter from overlapping or floating elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and confined to the left column on larger screens; on smaller screens it becomes a full-width section stacked beneath the configuration form and the pricing area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rental price summary panel is positioned in the right column of the ‘details’ region, aligned horizontally with the top of the rental configuration form. The right column occupies approximately 35–40% of the central content width and is flush with the right edge of the main content column. The price summary panel uses a light grey background to distinguish it from the white form and specs areas, with 24px padding on all sides and a subtle 1px border, forming a vertical rectangle that visually mirrors the height of the configuration form. At the top, a concise heading such as ‘Price Summary’ is left-aligned, followed by a breakdown of costs: daily or hourly rates, number of days, extras, and fees are listed in compact rows with labels left-aligned and amounts right-aligned, using 8–12px vertical gaps. Near the bottom of the panel, a bold row for ‘Estimated Total’ spans the width, possibly highlighted with a stronger weight or a thin red underline to catch the eye, supporting the brand color usage. If needed, a secondary ‘Add to cart’ or ‘Update’ button may be placed at the bottom edge but should remain subordinate to the main CTA in the configuration form. The panel’s overall height is moderate, typically not exceeding the height of the left-side form by much, creating a balanced two-column composition. On smaller screens, this card appears beneath the configuration form, still occupying full width, preserving a straightforward top-to-bottom reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, the panel can optionally use sticky behavior within its column: once the user scrolls past its initial position, it remains fixed within the viewport with a top offset of about 16–24px, so long as there is sufficient vertical space. Otherwise, it behaves as a normal static block in the document flow and always collapses into static full-width behavior on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rental cart mini summary is placed directly below the rental price summary panel in the right column, separated by a 16–24px vertical gap. It uses the same width as the price summary (35–40% of the main content column on desktop) and is styled as a compact, white or very light grey card, roughly half to two-thirds the height of the price summary panel. Inside, 16–24px of padding surrounds a concise heading such as ‘Cart Summary’ followed by a short list of current cart items or totals. Each cart item line uses a small font and tight 8px vertical spacing to keep the component compact and unobtrusive. Totals or key figures may be right-aligned at the bottom, complementing the detailed price summary above. Visually, the mini summary acts as a tertiary confirmation area: it reassures users of what they have already added without overpowering the primary configuration and price evaluation areas. On smaller screens, this panel drops below the price summary, forming part of a vertical stack that follows the configuration form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Generally static within the right column. If the price summary panel uses sticky behavior on large screens, the mini summary may scroll with the page or be grouped inside the same sticky container, depending on space; on small screens it behaves like a normal full-width block stacked after pricing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart action handler is a non-visual component that conceptually sits behind the rental configuration form and the rental price summary panel. It does not occupy explicit space on the page or alter layout dimensions. Instead, it responds to the primary ‘Add to cart’ action triggered within the configuration form, updating the price summary and mini cart panels as needed and, when appropriate, triggering the status banner above. From a spatial perspective, it is tightly associated with the configuration and pricing region but remains invisible, ensuring that all visible elements maintain the clear, structured arrangement defined by the design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual or spatial behavior; it has no rendered footprint and does not affect document flow. It only affects state and content within other visual components.

name: Rental Cart
title: Rental Cart
description: Page for reviewing, editing, and confirming selected rental equipment before proceeding to the rental summary.

core_features: Load the current rental cart using getRentalCart and display all cart items with equipment details, rental period, quantity, selected extras, daily rates, and line subtotals.
Allow users to edit each cart item (rental dates, quantity, and selected extras) and persist changes via updateRentalCartItem, updating the item row and cart totals from the API response.
Allow users to remove individual cart items using removeRentalCartItem, updating the remaining items list and totals from the API response and handling the empty-cart state.

Display a dynamic cost summary panel using the totals returned by getRentalCart, updateRentalCartItem, and removeRentalCartItem, including equipment subtotal, extras subtotal, and total estimated cost.
Provide a primary action button "Proceed to Rental Summary" that navigates to rental_summary.html, enabled only when the cart contains at least one item.
Provide a "Continue browsing equipment" link that navigates to equipment_rental.html so users can add more rental items to the cart.
Show API-driven feedback messages (success, warnings, errors) in response to updateRentalCartItem and removeRentalCartItem calls using the returned "message" field when available.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Review cart items and adjust details
1. User opens cart.html (direct URL or header cart icon).
2. On page initialization, the cart-loader component calls getRentalCart with no parameters.
3. The response provides cart_id, items[], and totals; cart-items-list renders each item and cart-totals-summary renders the totals.
4. User identifies an item to edit and clicks its "Edit" action or opens it by expanding the row.
5. cart-item-editor-row displays editable controls for rental_start_date, rental_end_date, quantity, and extras selection (derived from the current item state and bound RentalExtra options).
6. User updates one or more fields and triggers "Apply" (or equivalent commit action) in the item editor.
7. cart-item-editor-row calls updateRentalCartItem with cart_item_id and the current field values (rental_start_date, rental_end_date, quantity, selected_extra_ids).
8. On success, the updated_item and totals in the response are used to update that row and the cart-totals-summary; cart-feedback-banner shows a success message if provided.
9. On failure (success === false), cart-feedback-banner displays the API message and cart-item-editor-row reverts to the last known valid state from the server.

Workflow: Remove an item from the cart
1. User clicks the "Remove" action for a specific item in cart-items-list.
2. cart-remove-item-confirmation-modal opens, showing contextual information (from the selected RentalCartItem) and asking for confirmation.
3. If user cancels, the modal closes and no API call is made.
4. If user confirms, the component calls removeRentalCartItem with cart_item_id of the selected item.
5. On success, the item is removed from the in-memory list, totals are updated using the totals object in the response, and remaining_items_count is used to determine whether to show the empty-cart state.
6. If remaining_items_count is 0, cart-items-list switches to an empty state view and cart-navigation-actions disables the "Proceed to Rental Summary" button.
7. cart-feedback-banner displays the API message (if any). On failure, the item remains visible and the banner shows the error message.

Workflow: Proceed to rental summary
1. After reviewing items and verifying totals, user scrolls to the cart-totals-summary or is automatically scrolled there if URL parameter scrollTo=summary is present.
2. cart-navigation-actions checks the current cart state: if there is at least one item and there is no ongoing update/remove request, the "Proceed to Rental Summary" button is enabled.
3. User clicks "Proceed to Rental Summary".
4. url-state-sync optionally appends or updates a from=cart URL parameter to the rental_summary.html navigation (for tracking origin) without passing any data fields that belong to the APIs.
5. Browser navigates to rental_summary.html, where the same underlying cart_id (managed by the backend/session) is used by that page's own interfaces to continue the flow.

Workflow: Handle empty cart and continue browsing equipment
1. User navigates to cart.html when the cart has no items (getRentalCart returns an empty items array and totals values from the backend).
2. cart-items-list detects that items[] is empty and displays a functional empty-cart state, while cart-totals-summary reflects totals from the response.
3. cart-navigation-actions disables the "Proceed to Rental Summary" button based on the empty-cart state.
4. User clicks the "Continue browsing equipment" link.
5. Browser navigates to equipment_rental.html; url-state-sync may append from=cart to the URL to indicate that the user came from the cart, but no static data is embedded.
6. After adding equipment on equipment_rental.html, the user returns to cart.html (via header cart icon or a dedicated link), and cart-loader calls getRentalCart again to show the updated state.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial page load
- Trigger: cart.html is loaded.
- Behavior: cart-loader fires onInit, calls getRentalCart with no parameters, and populates shared cart state with the returned cart_id, items, and totals. url-state-sync reads view, editItemId, and scrollTo URL parameters to configure initial UI (view mode, which item is expanded, and whether to scroll to the summary).

Interaction: Toggle cart item editing
- Trigger: User clicks an "Edit" control on a cart item row or a row is auto-expanded because the editItemId URL parameter matches its cart_item_id.
- Behavior: cart-items-list sets that item as the active editor row and updates the URL parameter editItemId=<cart_item_id> via url-state-sync (using history.replaceState to avoid a full reload). cart-item-editor-row for this item becomes editable; any previously edited item is collapsed and its pending, unsubmitted changes are discarded.

Interaction: Change rental dates
- Trigger: User changes rental_start_date or rental_end_date in a cart-item-editor-row.
- Behavior: The component updates its internal form state and can optionally validate date ranges on the client (e.g., end after start) without hard-coded limits. When the user clicks "Apply changes", updateRentalCartItem is called with cart_item_id and the new rental_start_date and rental_end_date (along with current quantity and selected_extra_ids). The response's updated_item.rental_days and updated_item.line_subtotal replace the local values, and totals are refreshed from the response.totals object.

Interaction: Change quantity
- Trigger: User adjusts the quantity field in cart-item-editor-row and confirms the change.
- Behavior: On confirmation, updateRentalCartItem is called with cart_item_id, existing rental_start_date and rental_end_date, the new quantity, and current selected_extra_ids. On success, the updated_item and totals from the response are used to update the row and summary. If success is false, cart-feedback-banner displays the message and the quantity reverts to the last known server value.

Interaction: Modify extras selection
- Trigger: User checks/unchecks available extras in the extras selector for a cart-item-editor-row.
- Behavior: The component computes selected_extra_ids from the current selection. When the user confirms the edit, updateRentalCartItem is called with cart_item_id, current dates, current quantity, and selected_extra_ids. The response's updated_item.selected_extras and updated_item.line_subtotal, as well as totals, are used to update the UI. If no extras are selected, an empty array is sent for selected_extra_ids.

Interaction: Remove item from cart
- Trigger: User clicks the "Remove" action on a cart item and confirms in cart-remove-item-confirmation-modal.
- Behavior: removeRentalCartItem is called with the cart_item_id. If success is true, cart-items-list removes that item from its list, cart-totals-summary updates from response.totals, and remaining_items_count drives whether the empty-cart state is shown. If success is false, no changes are made to the items list; cart-feedback-banner shows the returned message.

Interaction: Proceed to Rental Summary
- Trigger: User clicks the "Proceed to Rental Summary" button in cart-navigation-actions.
- Behavior: The button is enabled only when items.length > 0 and there is no pending API call. On click, url-state-sync updates the current page URL to reflect state (e.g., view or last edited item) if needed, then navigates to rental_summary.html, optionally appending from=cart in the destination URL purely for navigational context.

Interaction: Continue browsing equipment
- Trigger: User clicks the "Continue browsing equipment" link.
- Behavior: Navigation is performed to equipment_rental.html. If configured, url-state-sync appends from=cart, but no cart content is serialized into the URL; the cart state remains managed by the backend and will be retrieved on return via getRentalCart.

Interaction: Change view mode
- Trigger: User toggles between "Detailed" and "Compact" view in a control managed by url-state-sync.
- Behavior: The control sets a local view state and updates the view URL parameter (e.g., view=compact or view=detailed) with history.replaceState. cart-items-list and cart-item-editor-row read this state to decide how much detail (e.g., extras and dates) to show by default; data from APIs is unchanged.

Interaction: Scroll to totals via URL
- Trigger: cart.html is loaded with scrollTo=summary in the URL.
- Behavior: url-state-sync, after getRentalCart completes, scrolls the viewport to the cart-totals-summary component and sets keyboard focus to the "Proceed to Rental Summary" button if it is enabled. This does not affect any values returned from the APIs.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary data state (cart contents, item configurations, and cost totals) is owned by the backend and retrieved via getRentalCart, updateRentalCartItem, and removeRentalCartItem. The page does not infer or reconstruct these values from URL parameters; instead, URL parameters are used only to control the user interface state and navigation context.

URL parameters used on cart.html:
- view: Controls how cart items are displayed. Recognized values include "compact" and "detailed". On initial load, url-state-sync reads view and exposes it to components (e.g., cart-items-list); user toggles update this parameter using the History API (replaceState or pushState) without reloading the page. This parameter does not alter any API inputs or outputs; it only affects visibility of sections such as extras or secondary details.
- editItemId: Holds the cart_item_id of the item that should be expanded in edit mode when the page loads or when the user chooses a different item to edit. url-state-sync reads this value after getRentalCart returns; if a matching item is found in the items array, cart-items-list marks it as the active editor row. When the user selects a different item to edit, editItemId is updated in the URL. This parameter has no impact on server data; it is strictly presentational.
- scrollTo: Used to request scrolling to a particular section, e.g., scrollTo=summary. After getRentalCart completes and components are rendered, url-state-sync interprets this parameter and scrolls to the matching component (such as cart-totals-summary). The parameter may be cleared from the URL afterwards to avoid repeated scrolling on subsequent history navigations.
- from: Indicates the source page for navigation context, such as from=equipment_rental or from=header. This parameter is optional and is used only to adjust the behavior or label of the "Continue browsing equipment" link (for example, deciding whether to return to a filtered view on equipment_rental.html). It does not affect API calls.

No URL parameter is used to pass or override cart_id, cart item data, dates, quantities, prices, or totals. Those values always come from getRentalCart, updateRentalCartItem, and removeRentalCartItem responses. When updateRentalCartItem or removeRentalCartItem succeed, the response.totals object replaces the current totals state. When these calls fail, the response.message is shown to the user and the UI reverts to or maintains the last known valid state retrieved from the server. All interactions that change the cart use the interfaces directly; URL parameters are used only to coordinate which parts of the UI are visible or focused.

components.functionality: On page initialization, fetches the current rental cart using getRentalCart, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting cart_id, items array, and totals object to other components. It can be triggered again (e.g., via a retry action) to refresh the cart state.
event_handlers: onInit (calls getRentalCart and populates shared cart state and totals)
onReload (re-calls getRentalCart, replacing existing cart data with the latest response)
onErrorRetry (triggered from cart-feedback-banner to reattempt getRentalCart when initial load fails)

components.functionality: Displays all items from the current rental cart as a list, handling both populated and empty-cart states. Each item row shows equipment name, type, dates, quantity, selected extras, daily and extra rates, and line_subtotal. It coordinates which item is in edit mode, based on internal state and the editItemId URL parameter, and delegates editing to cart-item-editor-row. It exposes actions to remove items and to open the editor for a specific item.
event_handlers: onItemsLoaded (receives items and totals from cart-loader and renders them)
onItemEditClick (sets the clicked item as the active editor, updates the editItemId URL parameter via url-state-sync)
onItemEditClose (collapses the editor for an item, optionally clearing editItemId if no other item is being edited)
onItemRemoveClick (opens cart-remove-item-confirmation-modal for the selected cart_item_id)
onEmptyStateAction (invoked when the cart is empty, e.g., to focus the "Continue browsing equipment" link)

components.functionality: Provides an editable interface for a single RentalCartItem. It shows equipment details (read-only) and editable fields for rental_start_date, rental_end_date, quantity, and extras selection. When the user applies changes, it calls updateRentalCartItem and uses the response to update the cart item and global totals. It handles client-side validation and reverts to the last known successful state if the API indicates failure.
event_handlers: onStartDateChange (updates local rental_start_date value and validation status)
onEndDateChange (updates local rental_end_date value and validation status)
onQuantityChange (updates local quantity value and performs basic non-negative validation)
onExtrasSelectionChange (updates local selected_extra_ids based on user selection of extras options)

onApplyChanges (calls updateRentalCartItem with cart_item_id, rental_start_date, rental_end_date, quantity, and selected_extra_ids; on success, replaces local item state with updated_item and notifies cart-totals-summary using response.totals; on failure, invokes cart-feedback-banner and reverts local changes)
onCancelChanges (discards unsaved edits and restores the item to its latest server state, closing the editor and optionally clearing editItemId via url-state-sync)

components.functionality: Prompts the user to confirm removal of a selected cart item. If confirmed, it calls removeRentalCartItem and updates the cart state using the response. It ensures that UI state remains consistent with the backend, showing the updated counts and totals or error messages as appropriate.
event_handlers: onOpen (receives the target cart_item_id and corresponding item details from cart-items-list for display)

onConfirmRemove (calls removeRentalCartItem with cart_item_id; on success, notifies cart-items-list to remove the item from the list and cart-totals-summary to refresh totals based on response.totals; uses remaining_items_count to determine if the empty-cart state should be shown)
onRemoveFailure (if success === false, keeps the item in the list and passes the response.message to cart-feedback-banner)
onCancelRemove (closes the modal without calling the API)

components.functionality: Displays the current cost summary of the rental cart using the totals object from getRentalCart, updateRentalCartItem, and removeRentalCartItem responses. It shows equipment_subtotal, extras_subtotal, and total_estimated_cost, and updates automatically whenever any cart item is changed or removed.
event_handlers: onTotalsLoaded (initially sets totals from getRentalCart response.totals)
onTotalsUpdated (replaces the displayed totals with the latest response.totals from updateRentalCartItem or removeRentalCartItem)
onScrollIntoView (invoked by url-state-sync when scrollTo=summary is present, to enable focusing the proceed button in cart-navigation-actions)

components.functionality: Provides the main page actions: the "Proceed to Rental Summary" button and the "Continue browsing equipment" link. It enables or disables the primary button based on whether the cart has items and no pending updates. It orchestrates navigation to rental_summary.html and equipment_rental.html without embedding cart data into the URLs.
event_handlers: onCartStateChange (enables or disables the "Proceed to Rental Summary" button when items or totals change, e.g., after removeRentalCartItem or updateRentalCartItem)

onProceedClick (validates that there is at least one item and no in-flight API calls, optionally sets or updates local URL parameters, then navigates to rental_summary.html, optionally appending from=cart as a context-only parameter)
onContinueBrowsingClick (navigates to equipment_rental.html, optionally appending from=cart; does not modify any backend state)
onDisableProceed (triggered when the cart becomes empty or there is an API error requiring user attention before proceeding)

components.functionality: Displays success, warning, or error messages related to cart operations. It is driven entirely by API responses (e.g., the message field from updateRentalCartItem and removeRentalCartItem) and by failures from getRentalCart. It provides a way for the user to dismiss messages and, in error cases, to retry relevant actions.
event_handlers: onShowMessage (called by cart-loader, cart-item-editor-row, or cart-remove-item-confirmation-modal with the message text and severity derived from API responses)
onDismiss (hides the current message without altering any cart data)
onRetryLoad (when a load error occurs, calls cart-loader.onReload to re-invoke getRentalCart)

components.functionality: Manages the synchronization between UI state and URL parameters on cart.html. It reads initial parameters (view, editItemId, scrollTo, from) on page load and exposes them to other components, and it updates the URL as the user interacts with the page, without reloading or altering backend data.
event_handlers: onInit (parses the current URL for view, editItemId, scrollTo, and from parameters and passes them to cart-loader, cart-items-list, and cart-totals-summary as needed)
onViewToggle (updates the view parameter when the user changes display mode, using History API methods to avoid a full reload)
onActiveItemChange (updates the editItemId parameter when the user selects a different item to edit or clears it when no item is in edit mode)
onScrollTargetChange (interprets scrollTo parameters on load, triggers scrolling actions, and can remove or adjust the parameter to prevent repeated scrolling)
onNavigateToSummary (appends from=cart to the rental_summary.html URL when navigating via cart-navigation-actions, without passing any sensitive or data-related fields)
onNavigateToEquipment (appends from=cart to equipment_rental.html when the user clicks "Continue browsing equipment", again without embedding cart content in the URL)

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Within the existing site chrome (light grey utility bar, black header, dark grey navigation), the Rental Cart lives entirely in the white content band as a single, centered column. From top to bottom inside this column, the sequence is: cart feedback banner (if present), cart items list (with editable rows), cart totals summary, and finally cart navigation actions at the bottom. The loader overlays this entire content column when active, and the remove-item confirmation modal floats centered over the whole viewport. All elements align to a common left edge within the central column, maintaining the linear, stacked structure of the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Within the existing site chrome (light grey utility bar, black header, dark grey navigation), the Rental Cart lives entirely in the white content band as a single, centered column. From top to bottom inside this column, the sequence is: cart feedback banner (if present), cart items list (with editable rows), cart totals summary, and finally cart navigation actions at the bottom. The loader overlays this entire content column when active, and the remove-item confirmation modal floats centered over the whole viewport. All elements align to a common left edge within the central column, maintaining the linear, stacked structure of the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header/utility/navigation stack still consumes roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport. The Rental Cart content occupies the remaining 75–80% in the white band. Inside the cart area, the cart items list uses the majority of vertical space (around 55–65% of the content area), the totals summary sits directly below it with moderate height (about 15–20%), and the navigation actions sit at the very bottom with a compact height (10–15%), separated by consistent vertical gaps of about 32–40px. Horizontal width of the central content column is around 70–80% of the viewport up to a max width (e.g., 1120–1200px), with generous side margins echoing the mockup’s central image treatment.
layout_strategies.content_density: The top of the site (utility/header/nav) stays moderately dense, while the cart’s main content remains more open and structured. The cart items list rows are relatively compact but clearly separated, with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between rows, reflecting a serious, information-forward layout. The totals summary is slightly denser to convey importance, with grouped values and tight alignment, while still leaving enough whitespace to be scannable. The feedback banner and navigation actions use moderate padding and clear breathing room above and below to break the page into legible sections.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye follows the established F/Z pattern: starting at the site’s logo and header, scanning across to header contact info, then along the dark navigation bar to the active menu item (e.g., 'Rental Cart'). From there, the gaze drops into the white band, first encountering the feedback banner (if present) spanning the central column, then moving down the cart items list from top to bottom. The right-aligned price and quantity fields within each row create a secondary horizontal scan. Below the list, the eye is drawn to the totals summary on the right side due to stronger contrast and numeric emphasis, and finally to the right-aligned navigation buttons at the bottom, often highlighted with the brand red for the primary action.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s stacked header bands, the Rental Cart page opens into a clean white content area with a centered column that echoes the mockup’s large central image region. This column sits within wide left and right margins, reinforcing a focused, single-column layout. At the very top of this white band, an inline feedback banner—if present—runs across the width of the content column, immediately signaling success, errors, or warnings. Beneath it, a vertically stacked list of cart items fills most of the central area, each row a full-width, rectangular strip with flat colors and subtle separators. At the bottom of this list, the layout shifts focus to the monetary totals on the right side inside a compact summary block, visually grounded and slightly darker to stress importance. Below that, the navigation actions form a final horizontal band of buttons aligned primarily to the right, mirroring the strong horizontal emphasis found in the header and navigation. When loading, a semi-transparent overlay with a centered indicator covers the white cart area, and when confirming removal, a centered modal overlay sits above everything, reinforcing a controlled, serious interaction pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart loader appears as an overlay confined to the white content band that contains the Rental Cart, not covering the global header or navigation. It stretches from the top of the cart content column (just below the dark grey navigation bar) down to the bottom of the cart area, spanning the full width of the central column. A semi-transparent white or light grey scrim dims the cart items, totals summary, and navigation actions beneath while they remain fixed in place. At the visual center of this overlay—approximately the middle of the content column—a simple, medium-sized spinner or progress indicator is placed, with a short text label beneath if required. The overlay respects the central column’s horizontal bounds, leaving the site’s outer white margins untouched, aligning with the restrained, centered content pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolutely or fixed-position overlay relative to the cart content container, covering the entire cart area when active and sitting above all other cart components with a semi-transparent scrim.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart items list sits near the top of the white content area, directly below any cart feedback banner. It is centered horizontally and restricted to the main content column, spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width up to the site’s max content width. Vertically, it occupies the bulk of the cart page, filling the central portion of the screen. Each item appears as a horizontally oriented row: a full-width rectangular band with flat background and subtle bottom border. Within each row, the left two-thirds are reserved for item identity—thumbnail or icon, equipment name, and key specs stacked in 1–3 lines—while the right third contains interactive controls and pricing, aligned to the right edge: quantity selector, rental duration, per-day rate, and line total. Vertical padding inside rows is about 16–24px, with approximately 16px spacing between rows, creating a moderately dense but readable stack. If the list scrolls, it scrolls within the page body, not in an inner panel, preserving the feeling of a long, continuous, central list similar to reading vertically down a news article.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document layout as a vertical stack, expanding downward as items are added. It pushes the totals summary and navigation actions further down but remains within the fixed-width central content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart-item-editor-row is a full-width row within the cart items list, aligned to the same left and right bounds as the rest of the content column. The row is a flat, rectangular strip with a light background contrast (e.g., white on a very light grey list background or vice versa) and a subtle bottom divider. The left side of the row—roughly 60–70% of its width—contains the item label stack: equipment name at the top in a slightly larger, bold type, followed by a smaller line or two of details (model, capacity, notes). These texts are left-aligned with 16–24px padding from the row’s left edge. The right side—about 30–40% of the row—is reserved for the editing controls and pricing: quantity stepper or input aligned vertically centered, rental period selector beneath or beside it, and the line item total anchored at the far right. Remove/edit actions appear as small text links or icon buttons, typically aligned near the top-right or beneath the quantity controls, using the brand red for the remove action. Vertical spacing between control groups is tight but clear, matching the moderately dense header feel without feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates as a repeated list row in the natural vertical flow, expanding in height as its controls require, but always filling the width of the central column. No independent scrolling or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart remove item confirmation modal appears centered in the viewport, independent of the page scroll position, floating above a darkened scrim that covers the entire page, including the header and navigation bands. The modal itself is a compact, rectangular panel with flat white background, sharp corners, and a subtle drop shadow to separate it from the dimmed surroundings. It occupies roughly 30–40% of the viewport width on desktop, never wider than the central content column, and sits around the vertical center of the screen. Inside, the content is arranged in a simple vertical stack: the confirmation message at the top, a concise item reference line beneath, and a button row at the bottom. The button row aligns with the site’s visual style: a primary red 'Remove' or 'Confirm' button on the right, a secondary neutral 'Cancel' button to its left, both with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. The overall modal spacing uses 24px padding around content and 16px between text and buttons, reflecting the clear but compact density of the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, with a full-screen semi-opaque backdrop that blocks interaction with underlying content until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart totals summary is positioned directly below the cart items list, still within the central content column and aligned to its left and right edges. It begins after a clear vertical gap of roughly 32–40px to visually separate it from the list. The component appears as a shorter horizontal band, resembling a simple summary panel. Within this band, the left side may remain mostly empty or show a muted label like 'Order Summary', while the right side contains a vertically stacked set of amounts: subtotals, fees, taxes, and a bolded grand total at the bottom. These lines are right-aligned to emphasize numerical scanning, with the grand total using slightly larger type and stronger contrast, potentially with a darker text color or subtle red accent. Internal padding is about 16–24px on all sides, and individual summary lines are spaced around 8–12px apart, creating a compact yet readable block that feels akin to the information-rich but tidy header area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document layout beneath the items list and above the navigation actions. It does not float or stick; as content grows, it moves downward in the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart navigation actions area sits at the bottom of the cart content column, after a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px below the totals summary. It forms a low horizontal strip across the width of the central column but is visually light, relying on alignment rather than strong backgrounds. Inside this strip, the primary call-to-action (e.g., 'Continue to Summary' or 'Proceed to Checkout') is right-aligned, using the brand red as a solid, flat button with 8–12px vertical and 20–24px horizontal padding. A secondary action (like 'Back to Equipment' or 'Continue Shopping') is positioned to the left of the primary button with a 12–16px gap, using a neutral style (outlined or text-only) to maintain hierarchy. On the far left of this area, there may be minimal or no elements, keeping the focus on the right-side actions and echoing the header’s right-weighted contact information. The vertical padding above and below the button row is around 16–24px, and the entire actions block visually acts as the last horizontal band in the cart content, mirroring the navigation bar’s structure but in a lighter tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the normal flow at the bottom of the cart page content. On long pages, it scrolls off-screen rather than sticking, maintaining consistency with the mockup’s non-sticky navigation pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart feedback banner is placed at the very top of the white content band dedicated to the cart, immediately below the dark grey navigation bar and above the cart items list. It spans the full width of the central content column, aligning with the same left and right margins as the cart list. The banner has a flat, high-contrast background color that reflects its status: light green for success, light red for errors, or light yellow/grey for informational messages, with text in a dark, readable color. The height is compact but noticeable, typically 48–64px depending on message length, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. Content inside is arranged in a single line on larger screens: an icon or status label on the left and the message text extending toward the right, truncating or wrapping to a second line if necessary. A close icon is placed at the far right, aligned to the banner’s vertical center. The banner’s strong color band at the top of the content column echoes the mockup’s full-width horizontal strips and naturally catches the eye as the first cart-specific element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element in the normal flow, appearing and disappearing above the cart items list without overlaying other content. When present, it pushes the list downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state sync component operates invisibly in the background with no visual or spatial presence in the layout. It does not occupy any horizontal or vertical space and does not affect the alignment or spacing of other components. Its function—synchronizing cart state with the URL—is entirely behavioral, occurring behind the scenes as the user interacts with the cart items, totals summary, or navigation actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No spatial behavior; it is a non-rendered, logic-only component that does not participate in the visual layout.

name: Rental Summary & Confirmation
title: Rental Summary & Confirmation
description: Displays the current equipment rental summary, collects required contact and billing details, and submits the rental order for processing.

core_features: Load and display the current rental summary using getRentalSummary, including line items, dates, extras, and calculated totals.
Provide a read-only overview of each rental cart item, including equipment details, rental period, quantity, selected extras, and line subtotal.
Display aggregated totals (equipment subtotal, extras subtotal, total estimated cost) returned by getRentalSummary.
Collect required contact details (name, email, phone) and optional billing details (company name, billing address, notes) in a structured form.
Validate the contact and billing form before submission, ensuring all required fields are present and correctly formatted.
Submit the rental order with submitRentalOrder, including all entered contact and billing fields.
Handle submission responses from submitRentalOrder, including success, failure, and status values, and reflect these in the page state.
Display a confirmation section after successful submission, showing the returned order_number, status, and total_estimated_cost from submitRentalOrder.
Provide a contextual "Return to Homepage" link to index.html once an order has been successfully submitted.
Allow the page to be reloaded or revisited (including with URL parameters) while preserving core state such as whether an order has been submitted and which order is being referenced.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Review summary and submit order: 1) User lands on rental_summary.html (typically from cart.html). 2) Page-state controller reads URL parameters (such as mode, order_id, order_number, status) to determine whether the page is in pre-submission or post-submission state. 3) rental-summary-loader calls getRentalSummary and populates the rental summary components with items and totals. 4) User reviews the list of rental items and totals in the summary components. 5) User fills in required contact fields and any optional billing fields in the contact-billing-form component. 6) User clicks the primary submit button on the contact-billing-form. 7) contact-billing-form validates the inputs and, if valid, calls submitRentalOrder with contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, company_name, billing_address, and notes. 8) While submitRentalOrder is in progress, the form shows a busy state and disables repeated submission. 9) When submitRentalOrder returns with success=true, the page updates in-memory state (e.g., currentOrder) with order_id, order_number, status, and total_estimated_cost, updates URL parameters to reflect a submitted mode, hides or locks the form, and shows the order-submission-status and confirmation-next-steps components including the link to index.html.

Workflow 2 – Handle validation errors and failed submissions: 1) User completes the contact-billing-form with missing or invalid required fields. 2) On submit, the form performs client-side validation; invalid fields are flagged and submitRentalOrder is not called until errors are resolved. 3) If submitRentalOrder is called and returns success=false or an error is encountered, the error-banner component is populated with the message returned from submitRentalOrder (if available) or a generic error, and the form is re-enabled. 4) User can correct any issues (such as adjusting email or phone formats) and resubmit, repeating the submission cycle. 5) The page does not change URL mode to submitted if success=false, keeping the user in an editable state.

Workflow 3 – Revisit or reload after submission: 1) After a successful submission, the page updates the browser URL for rental_summary.html to include query parameters representing the order context (for example, order_id, order_number, and a status parameter derived from the submitRentalOrder response). 2) When the user reloads the page or revisits the URL, page-state-controller reads these query parameters on load. 3) If the URL indicates a submitted state, the page requests the current cart summary via getRentalSummary and displays items and totals as a read-only reference, while order-submission-status uses the URL parameters (and any in-memory state if available within the same session) to show that the order has already been submitted and to display the associated order_number and status. 4) The contact-billing-form in this mode is either hidden or displayed in read-only / disabled mode to prevent duplicate submissions. 5) The confirmation-next-steps component remains visible, including the "Return to Homepage" navigation link.
interactions: On initial page load, the page-state-controller component reads URL parameters (such as mode, order_id, order_number, status) and sets an internal pageMode state (for example, "review" or "submitted"), which controls which components are active or read-only.

Once pageMode is determined, the rental-summary-loader component automatically calls getRentalSummary; during this call, it sets a loading state that hides or disables dependent components until data is available.

After getRentalSummary resolves, rental-items-summary-table binds to the returned items array, rendering one row per item. Each row exposes interactions such as expanding to show selected_extras_summary details when available.
rental-totals-summary binds to the totals object from getRentalSummary and updates its display whenever a new summary payload is received (for example, after a retry).

User interaction with the contact-billing-form includes changing input fields; each change updates internal form state and, optionally, per-field validation flags for required fields such as contact_name, contact_email, and contact_phone.

When the user clicks the form's primary submit button, contact-billing-form performs client-side validation; if any required field is missing or fails local format checks, the form prevents the submitRentalOrder call and highlights the respective fields.

If validation passes, contact-billing-form invokes submitRentalOrder with the current values of contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, company_name, billing_address, and notes. During this call, the component sets a submitting state, disables the submit button, and may show a loading indicator.

When submitRentalOrder returns, contact-billing-form inspects the success boolean; if success=true, it emits an event to page-state-controller containing order_id, order_number, status, total_estimated_cost, and message, then switches its view to a read-only or hidden state.
If submitRentalOrder returns success=false, contact-billing-form passes the returned message to error-banner, clears the submitting state, and re-enables the submit button so the user can retry.

On successful submission, page-state-controller updates the browser URL using history.pushState or a similar mechanism, adding query parameters that encode the order context (such as order_id, order_number, status=the returned status), so that the confirmation state is preserved across reloads.

The order-submission-status component listens for changes in either the submission result or the URL parameters; when it detects an order context, it displays the current status, order_number, and total_estimated_cost, and hides any "not yet submitted" messages.

The confirmation-next-steps component becomes visible only when an order context exists (either from recent submission or from URL parameters indicating a submitted order) and provides a "Return to Homepage" link that navigates to index.html without additional query parameters.

The error-banner component listens for error events from getRentalSummary (for example, network failures) and submitRentalOrder; when an error occurs, it displays an appropriate message and may present a "Retry" action that triggers a new getRentalSummary call.

If the user clicks a "Retry loading summary" control in the rental-summary-loader or error-banner, the page triggers getRentalSummary again and rebinds results to rental-items-summary-table and rental-totals-summary.
