page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state of rental_summary.html is managed via URL parameters rather than localStorage. On each load, the page parses query parameters such as: (1) mode – indicates the view mode, for example, a value representing "review" (pre-submission) versus "submitted" (post-submission). If mode is absent, the page defaults to the review state. (2) order_id – identifies a submitted rental order; this value is obtained from the submitRentalOrder response and written into the URL after a successful submission. (3) order_number – a human-readable identifier returned from submitRentalOrder; this is optionally mirrored into the URL so that it can be displayed on reload without re-calling submitRentalOrder. (4) status – reflects the order status string returned by submitRentalOrder (for example, submitted or confirmed); when present, it signals that the order has already been submitted and should be shown in a confirmation state. The page-state-controller reads these parameters on every load. If status indicates a submitted order, the page: (a) still calls getRentalSummary to show the current cart summary, (b) configures order-submission-status and confirmation-next-steps to display confirmation details, and (c) disables or hides the contact-billing-form to prevent duplicate submissions. If no order-related parameters are found, the page stays in review mode: it calls getRentalSummary, shows the editable contact-billing-form, and hides confirmation components. After submitRentalOrder returns success=true, the controller updates the URL to include mode, order_id, order_number, and status values using the browser history API, ensuring that refreshing the page or sharing the URL preserves the submitted state without relying on localStorage.

components.functionality: Central coordinator for page load, URL parameter parsing, interface calls, and mode switching between pre-submission (review) and post-submission (confirmation) states.
Fetches the consolidated rental summary from getRentalSummary whenever the page initializes or the user retries loading the summary, and exposes loading, success, and error states.

Displays the list of rental cart items returned by getRentalSummary, one row per item, including equipment_name, equipment_type, rental_start_date, rental_end_date, rental_days, quantity, selected_extras_summary, and line_subtotal, with optional row-level expansion to show additional text details for extras.
Shows the aggregated totals from getRentalSummary.totals, including equipment_subtotal, extras_subtotal, and total_estimated_cost, and updates whenever a new summary payload is loaded.

Collects contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone (required) and company_name, billing_address, notes (optional) and submits them via submitRentalOrder; handles client-side validation and transitions to read-only or hidden mode upon successful order submission.

Displays the current order submission status using data from the latest submitRentalOrder response or URL parameters, including order_number, status, total_estimated_cost, and any message; visible primarily in the submitted state.

Provides next-step guidance after successful order submission, including a "Return to Homepage" navigation control that links to index.html and may include non-interactive text about what happens next.

Displays error messages related to loading the rental summary (getRentalSummary) or submitting the order (submitRentalOrder), with optional controls to dismiss the message or retry the relevant operation.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Within the existing site chrome (light grey utility bar, black header, dark grey navigation), this page uses a single, centered content column inside a full-width white content band. All eight components stack vertically in a logical, linear order under the navigation: state/loader/error at the top of the white band, followed by rental summary (table then totals), then the contact & billing form as the main body, and finally submission status and confirmation/next steps at the bottom. Each block is visually separated with consistent vertical spacing, matching the stacked-band, top-to-bottom reading pattern from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Within the existing site chrome (light grey utility bar, black header, dark grey navigation), this page uses a single, centered content column inside a full-width white content band. All eight components stack vertically in a logical, linear order under the navigation: state/loader/error at the top of the white band, followed by rental summary (table then totals), then the contact & billing form as the main body, and finally submission status and confirmation/next steps at the bottom. Each block is visually separated with consistent vertical spacing, matching the stacked-band, top-to-bottom reading pattern from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header stack above this page content occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport. The white content band fills the remaining 75–80%. Inside this band, the centered content column spans about 70–80% of the viewport width (max-width ~1040–1140px) with generous left/right margins. The rental items summary table and contact/billing form consume most of the vertical space. Loader and state-related components occupy compact horizontal strips at the top or bottom of the column. The totals summary is given a medium-sized block, slightly more compact than the table and form.
layout_strategies.content_density: The top control layer (loader, error banner) is dense and compact, with minimal vertical height and tight padding, echoing the compact utility/header bands. The rental summary table has a moderate density with clearly separated rows and columns but minimal excess white space. The totals summary is slightly denser, with values grouped tightly to support quick scanning. The contact & billing form is more open, with clear vertical spacing (24–32px) between groups of fields to maintain clarity. Status and next steps panels at the bottom are compact but readable, similar in density to the navigation area rather than the airy content blocks.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye enters the white content band at the top center, immediately encountering the loader or error banner if present. From there, the eye moves downward to the bold rental summary table header, scans left-to-right across equipment details, then diagonally down-right to the totals summary panel. From the totals, the flow continues straight down into the contact & billing form, which is the primary interaction zone. After submitting, attention shifts to the order submission status panel directly below the form and finally to the confirmation/next steps panel at the bottom. The flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z-pattern within a single column: small control elements at the very top, then primary data, then the main action area, then post-action information.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: Below the site’s stacked utility, header, and navigation bands, the page opens into a full-width white content band with a centered column that feels spacious compared to the compact header. At the very top of this white area, a narrow horizontal strip is reserved for dynamic system feedback: a slim loader bar and, if needed, a strong red error banner that span the full width of the content column, immediately catching attention without dominating the screen. Directly beneath this control strip, the rental items summary table stretches across the column, presenting equipment details in a structured, high-contrast tabular layout reminiscent of the site’s serious, corporate style. Just below the table and aligned to the right half of the column, a totals summary block groups the key monetary figures in a compact, card-like area, using bold black type and red accents to emphasize totals. The layout then opens further into the core interaction region: a tall contact & billing form that runs nearly the full width of the column, with fields arranged in a clear, vertically flowing structure and occasional two-column rows for efficiency. Beneath the form, a horizontally centered submission status panel appears once the user interacts, providing immediate feedback without shifting the rest of the layout. At the very bottom of the column, a confirmation and next steps panel closes the page, visually echoing the navigation band’s compact density while maintaining generous side margins, guiding the user toward what to do after submitting. The result is a vertically stacked, clearly segmented page that feels consistent with the mockup: compact and information-dense near the top, expanding into a more open, user-focused area in the center, then resolving into concise, directive content at the bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state-controller is not visually rendered but logically sits at the top of the content column, just inside the white content band below the dark grey navigation bar. It occupies no visible space but governs the visibility and states of the loader, error banner, submission status, and confirmation panels. In terms of layout, it is conceptually aligned with the top control strip, orchestrating which of the stateful components appear in the upper portion of the column without altering the overall stacked, single-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that does not occupy fixed dimensions; it influences which components are mounted in the normal document flow but does not itself change spatial behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rental-summary-loader appears as a thin, full-width bar at the very top of the white content band, directly under the dark grey navigation bar and aligned to the centered content column. It spans 100% of the column width (about 70–80% of the viewport) with 8–12px vertical height, using a strong red or dark accent to indicate loading progress. There is minimal vertical margin above (it nearly kisses the bottom of the nav) and 8–16px of space below before the next element (either the error banner or the rental summary table). When active, it visually behaves like a compact utility strip, similar in density to the top utility bar in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in the normal flow at the top of the content column; when present, it pushes subsequent components downward but does not overlay or remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-banner is positioned directly below the loader bar (if present) or immediately under the navigation bar within the white content band. It spans the full width of the centered content column, with a height of roughly 48–64px depending on message length. The background is solid red with white or very light text, matching the brand’s accent color and creating a strong, high-contrast strip similar in visual strength to the black header band. The banner has 16px padding on all sides, with a small error icon on the left and the message text to its right, both aligned vertically to the center. There is a 16–24px gap below the banner before the rental items summary table begins, clearly separating the alert from the main content while keeping it within the top control portion of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displays in the normal document flow, pushing the rental content downward. It remains non-sticky, scrolling away with the rest of the content once the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rental-items-summary-table is the first major content block in the white band, positioned below the loader and error banner region with about 24–32px of top margin to introduce a visual break. It is centered within the content column, spanning nearly 100% of that column’s width (approximately 70–80% of the viewport), with 16–24px padding inside the table container. The table header row uses a dark grey or black background with white text, echoing the black brand bar above, while the body rows alternate light greys on a white base for clear readability. Column headings align left for text fields and right for numeric values, with consistent 16px inner cell padding. A bold, left-aligned title such as “Rental Summary” sits directly above the table, separated by a small 8–12px gap. There is a 24–32px gap below the table before the totals summary, making this block feel like the main data anchor at the top of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element within the vertical stack, expanding vertically as rows increase while maintaining fixed side alignment within the column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rental-totals-summary sits directly beneath the rental items summary table, separated by approximately 24px of vertical space to maintain a clear but related grouping. It is right-aligned within the same centered content column, occupying roughly the right 40–50% of that column while leaving open white space to its left, which lightens the composition and creates a natural visual pull toward the key totals. The block has a subtle light grey border or background, with 16–24px internal padding. Line items (subtotal, taxes, fees) are stacked vertically in compact rows, left-aligned labels and right-aligned amounts, culminating in a bold ‘Total’ row with slightly larger text and a red accent for the amount. There is a 32px gap below this block before the contact & billing form begins, signaling a transition from data review to data entry.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that aligns to the right side of the content column on larger screens; on narrower viewports it gracefully stretches to full column width while preserving its vertical stacking and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-billing-form is the central interactive region, placed directly below the totals summary with approximately 32px of vertical separation to give it breathing room as a new section. It spans almost the full width of the centered content column (around 90–100% of that column), with 24–32px of internal padding if it is framed as a card. A section heading, such as “Contact & Billing Details,” is left-aligned at the top of the form, matching the table title’s style but slightly closer in vertical distance to the first row of fields (16px). Form fields are arranged in a clean vertical rhythm, with 16–24px vertical spacing between rows. Where appropriate (e.g., first/last name, city/state/zip), fields are laid out in two columns within the same row, each taking about 48% of the form width with an 8–16px gutter, reflecting the grid’s 12-column logic. Labels are top-aligned above inputs, in dark grey text; inputs are full-width within their column, with 8–12px internal padding and subtle grey borders. Key required fields and the primary submit button use the brand red for emphasis. The submit button is positioned toward the bottom right of the form area, aligned with the right edge of the input fields, helping the user’s eye move from left-to-right and down. There is a generous 32–40px margin below the form before any status panels, reinforcing it as the main action block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding block in the main flow; on narrow screens, two-column field rows collapse into a single column while maintaining consistent vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-submission-status panel appears immediately below the contact & billing form, generally close to the submit button to provide direct feedback after interaction. It is horizontally centered within the content column and spans about 60–80% of the column width, making it feel clearly related to the form but visually lighter than the main blocks. The panel has 16–24px of padding and a subtle border or background tint that varies by state (e.g., soft green for success, amber for in-progress). Status iconography (check mark, spinner, warning) is placed on the left, with concise status text to the right, both vertically centered. There is a 24–32px gap below this panel before the confirmation-next-steps section, allowing the status to be seen as an immediate response area while still part of the downward flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in the normal flow only when relevant. It does not overlay content; instead, it reserves its own vertical space beneath the form and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation-next-steps panel is positioned at the bottom of the white content band, below the order submission status with approximately 24–32px of vertical separation. It spans the full width of the centered content column and has a moderate height (roughly 96–160px depending on content) with 24px internal padding. The panel is structured as a simple, horizontally oriented or stacked list of next-step items: a bold section title at the top left (e.g., “What Happens Next”), followed by 2–4 short lines or bullet points in a single column. Text alignment is generally left, with occasional small icons or red accent bullets to create visual anchors. This block visually echoes the compact density of the navigation bar: concise, clearly structured, and slightly more compact than the main content. A larger vertical margin (32–40px) is left below this panel and above the page footer, giving a sense of closure to the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static terminal block in the vertical stack; it anchors the bottom of the content column and scrolls normally with the page.

name: Maintenance Programs
title: Maintenance Programs
description: Page for filtering, listing, and reviewing maintenance programs by facility criteria, and submitting a quote request for a selected program.

core_features: Load dynamic filter options for facility type, emergency response time, and monthly fee using getMaintenanceFilterOptions, and initialize controls from URL parameters when present.
Search and list maintenance programs according to selected filters and sorting using searchMaintenancePrograms, updating results whenever filter or sort URL parameters change.
Allow users to focus on a specific maintenance program in-page by selecting it from the list; when a focused program ID is present in the URL, load its full details via getMaintenanceProgramDetail.

Provide an in-page quote form tied to the currently focused program, capturing contact name, email, facility status, and additional details, and submit the request using submitMaintenanceQuoteRequest.

Support inbound deep-linking via facility_type, emergency_response_time, max_monthly_fee, sort_by, and maintenanceProgramId URL parameters so other pages can link into pre-filtered or pre-focused views.
Provide navigation to contact.html with inquiry_type=maintenance_program via a 'Need custom maintenance support?' call-to-action for users who do not find a suitable predefined program.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Arrive with pre-set filters from services.html or index.html
1. User navigates from another page with some combination of facility_type, emergency_response_time, max_monthly_fee, and sort_by in the URL.
2. On page load, maintenance-programs-controller reads URL parameters.
3. Page calls getMaintenanceFilterOptions to populate filter controls.
4. Filter UI initializes its selected values from the URL parameters.
5. Page calls searchMaintenancePrograms with these parameters.
6. The list displays the returned maintenance programs; if maintenanceProgramId is also in the URL, getMaintenanceProgramDetail is called and the corresponding detail panel is shown.
7. User may adjust filters or sorting, which updates the URL parameters and triggers a new searchMaintenancePrograms call.

Workflow: Browse maintenance programs and focus on one
1. User opens maintenance_programs.html without specifying a maintenanceProgramId (filters may be default or URL-driven).
2. Page obtains filter options via getMaintenanceFilterOptions and initial results via searchMaintenancePrograms.
3. User scans the list and clicks a specific maintenance program card.
4. The click updates the maintenanceProgramId parameter in the URL.
5. Page calls getMaintenanceProgramDetail with the selected maintenanceProgramId.
6. The detail panel displays the full program details while keeping the list visible with the same filters and sorting.
7. User can switch to another program by clicking a different card, which updates the URL and fetches the new detail.

Workflow: Submit a quote request for a focused maintenance program
1. User has a focused maintenance program (either via deep link with maintenanceProgramId or by selecting a card).
2. The quote form is bound to the current maintenanceProgramId shown in the detail panel.
3. User clicks 'Add Plan to Quote' (or a similar trigger) which ensures the quote form is visible and focused.
4. User fills in contact_name, contact_email, facility_status (selecting from the available enum values), and optional additional_details.
5. User submits the form; the quote form component validates required fields on the client.
6. On successful validation, the page calls submitMaintenanceQuoteRequest with maintenanceProgramId and the form fields.
7. If the API returns success=true, the component shows a success state/message and may disable or reset the form; if not, it displays the returned message and keeps the user’s input for correction.
8. The workflow does not alter filters or the currently focused program, so the URL parameters remain stable for reference.

Workflow: Request custom maintenance support via contact page
1. User reviews the list and detail of maintenance programs and decides none match their needs.
2. User clicks the 'Need custom maintenance support?' CTA.
3. Browser navigates to contact.html with the query parameter inquiry_type=maintenance_program.
4. The contact page can then handle this inquiry_type for routing, but this page’s responsibility ends at constructing the correct URL and performing the navigation.
interactions: On page load: maintenance-programs-controller reads URL parameters, calls getMaintenanceFilterOptions to build filters, then calls searchMaintenancePrograms using facility_type, emergency_response_time, max_monthly_fee, and sort_by from the URL. If maintenanceProgramId is present, it also calls getMaintenanceProgramDetail to populate the detail panel.

Filter change (facility type or emergency response time): when a user changes a filter control, the filter component updates the corresponding URL parameter, preserves other existing parameters (including sort_by and maintenanceProgramId), and triggers a new searchMaintenancePrograms call with the updated values.
Maximum monthly fee change: when the user adjusts the max fee control, the component updates the max_monthly_fee parameter in the URL and re-runs searchMaintenancePrograms with the new limit.

Sort change: when a user selects a sort option (including an option that corresponds to prioritizing longer warranty length), the sort_by parameter in the URL is updated and searchMaintenancePrograms is called again with the current filters and the new sort_by.

Program selection from list: clicking on a maintenance program card updates the maintenanceProgramId parameter in the URL, visually marks the card as selected, and invokes getMaintenanceProgramDetail for that ID. The detail panel updates to show the retrieved program data, and the quote form is bound to this ID.

Deep link to a specific program: if the page is loaded with a maintenanceProgramId parameter, the controller immediately calls getMaintenanceProgramDetail for that ID after filters and list are loaded, and the corresponding card (if present in the results) is highlighted as focused.

Quote form input: as the user types into contact_name, contact_email, or additional_details, or changes facility_status, the form’s internal state is updated. This state is not written to the URL and is kept only for the current page session.

Quote form submission: when the user submits the form, the component validates required fields locally. Upon passing validation, it calls submitMaintenanceQuoteRequest with maintenanceProgramId and the current field values. The returned success, quote_request_id, status, and message are used to render either a success state or display error feedback without navigating away.

CTA navigation for custom support: clicking the 'Need custom maintenance support?' element executes a navigation to contact.html with the query string parameter inquiry_type=maintenance_program appended to the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: This page treats URL parameters as the single source of truth for list and selection state. On initial load and whenever the browser URL changes (including back/forward navigation), the page controller re-parses facility_type, emergency_response_time, max_monthly_fee, sort_by, and maintenanceProgramId and updates components accordingly. The filter component initializes its selections from facility_type, emergency_response_time, and max_monthly_fee. The sort selection initializes from sort_by. The list data is always the direct result of calling searchMaintenancePrograms with whatever parameter values are currently present (or undefined when absent). The focused maintenance program is defined solely by maintenanceProgramId; when set, the page calls getMaintenanceProgramDetail and displays that program in the detail panel and keeps the ID in the URL. When the user selects a different program, the component updates maintenanceProgramId in the URL instead of internal-only state, ensuring that deep links and browser history remain consistent. Removing focus (e.g., via a 'clear selection' control if implemented) would remove maintenanceProgramId from the URL, which results in no detail panel being shown. No state relevant to filters, sorting, or selection is stored in localStorage or similar mechanisms; all such state is derived from the URL and API responses. Form input values for the quote request remain local to the component and are not encoded into URL parameters for privacy and simplicity.

components.functionality: Coordinates data fetching and state synchronization between URL parameters, filter controls, list results, selected program detail, and the quote form. On load and on URL changes, it calls getMaintenanceFilterOptions, searchMaintenancePrograms, and getMaintenanceProgramDetail (when maintenanceProgramId is present) and passes the results to child components.

Displays and manages the facility type selector, emergency response time selector, and maximum monthly fee control. Options and ranges are populated dynamically from getMaintenanceFilterOptions. On user changes, it updates the corresponding URL parameters and notifies the controller to refresh the list via searchMaintenancePrograms.

Provides sorting options for the maintenance program list, including a sort that prioritizes plans with longer warranty coverage. It reads the current sort_by parameter from the URL to determine the active option and updates sort_by in the URL when the user selects a different sort, prompting the controller to call searchMaintenancePrograms again.

Renders the collection of maintenance program cards returned from searchMaintenancePrograms based on the active filters and sort. It highlights the card corresponding to the current maintenanceProgramId (if present in the results) and allows users to select a program to focus in the detail panel.

Represents a single maintenance program in the list. It displays key fields from the searchMaintenancePrograms result (such as name, facility_type, monthly_fee, emergency_response_time, warranty_length_months, and inclusion flags) and communicates selection events up to the list and controller when clicked.

Displays full information for the currently focused maintenance program using data from getMaintenanceProgramDetail. It stays in sync with the maintenanceProgramId URL parameter and exposes the selected program ID to the embedded quote form. It also provides the 'Add Plan to Quote' action to bring the quote form into focus.

Collects contact_name, contact_email, facility_status, and optional additional_details for the currently focused maintenance program. On submission, it validates required fields and calls submitMaintenanceQuoteRequest with maintenanceProgramId and the collected data, then displays success or error feedback based on the response.
Provides a clear path for users who need custom maintenance solutions by linking to contact.html with inquiry_type=maintenance_program as a URL parameter.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the stacked full-width band structure from the mockup: global light-grey utility bar, black brand header, dark-grey nav bar, then a full-width white content band. All provided components live inside the white content band within a centered max-width content column (≈70–80% of viewport width, respecting a 12-column grid). Inside this column, components follow a top-to-bottom, single-column flow: page controller (title/intro) at the top, then filter bar, then sort control, then the program list, followed by the detail panel and quote form, and finally the custom support CTA at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the stacked full-width band structure from the mockup: global light-grey utility bar, black brand header, dark-grey nav bar, then a full-width white content band. All provided components live inside the white content band within a centered max-width content column (≈70–80% of viewport width, respecting a 12-column grid). Inside this column, components follow a top-to-bottom, single-column flow: page controller (title/intro) at the top, then filter bar, then sort control, then the program list, followed by the detail panel and quote form, and finally the custom support CTA at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header stack consumes roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport, consistent with the mockup. The remaining 75–80% is the white content area where these components sit. Within that area, the program list, detail panel, and quote form occupy most of the vertical space. The content column is centered, with generous left/right margins. Vertical spacing is compact between closely related controls (title → filters → sort), and more generous (≈32–48px) before and after large blocks (start of list, detail panel, quote form) to create clear content groupings.
layout_strategies.content_density: Mimic the mockup’s moderate density at the top of the content area and more open layout lower down. The page controller and filter/sort controls are relatively dense: tighter vertical spacing and compact controls to keep the functional tools within a shallow vertical slice under the nav. The program list is moderately spaced, with each card clearly separated but not overly airy. The detail panel and quote form are more spacious, with increased padding and larger margins above and below, echoing the mockup’s large central image area that breathes within the white band.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve an F/Z-pattern within the content column: the eye starts at the top-left page title, moves right across any summary info or actions in the controller, then drops to the filter bar (scanning its controls left-to-right), then to the sort control aligned to the right. From there, the eye moves diagonally down to the first program card on the left and then flows vertically down the list. When a card is selected, attention is pulled to the centered detail panel (replacing the mockup’s large hero image in visual weight) and then naturally to the quote form directly beneath. A final sweep lands on the centered or right-leaning custom support CTA at the bottom.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s grey utility bar, black brand header, and dark grey navigation, the page opens into a clean white content band. In the center of this band sits a max-width content column, aligned to the underlying 12-column grid and occupying roughly three-quarters of the viewport width, with comfortable white margins on both sides. At the very top of this column, the page title and brief introduction form a compact header block, echoing the structured density just seen in the global header. Directly beneath, a horizontal filter bar spans almost the full content width, visually forming a functional strip similar in heft to the navigation bar but rendered lighter on white. The sort control is tucked just below and aligned to the right, creating a clear end point of the top Z-sweep. The maintenance program list then cascades down the page, each full-width card separated by consistent vertical rhythm, forming a strong vertical spine of options. When a program is selected, a broad, visually prominent detail panel appears further down in the same central column, occupying much of the horizontal space and echoing the mockup’s large central image with its solid block presence and generous padding. Immediately following this panel, the quote form continues the vertical flow, with field groups stacked neatly and ample breathing room. At the bottom of the content column, a simple, text-based custom support link or CTA is aligned in a way that feels like a subtle footer element—either centered or slightly right-biased—providing a quiet, tertiary navigation option that closes out the page while preserving the corporate, high-contrast aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned at the very top of the white content band, directly below the dark grey navigation bar, the maintenance programs controller spans the full width of the centered content column (≈70–80% of the viewport, capped by the site’s max-width). The main page title sits aligned to the left, starting at the same left edge as other content elements, anchoring the user’s first focal point. If there is secondary text (such as a brief description or facility context), it appears directly beneath the title in a single column, with tight vertical spacing (≈8–16px) to keep this block compact and in line with the header density above. Optionally, a small summary label or total program count can align to the right on the same baseline as the title, mirroring how contact details sit opposite the logo in the black header band and reinforcing the left–right F-pattern. Overall height is modest, roughly 80–120px, with top and bottom padding of around 24px within the white band to give a clear but not overly large page header feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, participates in normal document flow; scrolls away with the page, sitting immediately beneath the fixed/global header bands.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the page controller, the maintenance filter bar forms a horizontal strip that visually reads as a functional band within the white content area. It spans nearly the full width of the centered content column, leaving small inner side padding (≈16–24px) so inputs do not touch the column edges. Filters are arranged in a left-to-right line or in two compact rows depending on viewport width, with the most important criteria (e.g., facility type, location) aligned to the left to meet the eye’s natural starting point. Each filter control (dropdowns, text fields, checkboxes) is moderately compact, using around 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, consistent with the navigation item sizing from the mockup. The filter bar’s overall vertical height remains shallow (≈64–96px) to preserve a dense, toolbox-like feeling near the top of the page. Minimal vertical margin (≈16px) separates it from the controller above and from the sort control below, reinforcing that these three elements form a tight functional cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in normal flow. On smaller screens, filter controls wrap to additional lines within the same band while retaining left alignment and centered overall within the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance sort control sits just below the filter bar, aligned to the right edge of the content column to complete the top Z-shaped scan line. Horizontally, it occupies roughly the rightmost 25–30% of the column, leaving roomy space to its left that can either remain empty or host a simple result count aligned to the left, emphasizing the visual split. Vertically, it is compact: a label (e.g., “Sort by”) and a dropdown or button group are arranged in a single row with 8–12px internal padding, producing a slim bar-like control that visually matches the compactness of utility elements in the mockup’s topmost grey band. There is only a small vertical gap (≈8–12px) above and below, so the sort control feels clearly tied to the filter bar above and the list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in normal flow. Right-aligned within the content column, and on narrow screens it can stack above the results count while maintaining its rightward emphasis.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance program list begins immediately below the sort control, occupying the full width of the centered content column and forming the main vertical content spine of the page. The top of the list is separated from the sort control by a slightly larger gap (≈24–32px) than the gaps used within the control cluster, signaling a transition from controls to content. Program cards are arranged in a single column, stacked vertically, each card spanning nearly the entire column width with small inner padding on the sides (≈16–24px) to keep text away from the edges. The list extends downward as needed, with consistent vertical spacing (≈24–32px) between cards to maintain clarity without feeling airy. This stacked structure recreates the central, dominant content column suggested by the mockup’s large central image, but replaces the single image with a repeated pattern of equally proportioned cards. The list dominates the middle portion of the viewport height and will typically be the primary scrollable content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable as part of the normal page flow. Cards within the list may visually highlight on hover or selection but do not change the list’s spatial behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each maintenance program card sits as a full-width, rectangular block within the list, aligned to the same left and right edges as the page title and other content in the column. The card height is moderate, roughly 120–180px depending on content density. Internally, the card is laid out with a subtle left–right structure reflecting the F-pattern: on the left, the program title and key metadata (facility type, frequency, coverage summary) stack vertically, starting at the card’s left padding edge. On the right, occupying roughly 30–40% of the card width, key quantitative info (pricing indication, duration, or status) and the main action (a red “View Details” or “Select Program” button) are aligned to the card’s right padding edge, echoing how the phone and address sit on the right in the black header band. There is clear separation between this right-side action area and the left text block, with a vertical alignment that allows the button to sit either mid- or bottom-aligned, depending on content. Cards have a defined border or subtle shadow and generous internal padding (≈16–24px), but only moderate spacing between cards (≈24–32px), yielding a structured, corporate feel rather than a highly airy one.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, sitting within the normal flow of the list. The entire card is clickable/selectable; on selection it may visually emphasize (e.g., bolder border or red accent) and trigger the detail panel below without altering its physical size significantly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance program detail panel appears below the program list as a larger, more prominent block that visually replaces the role of the hero image seen in the mockup. It is separated from the last program card by a generous vertical margin (≈32–48px), which helps it stand out as a new section. Horizontally, it remains centered and spans most of the content column (≈80–90% of the column width), with more substantial internal padding (≈24–32px) and possibly a strong top border or header strip in the brand’s red or black to anchor attention. Inside the panel, content may be arranged in a two-column layout within the same centered block: on the left (≈60% width), detailed description, scope, inclusions, and schedule information stack in text sections; on the right (≈40% width), key highlights, KPIs, and a summary of facility criteria are presented in a compact, boxed style reminiscent of the contact info cluster on the black header. Titles and section labels are left-aligned to match the rest of the page, while important numbers or badges may align to the right within their sub-blocks. The panel’s height is larger than any single card, effectively occupying a substantial portion of the viewport when in view, mirroring the visual dominance of the mockup’s central image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content flow, appearing in place when a program is selected and pushing subsequent content (quote form and CTA) downward. On long pages, users may scroll back up to the list; there is no sticky behavior by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance quote form is positioned directly below the detail panel, separated by a comfortable vertical gap (≈32px) to indicate a new but closely related step. Like the detail panel, it is centered and spans most of the content column (≈80–90% of the column width), with consistent left and right alignment so both components visually stack as a unified block. The form fields are arranged in a vertical sequence, following the page’s single-column flow, with occasional two-field rows (e.g., name and email side by side) aligned to the underlying grid. Each field group has moderate vertical spacing (≈16–24px) between them, giving the form a more open and approachable feel compared to the denser control cluster at the top. Section headings within the form (such as “Facility Details” or “Contact Information”) are left-aligned and slightly heavier, echoing the typography treatment of the page title but at a smaller scale. The primary submission button is placed at the bottom right of the form content area, aligning with the right edge of the fields and recalling the emphasis on right-hand calls to action seen in the header and cards. The form’s overall vertical footprint is substantial but well-organized, occupying a large portion of the lower page with clear breathing room above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in normal flow. The form extends the scrolling content and does not stick or float; it simply follows the detail panel and precedes the bottom CTA.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The custom maintenance support CTA sits at the very bottom of the content column, acting as a subtle, tertiary navigation element similar in visual weight to the utility links in the site’s top grey bar. It is separated from the quote form by a clear but not excessive vertical margin (≈32–40px), indicating the end of the main transactional flow. The CTA itself is relatively compact—typically a single line of text such as “Need a custom program? Contact our maintenance team” with a small arrow icon—aligned either centered or slightly right of center to mirror the right-biased emphasis of primary actions elsewhere. It does not span the full column width with a large button; instead, it appears as a concise text link or a small pill-shaped button, maintaining a low visual profile so it doesn’t compete with the quote submission action. There is additional whitespace (≈48px) below this CTA before the page footer or end of the white content band, giving a calm, finished feel to the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the content area. It scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the page and behaves like a normal text link or small button without fixed positioning.

name: Training & Certifications
title: Training & Certifications
description: Searchable catalog of training courses and certifications with keyword search, filters (category, duration, date range), sorting, and navigation to detailed course pages for registration.

core_features: Keyword search field that passes the entered query value to the searchTrainingCourses interface and synchronizes it with the query URL parameter.

Filter controls for category, minimum duration, and start-date range, with options loaded dynamically via getTrainingFilterOptions and values synchronized with category, min_duration_days, start_date_from, and start_date_to URL parameters.
Sorting control that manages the sort_by URL parameter and passes its value to searchTrainingCourses to order results (for example by price or date, as supported by the backend).
Results loader that calls searchTrainingCourses with the current URL parameter state and updates when any relevant parameter changes.

Course results list displaying course cards using the properties returned by searchTrainingCourses (course_id, title, category, duration_days, description_short, starting_from_price, next_session_start_date, next_session_location).
Navigation from each course card to the course detail page (course_detail.html) using the courseId URL parameter derived from the selected course_id.
Loading, empty, and error handling states for both filter options (getTrainingFilterOptions) and course search results (searchTrainingCourses), without relying on any hardcoded data.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Landing and browsing courses without pre-filters: Step 1: User navigates to training.html via the header navigation or direct URL without additional URL parameters. Step 2: On page load, the page reads the current URL parameters (which are empty or default) and invokes getTrainingFilterOptions to populate category and duration filter options. Step 3: Once filter options are loaded, the page invokes searchTrainingCourses with the current URL parameters (no query/category/dates/sort_by, so only default backend behavior applies). Step 4: The results list displays course cards based on the searchTrainingCourses response. Step 5: The user may adjust filters or sorting (e.g., choose a category, set minimum duration, define a start date range); each change updates the corresponding URL parameter and triggers a new call to searchTrainingCourses with the updated parameter set. Step 6: The user scrolls through and reviews the updated course cards.

Workflow 2 – Landing with pre-populated filters from another page: Step 1: The user clicks a link on index.html that navigates to training.html with some URL parameters set (such as query, category, or date range). Step 2: On page load, the page parses these URL parameters and pre-fills the search field and filters with the values from the URL. Step 3: The page calls getTrainingFilterOptions; once the options are available, the relevant selected options are matched to the pre-populated URL parameter values where applicable. Step 4: The page calls searchTrainingCourses using the query, category, min_duration_days, start_date_from, start_date_to, and sort_by values from the URL. Step 5: The user can refine search criteria; any change updates URL parameters and triggers searchTrainingCourses again. Step 6: The user reviews the filtered list of course cards.

Workflow 3 – Refining results with keyword search and filters: Step 1: The user types a term into the keyword search field in the search form. Step 2: The user submits the form (e.g., pressing Enter or clicking a search button). Step 3: The search form updates the query URL parameter with the entered keyword and removes any obsolete query values. Step 4: The results component detects the URL change and calls searchTrainingCourses with the updated query plus the existing filter and sorting parameters. Step 5: Updated results are displayed; if no results are returned, an empty state message is shown that is independent of any hardcoded example text.

Workflow 4 – Adjusting dates and duration: Step 1: The user selects a minimum duration via the duration filter control, which is populated via getTrainingFilterOptions.duration_options. Step 2: The duration filter updates the min_duration_days URL parameter based on the selected option. Step 3: The user selects a start date range (from and to) via date picker controls. Step 4: The date controls set or update start_date_from and start_date_to URL parameters in the correct format expected by searchTrainingCourses. Step 5: Each change to duration or dates triggers a new call to searchTrainingCourses with the full set of parameters, and the results list re-renders the course cards accordingly.

Workflow 5 – Sorting by price or other criteria: Step 1: The user chooses a sort option from the sorting control (for example, a lowest-price-first option). Step 2: The sort control updates the sort_by URL parameter with the corresponding sort key without hardcoding the meaning of that key beyond what the backend supports. Step 3: The results component calls searchTrainingCourses with the updated sort_by plus all current filters. Step 4: The results list reorders the course cards based on the response from searchTrainingCourses.

Workflow 6 – Navigating to a course detail page: Step 1: The user identifies a course of interest in the results list. Step 2: The user clicks the course card or a dedicated action element within the card. Step 3: The click handler reads the course_id from the selected course card data. Step 4: The browser navigates to course_detail.html with the courseId URL parameter set to that course_id value (e.g., course_detail.html?courseId={course_id}). Step 5: The course detail page (outside the scope of this design) then loads and can use courseId as needed.

Workflow 7 – Sharing or bookmarking filtered search results: Step 1: After applying keyword, filter, and sorting choices, the user copies the browser URL. Step 2: Because the current query, category, min_duration_days, start_date_from, start_date_to, and sort_by values are all reflected in the URL, another user opening that URL (or the same user returning later) sees the filters and search field pre-populated based on those parameters. Step 3: On load, the page re-runs getTrainingFilterOptions and searchTrainingCourses with the URL parameters, reconstructing the same result set ordering without relying on any hardcoded data.
interactions: Typing in the keyword search field updates internal component state; submitting the search form triggers an onSubmit handler that updates the query URL parameter and initiates a call to searchTrainingCourses with the full set of current parameters.

Changing the category filter triggers an onChange handler that updates the category URL parameter to the selected value (or removes it if cleared), then calls searchTrainingCourses with the updated parameter set.

Changing the minimum duration filter triggers an onChange handler that updates the min_duration_days URL parameter using the selected option's min_days value from getTrainingFilterOptions, then re-invokes searchTrainingCourses.
Selecting or clearing the start date range triggers onChange handlers on the date pickers that update start_date_from and start_date_to URL parameters; any change re-triggers searchTrainingCourses.
Changing the sorting option triggers an onChange handler on the sort control that updates the sort_by URL parameter and causes a fresh call to searchTrainingCourses with the selected sort criterion.

On initial page load (and on any browser navigation that changes URL parameters), a central initialization or onURLChange handler reads all relevant URL parameters, ensures they are reflected in the search and filter controls, then calls getTrainingFilterOptions followed by searchTrainingCourses.

Clicking a course card (or a specific detail link within it) triggers an onClick handler that reads the card's course_id property and performs navigation to course_detail.html with courseId set in the query string.

If getTrainingFilterOptions returns an error, the filters panel displays an error state via an internal flag; users may trigger a retry action that re-calls getTrainingFilterOptions without modifying URL parameters.
If searchTrainingCourses returns an error, the results area displays an error state and a retry control that reuses the current URL parameters to re-call searchTrainingCourses.

If searchTrainingCourses returns an empty array, the results area displays an empty state message and optionally suggests adjusting filters, without referencing any specific static course names or categories.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully derived from URL parameters and API responses. On every load or URL change, the page reads query, category, min_duration_days, start_date_from, start_date_to, and sort_by from the URL query string. These parameters are considered the single source of truth for the current search and filter state. The filter and search controls initialize their visible values based on these URL parameters. The getTrainingFilterOptions interface is called once per load (or upon explicit user retry) to retrieve dynamic lists of categories and duration options; these results determine the available filter options. The searchTrainingCourses interface is called with the current set of URL parameters, omitting any parameter that is not present in the URL, so that default backend behavior applies. When the user interacts with search, filters, or sorting, the corresponding handlers update the URL parameters (adding, changing, or removing keys), and the change in URL triggers a new call to searchTrainingCourses with the updated parameters. No search or filter state is stored in localStorage or any other persistent client store; the URL and live API responses are the only drivers of visible state. Navigation to course_detail.html uses a courseId URL parameter so that the chosen course can be retrieved there without any local client state.

components.functionality: Initializes and maintains the list of available filter options for categories and duration thresholds by calling getTrainingFilterOptions, and exposes this data to dependent components such as the filter panel.
Manages the keyword search input, synchronizes it with the query URL parameter, and triggers new searches via searchTrainingCourses when submitted.

Provides controls for category, minimum duration, and start-date range filters; maps selections to the category, min_duration_days, start_date_from, and start_date_to URL parameters; and triggers updated searches when any filter changes.
Allows the user to select a sort option, updates the sort_by URL parameter accordingly, and triggers searchTrainingCourses to reload results using the selected sort order.
Central component that invokes searchTrainingCourses whenever relevant URL parameters change, tracks loading and error states, and provides the resulting course list to the results list component.
Renders the list of course cards based on the array returned by searchTrainingCourses, and delegates click events on each card to navigate to the corresponding course detail page.

Represents a single course in the results list, binding to one element of the searchTrainingCourses response and exposing the course_id for navigation as well as displaying key course/session attributes.

Controls the visual state of the results area based on the outcome of searchTrainingCourses, showing loading state, empty state when the returned array is empty, and error state when the search fails, with a retry action.
Controls the state of the filter panel based on getTrainingFilterOptions, showing loading, error, and loaded states and enabling a user-triggered retry for loading filter options.
Handles navigation from a course card to the course detail page by reading the attached course_id and constructing a URL to course_detail.html with courseId set to that value as a query parameter.

Keeps the UI components (search form, filters, sort control) synchronized with URL parameters and notifies data-loader components when the URL changes so they can re-fetch data via getTrainingFilterOptions or searchTrainingCourses as appropriate.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the stacked full-width band structure from the mockup (utility bar → black header → dark grey nav → white content). Within the white content band, use a centered max-width container that behaves as a single main column, but internally split that column into a left filter sidebar and right results area beneath a full-width search bar. The page reads top-to-bottom: global header/nav, then the Training & Certifications search block, then filters and listings.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the stacked full-width band structure from the mockup (utility bar → black header → dark grey nav → white content). Within the white content band, use a centered max-width container that behaves as a single main column, but internally split that column into a left filter sidebar and right results area beneath a full-width search bar. The page reads top-to-bottom: global header/nav, then the Training & Certifications search block, then filters and listings.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport for the existing header bands, unchanged from the mockup. The Training & Certifications page content occupies the remaining 75–80% with a tall, relatively spacious white area. Inside this area, the keyword search form takes the top 15–20% of the content band as a dominant horizontal block. Below it, the inner grid allocates about 25–30% of the content width to the left filter sidebar and 70–75% to the right results column. Vertical spacing between content sections follows the 32–48px rhythm, while internal padding in components uses 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The upper header remains moderately dense. The search form is visually prominent but not overly crowded—large input with concise controls. The filter sidebar is more compact, stacking multiple controls vertically with minimal but clear separation. The results column is moderately dense: vertically stacked course cards with comfortable separation, each tightly organizing a fair amount of information. There is generous white space at the outer sides of the centered content container to keep the layout from feeling heavy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow the existing F/Z pattern: eye starts at logo and utility bar, moves across the black header to contact info, then across the dark grey nav where the 'Training & Certifications' item is highlighted in red. From that active nav item, the eye drops directly onto the large centered search form just below. From the search form, the eye moves left to the filter sidebar headings, then sweeps right across to the sort control and down through the vertical list of course cards. Red accents on active filters, sort state, and the course detail buttons anchor attention at key interaction points.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar stacked header bands (light grey utility bar, black brand header, dark grey navigation), the page opens into a clean white content area centered on the screen within a max-width container. At the very top of this white band, a wide, horizontally oriented Training & Certifications search form spans about 70–80% of the content width, centered with generous left and right margins. This search block effectively replaces the large landscape image seen in the mockup, becoming the new focal 'hero' element. Beneath it, the content column splits into two: a left-aligned, narrow filter sidebar and a wider right-hand results column. The filter panel is a tall, light-bordered block that starts just below the search form and runs down the left third of the inner grid, stacking filter groups vertically. To its right, aligned at the same top baseline, a compact sort control sits flush to the top-right of the results column, followed by a vertical stack of full-width course cards. Each card is a white rectangle with subtle borders and 16–24px padding, separated by consistent vertical gaps that give the list a structured but breathable rhythm. State-based messages (loading, no results, filter loading) appear centered within their respective regions without shifting the overall grid. The entire composition preserves the mockup’s strong horizontal bands and high-contrast black/red accents, while the central content feels orderly and professional, appropriate for a construction and engineering training catalog.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual data loader is conceptually positioned at the top of the white content area, logically wrapping the filter sidebar. It does not occupy any visible space on the page. When active, its effect is only seen through the training-filter-state-view inside the filter panel (e.g., showing skeletons or a loading message), but the overall layout of the search form, filters, and results does not shift. It sits in the hierarchy just above the training-filter-panel so that the panel’s content is populated once options are ready.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; occupies no layout space. Influences only the rendered content inside the filter panel through state changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form sits at the top center of the white content band, directly below the dark grey navigation bar, with about 32–40px of vertical space separating it from the nav. It is horizontally centered within the max-width container and spans roughly 70–80% of that container, leaving wide side margins that echo the spacious hero image margins in the mockup. Vertically, the form appears as a single, tall horizontal block: a primary keyword input stretching across most of the width, with a prominent red or dark button to the right or directly below, depending on available width. The form has 16–24px of padding inside a subtle light-grey or white panel with a faint border to distinguish it from the bare white background. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical space below the form before the split layout of filters and results begins, making the search the primary focal element when the content area first appears.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element, anchored at the top of the main content column. On smaller screens, it collapses into a full-width block above filters and results, maintaining its centered prominence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter sidebar is positioned directly beneath the search form, aligned to the left edge of the centered content container. It begins about 24px below the bottom of the search form and runs vertically down the page. Within the main content width, it occupies roughly 25–30% (about one quarter to one third) of the inner grid, allowing enough width for clear form controls but clearly appearing as the narrower column. The panel is rendered as a tall white or very light grey rectangle with a thin light-grey border, mirroring the card style used in course results. Inside, filter groups (e.g., Category, Duration, Date Range) are stacked vertically with 16–24px padding around the edges and 16px spacing between each group. Each group uses a bold or uppercase heading aligned to the left, with control elements (checkboxes, dropdowns, date pickers) arranged in a single column to keep the look straightforward and slightly classic. The top of the filter panel aligns horizontally with the top of the results column’s content (just below the sort control), maintaining a clean horizontal baseline across the main inner grid. The panel extends down to match or slightly exceed the height of the visible results list, forming a solid vertical anchor on the left side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar within the centered content container. On narrower viewports, it can stack beneath the search form and above the results (still full-width), but on desktop it remains a left column occupying about 25–30% of the content width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is located in the right-hand results column, horizontally aligned with the top of the filter panel. It sits about 24px below the search form, flush against the top-right edge of the results column (which itself begins to the right of the filter panel with a 24–32px gutter between sidebar and results). The control occupies a relatively small horizontal area—about 25–30% of the results column width—and is right-aligned so that the label (e.g., 'Sort by') and dropdown appear in the upper-right corner, creating a natural F-pattern endpoint after scanning from the filter headings on the left. There is a modest 16–24px vertical gap between the sort control and the first course card. The design is compact: no heavy panel, just label and a dropdown or segmented control on a plain white background, with a subtle underline or border on the dropdown. Red may be used for the active sort option or focus state, tying it into the brand's accent color.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element, fixed at the top of the results column’s content. It scrolls away with the results list and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results loader occupies the same rectangular area as the training-results-list in the right-hand column but is only visible during data fetching. It is centered within the results column, starting just below the sort control. While active, it displays a spinner or loading indicator centered both horizontally and vertically within the available results area, possibly with a simple 'Loading courses…' label. The loader does not change the width or position of the results column; instead, it overlays or temporarily replaces the course cards area while preserving the overall grid: filters remain visible on the left, and the search form and sort control keep their positions above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-structural overlay/placeholder within the results column; does not affect surrounding layout geometry besides temporarily replacing card content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list resides in the right-hand column to the immediate right of the filter panel, separated by a consistent vertical gutter of about 24–32px. It begins directly below the sort control, with about 16–24px of vertical space between them, and spans the remaining 70–75% of the inner content width. The list is a vertical stack of full-width course cards, each extending from the left to the right edge of the results column. There is a uniform vertical gap of 16–24px between cards to keep the column visually structured but not crowded. The list can extend well beyond the initial viewport height, producing a vertical scroll while the header bands and content container structure remain intact. Any pagination or 'Load more' controls appear at the bottom of this list, centered within the results column with extra vertical spacing (~24–32px) separating them from the last card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable vertical list within the page. It grows in height with the number of courses; only the browser viewport constrains its visible portion. The list itself is not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each training course card is a horizontally oriented rectangular block that fills 100% of the results column width. The card height is variable depending on content but typically occupies about 1/5 to 1/4 of the viewport height per item on desktop, giving 3–4 cards visible at a time. The card has a white background with a light grey 1px border and possibly a very subtle shadow, matching the conservative, corporate aesthetic. Inside, there is 16–24px of padding on all sides. The content layout follows a clear hierarchy: at the top left, the course title appears in a larger, bold type, taking one or two lines. Directly beneath, a row of key metadata (e.g., category, duration, level) is laid out horizontally with small separators, all left-aligned. A short description paragraph follows, constrained to 2–3 lines to maintain a compact height. At the bottom of the card, a horizontal footer row holds secondary information on the left (e.g., upcoming date, location or delivery method) and the primary call-to-action ('View Details' / 'Register') on the right, implemented via the course-detail-navigation button. The bottom row aligns with the right edge and uses the brand red for the button, making it the key interaction focal point. Vertical spacing within the card follows 8–16px steps, keeping the content dense yet readable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level elements within the results list. They expand vertically based on content but maintain consistent width and internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results state view occupies the same spatial region as the training-results-list within the right-hand column. When active (e.g., no results, error), it replaces the card stack with a centered message. The message block is horizontally centered within the results column and positioned slightly above the vertical midpoint, approximately one third down from the top of the visible results area, to keep it closer to the sort control and within easy scanning range. It may include a simple icon above a short explanatory text and an optional action (e.g., 'Clear filters'), aligned to the center. The left filter panel and the top search form remain visible and unchanged, reinforcing that the cause of the state is related to filtering or search criteria rather than a global error.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional placeholder within the results column; it fully replaces the results list but keeps the same size and position in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter state view appears within the bounds of the training-filter-panel, taking over the internal area when filters are loading or unavailable. It is centered both horizontally and vertically inside the filter panel’s bordered rectangle, typically displaying a small spinner or brief status text like 'Loading filter options…'. The panel’s dimensions, position to the left of the results, and relationship to the search form and sort control remain unchanged; only the content inside the filter panel changes. Once filter options are loaded, this state view is replaced by the full stack of filter controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional content inside the filter panel; it does not alter the panel’s size or its placement within the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within each training-course-card, the course detail navigation appears at the bottom right corner as the primary call-to-action element. It is rendered as a rectangular button or bold text link aligned to the right edge of the card’s content area. The button uses the brand’s red background with white text to stand out against the card’s white surface, typically measuring around 40–48px in height with 16–24px horizontal padding. It is vertically aligned within the card’s bottom row, opposite secondary information (such as date and location) on the left, creating a balanced horizontal bar that visually closes the card. On hover, a darker red or subtle border change reinforces its interactivity. The button sits about 8–12px above the card’s bottom padding edge, with consistent spacing from adjacent text elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the card’s layout; scrolls with its parent card. No fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL sync manager is a purely behavioral component that does not produce any visible UI. Conceptually, it wraps or connects the search form, filter panel, sort control, and results list so that their current state is reflected in and restored from the page URL. It has no width, height, or visual representation and therefore does not occupy space or alter the layout. Its presence is only perceived when reloading or sharing URLs that preserve the current search, filter, and sort configuration.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, non-spatial; operates at the application state level without affecting the physical layout.

name: Course Detail & Registration
title: Course Detail & Registration
description: Displays a specific training course with available sessions and allows users to register participants for a selected session on the same page.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for a specific training course based on the courseId URL parameter using getTrainingCourseDetail.
List all sessions for the course (dates, location, availability, pricing) returned by getTrainingCourseDetail.
Allow the user to select a specific session (date/location) from the available sessions that are scheduled and have seats remaining.
Allow the user to specify the number of participants and see an estimated total price based on the selected session or the course base price.
Provide a 'Register Now' action that scrolls to or reveals the registration form on the same page with the selected session pre-filled.
Provide a registration form that collects participant name, participant email, number of participants, and submits the registration via submitCourseRegistration.
Display confirmation or error messages after registration, including registration status and total_price from submitCourseRegistration.
user_workflows:
View course & sessions and then register:
1) User navigates to course_detail.html with a courseId in the URL.
2) On page load, the page calls getTrainingCourseDetail with the courseId parameter.
3) The course information and the list of sessions are displayed.
4) The user reviews course details and selects a desired session from the sessions list.
5) The user enters the number of participants.
6) The page calculates and displays the estimated total price using the selected session price_per_participant if available, otherwise using course.base_price_per_participant.
7) The user clicks 'Register Now', which scrolls to or reveals the registration form.
8) The registration form is pre-filled with the selected course and session context and the current number of participants.
9) The user enters participant_name and participant_email.
10) The user submits the form, triggering submitCourseRegistration.
11) On success, the page shows a confirmation message with registration_id, status, and total_price, and may lock or reset the form.
12) On failure, the page shows an error message and keeps the form for correction/resubmission.

Deep-linking to a specific session and participant count:
1) A user receives or creates a link to course_detail.html that includes courseId, and optionally sessionId and number_of_participants as URL parameters.
2) On page load, getTrainingCourseDetail is called with courseId.
3) Once sessions are loaded, the page looks for a session whose session_id matches the sessionId URL parameter.
4) If a matching, selectable session is found, that session is pre-selected in the sessions list.
5) If number_of_participants is present and valid in the URL, it is used to pre-fill the participants input.
6) The estimated total price is calculated and displayed immediately.
7) The user can adjust selections or proceed directly to 'Register Now' and complete the registration.

Handling unavailable or full sessions:
1) User navigates to course_detail.html with a courseId.
2) getTrainingCourseDetail returns the course and its sessions.
3) For sessions with status not equal to 'scheduled' or with remaining_seats less than or equal to zero (when remaining_seats is provided), the page displays them as non-selectable with an appropriate unavailable indication.
4) The user can only select sessions that are scheduled and, when remaining_seats is available, have at least one remaining seat.
5) If the URL specifies a sessionId that is not selectable, the page does not pre-select it and may show a notice that the requested session is no longer available.

Navigating back to the course catalog:
1) User is viewing course_detail.html for a given course.
2) The user clicks the 'Back to all courses' link.
3) The page navigates to training.html without additional parameters (or using any navigation pattern defined globally).
interactions: On page initialization, read the courseId (and optional sessionId and number_of_participants) from the URL and call getTrainingCourseDetail with courseId.
When getTrainingCourseDetail returns, bind the course object to the course detail components and the sessions array to the session list component.

When the user clicks on a session entry (e.g., radio button or selectable row), update the selected sessionId in the page state and recalculate the estimated total price based on the selected session's price_per_participant or the course base price.
When the user changes the number of participants input, validate it (e.g., enforce minimum of 1, and optionally respect remaining_seats when available) and recalculate the estimated total price.
When the selected session or number_of_participants changes, update the URL (e.g., via history API) to include or update sessionId and number_of_participants parameters, without reloading the page.

When the user clicks the 'Register Now' button, scroll or jump to the registration form component (e.g., via an in-page anchor) and ensure the selected session and number_of_participants are reflected in the form fields.
When the user submits the registration form, validate that courseId, sessionId, number_of_participants, participant_name, and participant_email are present and valid before sending.
On registration form submission, call submitCourseRegistration with courseId, sessionId, number_of_participants, participant_name, and participant_email.

On successful submitCourseRegistration response (success = true), show a confirmation message including registration_id, status, total_price, and message, and optionally prevent duplicate re-submission for the same registration.
On unsuccessful submitCourseRegistration response (success = false) or transport error, display an error message, keep the form values, and allow the user to correct inputs or try again.
On clicking 'Back to all courses', navigate to training.html (clearing courseDetail-specific URL parameters).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is controlled via URL parameters and in-memory UI state derived from API responses. The courseId URL parameter is required to fetch and display the correct course; if it is missing or invalid, the page shows a load/error state and does not call submitCourseRegistration. Optional URL parameters sessionId and number_of_participants represent a pre-selected session and participant count. On initial load, getTrainingCourseDetail is called using courseId.
