Once the sessions are available, if sessionId exists in the URL and matches a selectable session (status 'scheduled' and, when remaining_seats is available, greater than or equal to the requested number_of_participants), that session is set as the selected session in the UI. If number_of_participants is provided and valid (e.g., a positive integer), it initializes the participants input. Whenever the user selects a different session or changes the number_of_participants in the UI, the page updates the URL query string to reflect the current sessionId and number_of_participants without reloading, so the state can be shared and restored on refresh. The registration form reads the current in-memory state (selected course, selected session, number_of_participants) rather than re-parsing the URL, but the URL parameters ensure that a reload reinitializes the same state. No state is persisted using localStorage or similar mechanisms; all persistence for the course and registration data resides in the backend APIs and the URL parameters.

components.functionality: Reads courseId (and optional sessionId and number_of_participants) from the URL, calls getTrainingCourseDetail with courseId, and exposes the loaded course and sessions to other components along with loading and error states.
event_handlers: onInit: read courseId, sessionId, number_of_participants from URL and call getTrainingCourseDetail(courseId)
onUrlParamChange: if courseId changes, refetch with getTrainingCourseDetail; if sessionId or number_of_participants change, update derived UI state without refetching course
onLoadSuccess: publish course and sessions data to dependent components
onLoadError: set an error state to be displayed by dependent components

components.functionality: Displays high-level information about the course, such as title, category, duration, base price per participant, and tags, using the course data loaded by course-detail-loader.
event_handlers: onCourseDataChange: update displayed title, category, description, duration_days, base_price_per_participant, and tags when course data is updated or reloaded

components.functionality: Shows the detailed description of the course and any additional descriptive fields that are provided in the course object, helping users understand course content before registering.
event_handlers: onCourseDataChange: update description content when course.description changes

components.functionality: Lists all sessions for the course and allows the user to select exactly one active session (date/location). It filters out non-selectable sessions (e.g., cancelled, completed, or with no remaining seats when remaining_seats is provided) from being chosen, but they may still be displayed as unavailable.
event_handlers: onSessionsDataChange: render or update the list of sessions using the sessions array from getTrainingCourseDetail
onUrlStateInit: when initialized, read the preferred sessionId from page state (derived from the URL) and pre-select the matching available session, if any
onSessionClick: when the user selects a session, set that session as selected in the shared page state, validate against remaining_seats, and trigger recalculation of estimated total price
onSessionSelectionStateChange: update the URL sessionId parameter to match the newly selected session without reloading the page

components.functionality: Allows the user to specify the number of participants and displays an estimated total price based on the selected session or the course base price per participant. It validates participant count inputs and optionally compares them with remaining_seats for the selected session.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl: if number_of_participants is present in the URL and valid, initialize the participants input with that value; otherwise initialize with a default valid value

onParticipantsChange: validate the input as a positive integer, ensure it does not exceed remaining_seats when that field is available for the selected session, recalculate estimated total price, and update the URL number_of_participants parameter
onSelectedSessionChange: when the selected session changes, recalculate estimated total price using session.price_per_participant if provided; otherwise fall back to course.base_price_per_participant
onCourseOrSessionPriceChange: recalculate estimated total price whenever relevant price fields change due to refreshed data

components.functionality: Provides the 'Register Now' action that moves the user focus to the registration form. It ensures that a valid session and participant count are selected before enabling or acting on the click.
event_handlers: onStateChange: enable the button only when there is a selected session that is selectable and the number_of_participants input is valid
onClick: if state is valid, scroll or jump to the registration form component using an in-page anchor or programmatic focus

components.functionality: Captures registration details for the selected course session, including participant_name, participant_email, and number_of_participants, then calls submitCourseRegistration to create a CourseRegistration. It also displays any confirmation or error messages returned by the API.
event_handlers: onFormInit: pre-fill non-editable fields such as selected course title, selected session dates/location, and the current number_of_participants from shared state for user confirmation

onSessionOrParticipantsStateChange: keep the summary information in the form synchronized with any changes made in the session selector or participants panel before the user begins typing participant details
onInputChange: update local form state for participant_name and participant_email as the user types and validate for required fields and format (e.g., email structure) before allowing submission
onSubmit: validate that courseId, selected sessionId, number_of_participants, participant_name, and participant_email are all present and valid, then call submitCourseRegistration with those values

onSubmitSuccess: when submitCourseRegistration returns with success = true, bind the response to a CourseRegistration-like state object, display a confirmation message using registration_id, status, total_price, and message, and optionally disable further edits or submissions for this registration instance
onSubmitError: when submitCourseRegistration returns success = false or an error occurs, show an error message, keep form values, and allow the user to correct data and resubmit

components.functionality: Shows real-time feedback for registration attempts, including loading state during submission, success confirmations, and error messages, drawing from the state set by the registration-form component.
event_handlers: onSubmissionStart: display a loading or 'processing' state while submitCourseRegistration is in progress
onSubmissionSuccess: display confirmation details including registration status and total_price and clear any previous error messages
onSubmissionFailure: display the error message from the API response if available or a generic error message if not

components.functionality: Provides navigation back to the training course catalog page (training.html).
event_handlers: onClick: navigate to training.html, optionally clearing the current courseId, sessionId, and number_of_participants URL parameters since they are specific to course_detail.html

components.functionality: Manages and displays error or empty states when courseId is missing, getTrainingCourseDetail fails, or no sessions are available for registration.
event_handlers: onInit: if courseId is missing from the URL, set and display an error state indicating that no course could be loaded
onLoadError: when getTrainingCourseDetail fails, display a generic or API-provided error message and hide interactive registration components
onSessionsDataChange: if sessions is empty or contains no selectable sessions (scheduled with seats), display a message indicating that no sessions are currently available for registration

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing site chrome (light grey utility bar → black header → dark grey navigation), the Course Detail & Registration page uses a single, centered content column within a white background band. Inside this column, elements are stacked in a clear top‑to‑bottom order: back navigation link at the very top, then a hero‑like course summary header, followed by the course description. Below that, a visually grouped registration area combines the session list on the left and participants/pricing on the right within a shared horizontal band, echoing the F/Z‑pattern. The registration form and any feedback/status banners are placed below this band, still in the same centered column. Loader and error/empty states temporarily override or sit on top of this main content column while staying centered.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing site chrome (light grey utility bar → black header → dark grey navigation), the Course Detail & Registration page uses a single, centered content column within a white background band. Inside this column, elements are stacked in a clear top‑to‑bottom order: back navigation link at the very top, then a hero‑like course summary header, followed by the course description. Below that, a visually grouped registration area combines the session list on the left and participants/pricing on the right within a shared horizontal band, echoing the F/Z‑pattern. The registration form and any feedback/status banners are placed below this band, still in the same centered column. Loader and error/empty states temporarily override or sit on top of this main content column while staying centered.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The stacked site header consumes the top ~20–25% of the viewport as on other pages. The main white content band occupies the remaining ~75–80%. Within that, the hero‑like course summary and description together take roughly the upper third of the content area. The registration band (session selector + participants/pricing + register button) occupies the middle third, emphasizing conversion, and the registration form plus feedback occupy the lower third. Horizontally, the main content column spans about 60–70% of the viewport on large screens (max-width container), centered with generous left/right margins. Internal elements respect the 8px spacing scale, with 24–48px vertical gaps between major sections and 12–24px padding inside cards and panels.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is slightly compact in information‑heavy areas, mirroring the mockup’s header behavior, but the column layout and white margins prevent clutter. The course summary header and description are moderately dense with clear hierarchy and comfortable line lengths. The registration band is denser, containing interactive controls and pricing details in a relatively tight vertical space, but remains structured with clear grouping and consistent padding. The registration form below is form‑dense but still separated into sections with breathable vertical spacing. Loader and error/empty states are minimalistic with centered content, keeping visual noise low when active.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows an F/Z‑pattern adapted for this page. The eye starts top‑left at the back‑to‑courses link, then moves right across the course title and key course details in the summary header. From there, it drops slightly to scan the description text, then moves down to the registration band where the eye first engages with the left‑aligned session list and then moves horizontally to the right‑hand participants/pricing panel and the prominent red 'Register Now' button. After the button, the eye continues downward into the registration form. Status banners appear directly above the form, so they are seen immediately after interaction. Strong horizontal bands, left alignment of text, and red accents on interactive elements guide this flow.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: Immediately below the dark grey navigation bar, a wide white content band fills the rest of the screen. In the center of this band sits a constrained, single main column that feels solid and corporate, with wide white margins on either side. At the very top of this column, a small, left‑aligned 'Back to Courses' link anchors the start of the content. Just below, a black‑and‑red accented course summary header spans the full width of the column, playing the role of a hero: the course title and key facts draw attention in a compact but bold block. Under this, the course description flows in a slightly narrower text block, left‑aligned and easy to read. Further down, a broader horizontal registration section appears, almost like a functional dashboard: on the left, a list of available sessions runs vertically, while on the right, a tighter panel shows participants and pricing, with a red 'Register Now' button sitting firmly at the lower right of this panel. Clicking to register draws the eye downward to a structured registration form that fills the column width. If there’s feedback—success or errors—a colored banner sits just above this form, bridging the interaction loop. When loading or when no sessions are available, a centered message or overlay replaces or sits on top of these sections, but always within the same central column, preserving the orderly stacked‑band aesthetic seen in the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The course-detail-loader appears within the main white content area, directly below the dark grey navigation band, and covers the entire central content column from the top edge of the back-to-courses link down to the bottom of the viewport (or available content height). It presents as a semi-opaque white overlay that dims any underlying content while a small spinner and 'Loading course…' text are vertically and horizontally centered within the content column. On wide screens, this centered loader occupies roughly the middle third of the viewport both horizontally and vertically, preserving generous white space around it and maintaining the structured, serious feel of the site.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay attached to the main content container, blocking interaction with all underlying components while visible; scroll position is effectively frozen relative to the content beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The course-summary-header is the first major block in the white content band, positioned immediately below a small gap after the dark grey navigation (and sitting just below the back-to-courses link). It spans the full width of the centered content column (about 60–70% of the viewport on desktop, within a max-width wrapper) and visually reads like a hero header. The block height is moderate—roughly 200–260px depending on content—with 24–32px of padding on all sides. Inside, content is left-aligned with the course title occupying the upper left area, taking roughly the left 70% of the block width, while a compact right-hand column (the remaining ~30%) contains key course metadata (duration, level, location, etc.) vertically stacked. A subtle bottom border or slight contrast in background (e.g., very light grey) visually separates this header from the description below, and red accents (e.g., underlines or small badges) reinforce the brand without overwhelming the clean, rectangular structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section in the normal document flow, anchoring the top of the course detail content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The course-description-section sits directly below the course-summary-header, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. It aligns with the same centered content column but may use a slightly narrower text width inside (e.g., 70–80% of the column) for comfortable reading, centered within the column. The section begins with a left-aligned subheading such as 'Course Description' in bold, with a red accent line or bar beneath to echo the brand. Paragraph text flows beneath in a single column, with 16–24px line spacing and 16–24px vertical spacing between paragraphs, creating an airy, readable block. The total height of this section may occupy about 20–30% of the main content area, depending on text length, with a clear 32–40px gap before the start of the more functional registration area below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in normal flow, expanding vertically based on content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session-list-selector forms the left portion of a broader 'registration band' that starts roughly one screen height below the top of the white content area, directly after the course-description-section with about 32–40px of vertical separation. This band appears as a single card or panel spanning the full width of the centered content column, with a thin grey border or subtle shadow. Inside the card, the internal layout becomes two columns on desktop: the session-list-selector occupies approximately 60–65% of the card’s width on the left. It is aligned to the top of this band, with its own title (e.g., 'Available Sessions') at the top left and a vertical list of sessions underneath. Each session row runs almost the full width of the left column, arranged with left-aligned text and right-aligned selection controls (radio buttons or similar). Vertical spacing between rows is tight but consistent (12–16px), giving a compact, data-focused feel similar to a corporate schedule. On narrower screens, this selector stacks above the participants-and-pricing-panel, still full width within the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the registration band, behaves as a left-side column that transitions to full-width stacking on smaller viewports; otherwise follows normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The participants-and-pricing-panel shares the same registration card as the session-list-selector and occupies the right-hand side on larger screens. Within the card’s internal grid, it takes about 35–40% of the width, aligned to the right and starting at the same vertical level as the session list’s header. The panel is visually framed—either via a subtle light grey background or an inner border—to make it feel like a compact summary box. Inside, content is vertically stacked: at the top, a bold label such as 'Participants & Pricing'; beneath, controls for adding participants (quantities or attendee types) and a clear price breakdown. The panel height is similar to or slightly shorter than the session list; any extra vertical space is left blank at the bottom, emphasizing the right‑aligned 'Register Now' button. Padding inside the panel remains consistent (16–24px), leading to a moderately dense but organized look. On mobile, this panel drops below the session list and stretches to the full width of the content column as a standalone block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a right-side column within the registration band on wider screens and reflows below the session list in a stacked layout on small screens; otherwise follows normal flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The register-now-trigger is visually anchored within the participants-and-pricing-panel as the primary call-to-action. On desktop, it is positioned at the bottom-right corner of that panel, with at least 16–24px padding from the panel edges. The button itself is rectangular with sharp corners, solid red fill, and white text, matching the brand’s high-contrast style. It spans only as wide as its label plus generous horizontal padding (16–24px per side), so it reads as a compact but prominent control rather than a full-width bar. Vertically, it sits 16–24px below the last pricing detail line, ensuring a clear visual separation between information and action. On narrower screens where the participants-and-pricing-panel becomes full width, the button remains aligned to the right edge within that block but may stretch slightly in width for usability while keeping margin on both sides. The space below the button (before the registration form) is about 24–32px, so when the form appears, it feels clearly like the next step in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard button anchored within the participants-and-pricing-panel; it does not float or stick but may cause the registration form below to expand downward when it triggers form visibility.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-form is positioned directly below the registration band (session-list-selector plus participants-and-pricing-panel) with a vertical gap of approximately 32–40px to create a clear separation between configuration and data entry. It spans the full width of the centered content column, matching the width of the course summary and registration card above. The form is structured in stacked sections (e.g., 'Contact Information', 'Participant Details', 'Company Details') with section headings left-aligned. On larger screens, form fields within a section can be arranged in a simple two-column grid (e.g., 50/50 split) to keep vertical height manageable yet maintain a classic look; on narrow screens, these fields stack to a single column. Each field row has 8–16px vertical spacing, and sections are separated by 24–32px, mirroring the mockup’s moderate density. The bottom of the form leaves a comfortable 48–64px margin before the footer area to avoid a cramped appearance when the user reaches the end.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a standard block in normal flow. It may initially be collapsed or hidden until the user selects a session and clicks 'Register Now', at which point it expands downward, pushing subsequent content (if any) further down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The registration-feedback-banner sits immediately above the registration-form within the same centered content column. When visible, it spans the full width of that column and has a relatively low profile height (roughly 56–80px), with 16–24px padding and left-aligned text. It uses strong background colors keyed to status—red or dark red for errors, green for success—contrasting sharply with the white content area while maintaining the site’s corporate seriousness. The banner sits about 16–24px below the bottom edge of the registration band to keep it connected to the action that caused it, yet visually distinct. There is a small vertical gap (12–16px) between the banner’s bottom edge and the top of the registration form, creating a clear visual hierarchy: status message first, form second. On long pages, this ensures that any feedback is seen as soon as the user scrolls back to the form area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block in normal flow, appearing and disappearing without changing the layout of other regions beyond shifting them up or down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-courses-link is the first element in the main white content band, positioned directly under the dark grey navigation bar with a small top margin of about 16–24px. It is left-aligned to the centered content column, matching the left edge of the course-summary-header. The link is relatively small and unobtrusive, consisting of a left-pointing chevron icon and text like 'Back to Courses', displayed in a dark grey or black with a red hover state. There is 8–12px of padding above and below the link’s text line, and it sits 16–24px above the course-summary-header, providing a clear but subtle breadcrumb-like affordance without competing with the main title. Horizontally, it occupies only the space needed by its text, leaving the rest of the row empty, which reinforces the top-left starting point of the visual flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element at the top of the content column, remaining in place as the user scrolls; it is not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-and-empty-state-handler occupies the same central content column and overall white content band as the main page content but is used when loading fails or when there are no available sessions or course data. In a full-error scenario (e.g., course not found), it replaces the course-summary-header, description, and registration sections with a centered message block. This block is horizontally centered within the column and vertically positioned roughly in the middle of the viewport’s remaining height (below the site header), occupying about the central third of that space. It may include a simple icon, a bold heading (e.g., 'Course Not Available'), concise explanation text, and a red-accented action link or button (such as 'Back to Courses'), all stacked and center-aligned. In cases of an empty session list but valid course data, the handler appears as a secondary card directly beneath the session-list-selector area inside the registration band, spanning the full width of that band and using a light grey background to indicate informational state. In all cases, spacing around the message follows the 24–32px vertical margin pattern so that it feels deliberate and consistent with the rest of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally replaces or augments normal content while staying within the same centered column; does not float over content except in extreme global-error implementations, where it can be vertically centered within the content area.

name: Cost Calculator
title: Cost Calculator
description: Interactive page where users configure construction/engineering project parameters, calculate an estimated project cost, and optionally submit that estimate for email delivery or a detailed quote request.

core_features: Load dynamic calculator configuration (project types, HVAC efficiency levels, and target energy savings range) using getCostCalculatorOptions and bind them to the calculator form inputs.

Prefill calculator inputs from incoming URL parameters (project_type, location_city, location_state, floor_area_sq_ft, hvac_efficiency_level, target_energy_savings_percent) when present, so users arriving from other pages see a preconfigured calculator.
Validate required calculator inputs and, when the user clicks "Calculate Estimate", call calculateCostEstimate with the current form values to create and retrieve a CostEstimate.
Display the returned CostEstimate details (including estimated_cost and related input values) in an estimate summary area after a successful calculateCostEstimate call.
Offer follow-up actions ("Email This Estimate" and "Request Detailed Quote") that open an embedded EstimateRequest form pre-bound to the current CostEstimate.
Submit the EstimateRequest form via submitEstimateRequest, passing the current costEstimateId together with user contact details, facility_type, submitted_via, and optional notes.

After a successful submitEstimateRequest call, show a confirmation state and expose an optional navigation link to contact.html with inquiry_type=design_build_project so users can discuss the estimate with the team.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Open calculator with prefilled parameters from another page
1. User clicks a link or button on index.html, service_detail.html, or project_detail.html that navigates to cost_calculator.html with one or more URL parameters (e.g., project_type, location_city, floor_area_sq_ft).
2. On page load, the calculator-options-loader component calls getCostCalculatorOptions and passes the result to the cost-calculator-form.
3. The cost-calculator-form reads the URL parameters and pre-populates matching form fields, provided the values are valid against the options returned by getCostCalculatorOptions.
4. User reviews or adjusts the prefilled inputs, then clicks the "Calculate Estimate" button.
5. The form validates inputs and calls calculateCostEstimate with the current values.
6. On success, estimate-summary-panel displays the returned CostEstimate, and estimate-actions-panel becomes active, allowing follow-up actions.
7. User optionally clicks "Email This Estimate" or "Request Detailed Quote", opening the estimate-request-form.
8. User completes name, email, facility_type, and optional notes, then submits the form.
9. submitEstimateRequest is called with costEstimateId from the latest calculation and the form values. On success, estimate-request-status shows a confirmation and the optional "Discuss this estimate with our team" link to contact.html?inquiry_type=design_build_project.

Workflow 2: Direct calculator use from a clean URL
1. User navigates directly to cost_calculator.html with no URL parameters.
2. calculator-options-loader calls getCostCalculatorOptions. cost-calculator-form initializes with empty inputs and option lists from the API.
3. User manually selects a project type and HVAC efficiency, enters location (city/state), floor area, and a target energy savings percentage within the allowed range.
4. User clicks "Calculate Estimate". The form validates required fields then calls calculateCostEstimate.
5. estimate-summary-panel shows the CostEstimate, including the estimated_cost value and relevant inputs.
6. If user wants a formal follow-up, they click one of the action buttons ("Email This Estimate" or "Request Detailed Quote"). This opens estimate-request-form with submitted_via preset based on the button clicked.
7. User completes contact details and submits. submitEstimateRequest is called and the resulting status and message are shown in estimate-request-status.

Workflow 3: Adjusting assumptions and recalculating
1. After seeing an initial estimate in estimate-summary-panel, user modifies one or more inputs in cost-calculator-form (for example, HVAC efficiency level or target energy savings).
2. Form change handler may update the URL query string to reflect the new values, without triggering a calculation yet.
3. User clicks "Recalculate" (same "Calculate Estimate" button). The latest form state is validated and sent to calculateCostEstimate again.
4. A new CostEstimate is returned; estimate-summary-panel replaces the previous estimate details with the latest data.
5. estimate-actions-panel remains available and any subsequent estimate-request-form submission will use the most recent costEstimateId.

Workflow 4: Submitting a detailed quote request then contacting the team
1. User configures the calculator and generates an estimate via calculateCostEstimate.
2. User opens estimate-request-form via the "Request Detailed Quote" button.
3. User fills in the form and submits; submitEstimateRequest returns success and an estimate_request_id and status.
4. estimate-request-status displays a success message using the returned message and status.
5. A link or button labelled functionally (e.g., "Discuss this estimate with our team") becomes available. When clicked, it navigates to contact.html with inquiry_type=design_build_project set in the URL.
6. The contact page can then use that inquiry_type parameter for its own logic (outside the scope of this page).
interactions:
Interaction: Initial options loading
- Trigger: Page load.
- Behavior: calculator-options-loader automatically calls getCostCalculatorOptions.
- On success: cost-calculator-form receives project_types, hvac_efficiency_levels, and target_energy_savings_range to configure select options and input constraints.
- On failure: A generic error state is shown near the form (via estimate-request-status or inline messaging), and the calculator inputs dependent on options are disabled until options can be reloaded.

Interaction: URL-based prefill
- Trigger: After options load, cost-calculator-form reads URL parameters project_type, location_city, location_state, floor_area_sq_ft, hvac_efficiency_level, and target_energy_savings_percent.
- Behavior: For each parameter present and valid, the corresponding input is set to that value. Values that do not match the allowed ranges or option lists are ignored rather than forcing invalid state.
- Optional: Form-change handlers may update the URL query parameters to mirror the current form state whenever inputs change or when a new calculation is performed.

Interaction: Calculate Estimate button
- Trigger: User clicks the "Calculate Estimate" button in cost-calculator-form.
- Behavior: The form validates that all required inputs are provided and within the ranges defined by getCostCalculatorOptions (especially target_energy_savings_percent).
- On valid input: calculateCostEstimate is called with the current form values.
- While pending: Button is disabled and a loading indicator is shown in the estimate-summary-panel area.
- On success: estimate-summary-panel displays the returned CostEstimate fields and estimate-actions-panel is enabled.
- On error: An error message is displayed and no previous estimate is overwritten until a successful response is received.

Interaction: Email This Estimate button
- Trigger: User clicks "Email This Estimate" in estimate-actions-panel when a CostEstimate is present.
- Behavior: estimate-request-form opens or expands, with submitted_via internally set to the appropriate value for an email-based request.
- The form requires name, email, and facility_type; notes is optional.
- On submit: submitEstimateRequest is called with costEstimateId from the latest CostEstimate and the user's form data.
- On success: estimate-request-status displays confirmation using the returned message and status.
- On error: A clear error state is shown; the user can correct inputs or retry submission.

Interaction: Request Detailed Quote button
- Trigger: User clicks "Request Detailed Quote" in estimate-actions-panel when a CostEstimate is present.
- Behavior: Similar to the email action, but estimate-request-form internally sets submitted_via to the detailed-quote value.
- The rest of the flow mirrors the email interaction, using submitEstimateRequest and showing status via estimate-request-status.

Interaction: Discuss this estimate with our team CTA
- Trigger: After submitEstimateRequest returns a successful response, and a CostEstimate exists, the status component shows an optional CTA.
- Behavior: When clicked, this link navigates to contact.html with inquiry_type=design_build_project added to the URL query string.
- No additional API calls are made from this page for this navigation.

Interaction: Form field changes and URL sync
- Trigger: User changes any calculator form field.
- Behavior: The cost-calculator-form component updates its internal state and may push the corresponding key/value pairs into the browser's URL query string (using standard navigation APIs) without reloading the page.
- This ensures that if the user reloads the page or shares the URL, the current calculator configuration will be reconstructed from these parameters on load.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven by a combination of URL parameters, API responses, and transient in-memory component state, with no use of localStorage or similar client storage.

URL input state:
- On each page load, the cost-calculator-form parses the current URL query string for the known parameters: project_type, location_city, location_state, floor_area_sq_ft, hvac_efficiency_level, target_energy_savings_percent.
- These parsed values are only applied to the form after getCostCalculatorOptions has returned, so that any enumerated fields (project_type, hvac_efficiency_level) can be validated against the allowed options.
- For numeric parameters (floor_area_sq_ft, target_energy_savings_percent), the component checks that they can be parsed to numbers and, for the energy savings percentage, that they fall within the min_percent and max_percent boundaries from target_energy_savings_range. Invalid values are ignored.

URL output/state synchronization:
- Whenever the user updates a calculator field, the form component can update the URL query string to match the new values (e.g., using history.replaceState to avoid full reloads). Only fields with non-empty values are written; empty fields are removed from the query string to avoid ambiguity.
- After a successful calculateCostEstimate call, the components may also ensure that the core input parameters in the URL reflect the values used for that calculation. This makes the URL a reliable representation of the current configuration used to compute the most recent estimate.
- The result of calculateCostEstimate (including cost_estimate_id and estimated_cost) is kept in memory by the estimate-summary-panel and related components and is not required to be encoded into URL parameters.

In-memory state:
- The most recent CostEstimate returned from calculateCostEstimate is held in component state (e.g., within estimate-summary-panel, and shared with estimate-actions-panel and estimate-request-form). This includes cost_estimate_id, project_type, location fields, floor_area_sq_ft, hvac_efficiency_level, target_energy_savings_percent, and estimated_cost.
- estimate-request-form maintains its own draft state for name, email, facility_type, and notes until the user submits or clears the form.
- estimate-request-status retains the latest response from submitEstimateRequest (success flag, estimate_request_id, status, message) and uses it to render confirmation or error messages.

Error and loading states:
- Separate flags are maintained in memory for the loading/error states of getCostCalculatorOptions, calculateCostEstimate, and submitEstimateRequest. These flags drive the availability and disabled/enabled state of buttons and inputs, but are not reflected in the URL.

No localStorage:
- The page does not store any data in localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies. All persistent configuration comes from the URL parameters and the current API responses.

components.functionality: On page initialization, fetches dynamic configuration for the cost calculator using getCostCalculatorOptions and passes the resulting project_types, hvac_efficiency_levels, and target_energy_savings_range to child components (primarily the cost-calculator-form). Manages loading and error states for this initial configuration step.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when the page is loaded; triggers getCostCalculatorOptions with no parameters.
onOptionsLoadSuccess: Receives the response from getCostCalculatorOptions and forwards the returned options to cost-calculator-form for binding to form inputs and constraints.

onOptionsLoadError: Handles errors from getCostCalculatorOptions by setting an error state that other components can use to display a message and by disabling calculator interactions that require these options.

components.functionality: Provides inputs for configuring the cost estimate calculation, binds API-supplied options to selectable fields, applies URL-based prefill logic, validates input, and triggers calculateCostEstimate when the user submits the form.
event_handlers: onOptionsReceived: Called when calculator-options-loader successfully returns configuration; populates select fields for project_type and hvac_efficiency_level and sets constraints for target_energy_savings_percent based on target_energy_savings_range.

onUrlParamsParsed: Reads URL parameters (project_type, location_city, location_state, floor_area_sq_ft, hvac_efficiency_level, target_energy_savings_percent) after options are available and pre-fills the corresponding form fields if values are valid.
onFieldChange: Updates internal form state whenever the user modifies any input; may also synchronize the relevant field value to the URL query string.
onValidate: Ensures all required fields are present and conform to constraints from getCostCalculatorOptions (e.g., numeric ranges for target_energy_savings_percent) before allowing a calculation.
onSubmit: Triggered when the user clicks the "Calculate Estimate" button; if validation passes, calls calculateCostEstimate with current form values.

onCalculateSuccess: Receives the CostEstimate returned from calculateCostEstimate, stores it in state, and emits an event or callback to estimate-summary-panel and estimate-actions-panel so they can update their views.
onCalculateError: Handles errors from calculateCostEstimate by exposing an error message and keeping any prior valid estimate visible until a new successful result is received.

components.functionality: Displays the latest CostEstimate returned by calculateCostEstimate, including the estimated cost and the key inputs that produced it, enabling users to clearly see the outcome of their configuration.
event_handlers: onEstimateUpdated: Triggered when cost-calculator-form successfully completes calculateCostEstimate; binds the returned CostEstimate data (including cost_estimate_id, input fields, and estimated_cost) to the display.
onLoadingStateChange: Listens to calculation in-progress state to show or hide loading indicators while calculateCostEstimate is running.
onClearEstimate: Optional handler used when form inputs are reset; clears any displayed estimate to avoid confusion when the configuration no longer matches the last calculation.

components.functionality: Provides action buttons that let the user take the next step with the current CostEstimate, specifically opening the embedded estimate-request-form to either email the estimate or request a detailed quote.
event_handlers: onEstimateAvailable: Enables the action buttons only after a valid CostEstimate has been set by estimate-summary-panel or cost-calculator-form.

onClickEmailThisEstimate: When the user clicks the email action, checks that a CostEstimate is present, then opens estimate-request-form with submitted_via set for an email-type request and passes the current costEstimateId.

onClickRequestDetailedQuote: When the user clicks the detailed quote action, opens estimate-request-form with submitted_via set for a detailed-quote-type request and passes the current costEstimateId.
onEstimateCleared: Disables the action buttons if no active CostEstimate exists (e.g., after form reset).

components.functionality: Captures user contact details and facility context for the current CostEstimate and submits them as an EstimateRequest using submitEstimateRequest, supporting both email and detailed quote submission paths.
event_handlers: onOpenWithMode: Invoked by estimate-actions-panel with parameters costEstimateId and submitted_via; initializes the form state for either email or detailed-quote mode and ensures costEstimateId is set.
onFieldChange: Updates internal state for name, email, facility_type, and notes as the user enters data.
onValidate: Before submission, verifies that required fields (name, email, facility_type) are provided and that a costEstimateId is available from the latest CostEstimate.
onSubmit: Called when the user clicks the form submit button; if validation passes, calls submitEstimateRequest with costEstimateId, name, email, facility_type, submitted_via, and notes.

onSubmitSuccess: Receives the response from submitEstimateRequest (success, estimate_request_id, status, message) and passes it to estimate-request-status for display; may also clear or lock the form fields to prevent duplicate submissions.
onSubmitError: Handles errors from submitEstimateRequest by surfacing an error message and leaving the form editable so the user can make corrections and retry.

components.functionality: Shows feedback for the latest call to submitEstimateRequest, including success or error messages, and provides an optional link to continue the conversation on the contact page.
event_handlers: onRequestResult: Triggered when estimate-request-form completes a submitEstimateRequest call; binds the returned success flag, estimate_request_id, status, and message to the status display.

onShowDiscussCTA: When a request is successfully submitted, exposes a CTA element that, when activated, navigates the user to contact.html with inquiry_type=design_build_project by updating the browser location.
onDismissStatus: Allows the user to dismiss the status banner, resetting its visual state while keeping any necessary internal data (such as the status) available if needed.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing stacked header (utility bar → black brand bar → dark grey navigation), the Cost Calculator page continues the mockup’s single, centered content column. Within this white content area, components are arranged in a clear top‑to‑bottom stack: an optional status banner, then a primary calculator block, followed by a secondary quote‑request block. Inside the main calculator block, content is split into a two‑column internal layout: the form on the left and the summary on the right, both contained within one centered white card to stay faithful to the single‑column page structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing stacked header (utility bar → black brand bar → dark grey navigation), the Cost Calculator page continues the mockup’s single, centered content column. Within this white content area, components are arranged in a clear top‑to‑bottom stack: an optional status banner, then a primary calculator block, followed by a secondary quote‑request block. Inside the main calculator block, content is split into a two‑column internal layout: the form on the left and the summary on the right, both contained within one centered white card to stay faithful to the single‑column page structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header bands occupy the top ~20–25% of the viewport as in the mockup. The main calculator block fills most of the remaining vertical space (~50–60% of the viewport), visually replacing the hero image with a large, white, centered card. This card spans about 70–80% of the viewport width (constrained to a max content width), with generous side margins. Within the card, the left form area occupies roughly 65–70% of the card width, and the right summary panel 30–35%. The action panel sits as a full‑width strip at the bottom of this card. The quote‑request form and its status banner sit below as a distinct, but smaller, white section with ~32–48px vertical separation, reflecting the spacing used under the hero image in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: The calculator card is moderately dense, mirroring the compact header: minimal vertical height per row, concise labels, and well‑grouped fields with ~16–24px vertical spacing between field groups and ~8–12px between related controls. The summary panel is visually compact but with sufficient padding (~16–24px) to feel like a focused data block. The action panel has tightly grouped buttons aligned horizontally. Below, the quote‑request form is slightly more spacious, with more vertical breathing room (~24–32px) between fields to indicate a secondary, less transactional step. Around all blocks, the white content area maintains broader margins top and bottom (~32–48px) to preserve the airy feeling below the dense header.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: From the navigation bar, the eye drops into the white content area and first encounters any status banner, then the top edge of the main calculator card. Inside this card, the eye follows an F/Z‑pattern: starting at the top‑left with the form title and first controls, scanning horizontally across to the summary title and key estimate on the right, then moving down through the form fields. The summary’s primary cost figure (styled with bold typography and brand red accent) anchors attention at the top‑right of the card. At the bottom of the card, the eye is drawn left‑to‑right across the dark action strip to the dominant red primary button on the right. After completing the main interaction, the visual flow continues downward to the quote‑request section, which feels like a natural next step rather than competing with the primary calculator.

layout.overall_description: Within the familiar stacked header of light grey, black, and dark grey bands, the Cost Calculator page opens into a wide, white content area where a single, centered column controls the layout. At the top of this column, a full‑width status banner (when present) forms a slim, high‑contrast bar that echoes the horizontal header bands. Below it, a large, rectangular white card dominates the center of the page, replacing the role of the hero image in the original mockup. This card spans most of the content width but leaves comfortable white margins to either side, reinforcing a formal, corporate aesthetic. Inside the card, the left two‑thirds are dedicated to the cost calculator form, arranged in tidy rows and occasional two‑column field groupings. The right third houses a vertically oriented summary panel framed by a subtle border, where the estimated cost is prominently displayed at the top in strong black and red. Along the bottom of this card runs a full‑width action strip, visually grounded by a slightly darker background and right‑aligned primary red button. Below the main card, separated by a clear band of white space, a secondary card contains the quote request form and its status indicators, matching the same centered width and rectangular, flat styling but with slightly more relaxed spacing. Overall, the page reads as a structured, high‑contrast stack of horizontal bands and central cards, guiding the user smoothly from understanding their estimate to initiating a formal request.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The options loader appears within the bounds of the main calculator card, centered both horizontally and vertically in the area reserved for the cost-calculator-form and estimate-summary-panel. When active, the card still occupies its normal width (about 70–80% of the viewport, centered), but its interior is simplified to a single, compact loading state: a spinner or progress icon positioned in the exact center, with a short status message directly beneath it. The loader area should maintain a minimum height of roughly 200–240px so that it visually replaces the form and summary, presenting a solid, rectangular white block that echoes the hero image proportions from the mockup. The loader does not extend beyond the card; instead, it uses the card’s existing padding (~24px) and sits within its subtle border or shadow, preserving the main content’s spatial footprint and alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays or temporarily replaces the inner content of the main calculator card; fixed within the card’s centered position, does not scroll independently and disappears once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cost-calculator-form occupies the left ~65–70% of the main calculator card’s interior. The card itself is centered in the white content area and spans approximately 70–80% of the viewport width, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. Within this, the form’s top-left corner aligns with the card’s top-left padding edge, forming the first focal point after the user’s eye drops from the navigation. A bold, left-aligned section title (e.g., “Project Details”) sits at the top, with form fields arranged in stacked rows beneath. Where appropriate, wider fields (like project name) span the full form width, while related shorter inputs (e.g., width/height, start date/end date) are grouped into two equal internal columns, each with ~16px horizontal gap. Vertical spacing between field groups is ~24px, and between label and input ~8px, creating a moderately dense but organized rhythm that reflects the compact header style. The bottom of the form aligns roughly with or slightly above the bottom of the estimate-summary-panel on the right, so their vertical footprints feel balanced. On narrower screens, the form expands to take 100% of the card width, stacking above the summary panel but preserving the same central alignment and padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main calculator card; participates in a two-column layout with the summary on larger screens and collapses to a full-width block above the summary on smaller screens while remaining centered in the single main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate-summary-panel is a tall, narrow data block positioned inside the main calculator card, flush with the card’s right internal edge. It occupies about 30–35% of the card’s width and extends from near the top of the card down toward the bottom, visually mirroring the height of the form on the left. A subtle border or slightly darker grey background differentiates it from the rest of the card, while maintaining strong contrast against the page’s white background. At the very top of the panel, a small title like “Estimate Summary” sits left-aligned, followed immediately by the main cost figure, set in large, bold black type with a red accent (currency symbol or underline) to echo the brand’s highlight color. Below the main figure, secondary information (line items, assumptions, ranges) is stacked vertically in compact rows with ~12–16px spacing, forming a tight, information-dense column. The panel has internal padding of ~16–24px all around, and its top edge lines up horizontally with the form title on the left to create a strong visual bar that the eye scans across. On smaller screens, the summary panel drops directly beneath the form within the same card, spanning nearly the full card width while retaining its bordered, card‑within‑a‑card appearance and centered alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned within the main calculator card on larger screens; responsive behavior stacks it below the form within the same centered card on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate-actions-panel forms a horizontal strip along the bottom of the main calculator card, spanning the card’s full width from left to right and visually grounding the content above. It is separated from the form and summary by ~24px of vertical space and a light top border or slightly darker background band (e.g., very light grey) that evokes the slim utility bar from the site header. Inside this strip, content aligns along a single baseline: auxiliary actions (such as “Reset” or “Download PDF”) are left-aligned in a compact cluster, while the primary action button (e.g., “Calculate Estimate” or “Update Estimate”) is right-aligned and visually dominant. The primary button uses the brand red background with white text, rectangular shape with minimal rounding, and padding of ~8–12px vertically and 20–24px horizontally, matching the density of navigation items. Overall strip height remains modest (around 56–64px), making this a compact but prominent call-to-action band at the bottom of the card. On narrow screens, the strip remains at the bottom of the card but may stack secondary actions above or below the primary button while keeping the primary button right- or center-aligned to preserve its emphasis.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, attached to the bottom inside edge of the main calculator card; scrolls with the page rather than sticking, maintaining the card-as-unit interaction pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate-request-form is placed in a separate white card directly beneath the main calculator card, centered on the page and aligned to the same max-width content column. A vertical gap of approximately 32–48px separates this card from the calculator above, similar to the space that would separate the hero image from subsequent content in the mockup, visually indicating a new section. The card width matches or is slightly narrower than the main calculator card (about 60–70% of viewport width, constrained by the same max-width container). At the top-left of this card, a clear, bold title such as “Request a Detailed Quote” anchors the section, potentially accompanied by a short one-line description beneath it in smaller type to soften the density. Form fields are arranged in a single column, each spanning the card’s internal width with ~24px vertical spacing between fields, making this section feel more open than the calculator form. Key required fields (name, company, email, phone, project details) stack vertically with labels above inputs and ample padding (~16–24px) inside the card. The submit button is placed at the bottom-right of the card contents, mirroring the right-aligned action from the main calculator but at slightly lower visual intensity (e.g., smaller, or with less surrounding contrast) to signal this as a follow-up step rather than the primary interaction.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming a separate, centered card stacked below the main calculator card within the single main content column; no sticky or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate-request-status appears as a slim, full-width banner within the main content column, positioned directly above the estimate-request-form card it refers to. It spans the same width as the quote-request card (aligned to the centered content column) and sits with a small vertical offset of ~8–16px above the form card, visually pairing the two. The banner height is compact (around 40–56px) and uses a strong, high-contrast background color (e.g., solid green for success, red for error, or dark grey for neutral info) reminiscent of the colored bands in the header. Inside the banner, an icon on the left (checkmark, warning, info) is followed by a short status message in a single line of text, left-aligned with ~16–24px left padding. The top and bottom edges of the banner create a crisp horizontal band that the eye notices quickly when scrolling down to the quote request area, but its smaller size and localized position keep it from competing visually with the main calculator card above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static banner that appears and disappears above the estimate-request-form; it scrolls with the page and does not overlay other content. When not needed, the space it occupies collapses so the quote request form moves up naturally.

name: Contact & Inquiries
title: Contact & Inquiries
description: Allows visitors to view primary contact information and submit different types of inquiries, including detailed design-build project requests, using dynamic configuration and the inquiry submission API.

core_features: Load dynamic contact configuration via getContactPageConfig, including main office details, available inquiry types, budget range options, referral sources, preferred contact methods, and preferred time windows.
Display main office contact information from configuration so users can contact the company directly without filling out a form.
Provide an inquiry type selector (e.g., tabs or segmented control) bound to inquiry_types from getContactPageConfig; optionally pre-select the type using the inquiry_type URL parameter.

Render a single inquiry form whose visible fields change based on the selected inquiry_type, including a specialized flow for design_build_project that captures project_type, project_size_sq_ft, budget range selection, desired_start_date, referral_source, and contact preferences.

Allow selection of a predefined budget range from budget_range_options for design-build inquiries, optionally preselected by budgetRangeOptionId URL parameter, and map the chosen option to the submitInquiry parameters (budgetRangeOptionId, budget_range_label, budget_min_amount, budget_max_amount).
Allow selection of referral_source from the configured referral_sources, optionally preselected via the referral_source URL parameter when it matches a configured value.
Capture contact preferences using preferred_contact_methods and preferred_time_windows from configuration and map them to preferred_contact_method and preferred_contact_time_window in submitInquiry.

Perform client-side validation of required fields (at minimum contact_full_name and contact_email for all inquiry types, plus additional required fields for design_build_project) before calling submitInquiry.

Submit the inquiry using submitInquiry with parameters derived from the current inquiry_type, form values, and selected configuration options, then display a success or error state based on the API response.
After successful submission, show a confirmation message including server-returned status and an optional navigation link back to index.html, without reusing stale form data.
user_workflows: Workflow 1: General inquiry from main navigation: (1) User opens contact.html without URL parameters from the site header navigation; (2) Page init calls getContactPageConfig and waits for configuration; (3) Once loaded, the inquiry-type selector defaults to a general_inquiry type, either by matching a value from inquiry_types or using the first available type if no explicit default is defined; (4) The general inquiry fields (contact_full_name, contact_email, optional contact_phone, message, referral_source, preferred_contact_method, preferred_contact_time_window) are displayed; (5) User fills in required fields; (6) User clicks Submit Inquiry; (7) The form validates that required fields are present; (8) If valid, the page calls submitInquiry with inquiry_type set to general_inquiry and other parameters set from the form (non-applicable parameters like project_size_sq_ft may be passed as null or omitted according to implementation); (9) On a successful response with success true, the page shows a confirmation message and may hide or disable the form; (10) User may click a Return to Homepage link that navigates to index.html.

Workflow 2: Design-build project inquiry deep-linked from a services page: (1) User clicks a call-to-action on services.html or service_detail.html that links to contact.html with URL parameters such as inquiry_type=design_build_project, project_type set to a supported project type, and optionally budgetRangeOptionId; (2) contact.html loads and calls getContactPageConfig; (3) After configuration is available, the page parses the URL parameters; (4) If inquiry_type matches a configured inquiry_types value, that type is selected in the inquiry-type selector; otherwise, the default logic for selecting an inquiry type applies; (5) If the selected type is design_build_project, the design-build specific fields are shown; (6) If project_type URL parameter is present and matches one of the supported project_type enum values, it is used to prefill the project type field; otherwise that field remains unselected; (7) If budgetRangeOptionId URL parameter is present and matches one of the budget_range_options from configuration, that option is preselected and its min_amount and max_amount are bound to the budget-related fields; (8) User can adjust any prefilled values, complete project_size_sq_ft, desired_start_date, referral_source (either from URL or user selection), preferred_contact_method, preferred_contact_time_window, contact_full_name, contact_email, and contact_phone; (9) On clicking Submit Inquiry, validation ensures that design_build_project-required fields are populated; (10) The page calls submitInquiry with inquiry_type set to design_build_project and parameters including project_type, project_size_sq_ft, budgetRangeOptionId (and associated label and amounts), desired_start_date, referral_source, contact preferences, and message; (11) On success, a confirmation state is displayed with server-returned status and an optional link back to index.html.

Workflow 3: Inquiry with pre-tagged referral source from homepage: (1) User clicks a contact link on index.html that routes to contact.html with a referral_source URL parameter; (2) contact.html loads and executes getContactPageConfig; (3) When configuration is available, the page attempts to match the referral_source URL parameter with one of the referral_sources values from configuration; (4) If a match is found, the referral_source selector in the form is initialized to that value; if no match is found, the field remains unselected; (5) The inquiry_type may be defaulted or controlled by an additional inquiry_type URL parameter; (6) User fills out the remaining form fields appropriate to the selected inquiry_type; (7) On Submit Inquiry, the selected referral_source is passed as the referral_source argument to submitInquiry along with other form-derived parameters; (8) On success, the confirmation state is shown; the user may then navigate away or use the Return to Homepage link.
interactions: On page initialization, a controller component calls getContactPageConfig once; until the response returns, interactive components that depend on configuration (such as inquiry type selector, budget range dropdown, referral source dropdown, and preferred contact options) remain disabled or show a loading state.

After configuration is loaded, the page parses URL parameters (inquiry_type, project_type, budgetRangeOptionId, referral_source) and applies them to the initial state: (a) selects the matching inquiry_type if present and valid; (b) for design_build_project, pre-populates project_type if it matches a supported enum value; (c) pre-selects a budget range if budgetRangeOptionId matches a budget_range_options entry; (d) sets the referral_source field if the URL parameter matches a configured referral_sources value.

When the user clicks a different inquiry type in the inquiry-type selector, the selected inquiry type state updates, the form re-renders to show only fields relevant to that type, and non-applicable fields are logically cleared from the model used for submission; the page may also update the inquiry_type query parameter in the URL using history.replaceState without reloading the page.

When the user changes the budget range selection within a design-build inquiry, the selected option's identifier and numeric range from budget_range_options are bound to the internal form state used to populate budgetRangeOptionId, budget_range_label, budget_min_amount, and budget_max_amount parameters for submitInquiry.

When the user selects a referral source from the referral_sources options, the selected value is bound to the referral_source parameter for submitInquiry; if no referral source is selected, the parameter may be omitted or left null, respecting the Inquiry model's optionality.

When the user selects a preferred contact method from preferred_contact_methods and a preferred time window from preferred_time_windows, their values update the form state and are used to populate preferred_contact_method and preferred_contact_time_window for submitInquiry.

When the user enters or edits text fields (contact_full_name, contact_email, contact_phone, message, and any project-specific text/number fields such as project_size_sq_ft), the internal Inquiry draft state is updated immediately so that the submit handler can construct the submitInquiry payload directly from this state.

On clicking the Submit Inquiry button, a submit handler performs validation based on the current inquiry_type: it checks that contact_full_name and contact_email are present for all types, and for design_build_project it also checks that any required project-specific fields (such as project_type and any required budget or size fields defined by business rules) are present; if validation fails, field-level and/or global error messages are displayed and submitInquiry is not called.

If validation passes, the submit handler calls submitInquiry with all parameters mapped from the form state and configuration-derived values; during this call, the form is typically disabled or a submitting state is shown to prevent duplicate submissions.

After submitInquiry resolves: (a) if success is true, the page displays a confirmation panel showing the message and status returned by the API and may clear or lock the form; (b) if success is false or the call fails, an error message is displayed and the form remains editable so the user can correct inputs and retry.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is initialized by the response from getContactPageConfig combined with URL parameters and evolves based on user interactions. URL parameters are read once after configuration is available to determine the initial inquiry_type, project_type (for design_build_project), budgetRangeOptionId, and referral_source. If inquiry_type is present in the URL and matches one of the inquiry_types values from configuration, it becomes the initial selected type; otherwise a default inquiry type is chosen (for example, the first entry from inquiry_types). If project_type URL parameter is present and the selected inquiry_type is design_build_project, the project_type form field is initialized with that value when it matches a supported enum; otherwise it remains empty. If budgetRangeOptionId is present in the URL, the page looks up the corresponding budget_range_options entry from the configuration; if an entry is found, it is used to initialize the selected budget range and to bind the numeric budget_min_amount and budget_max_amount values; if not found, no budget range is preselected. If referral_source is in the URL and matches one of the configured referral_sources values, it is bound as the initial referral_source selection; otherwise the referral source field is left unset. After initialization, user interactions (such as changing inquiry type or form fields) update in-memory component state rather than modifying URL parameters, with the optional exception of inquiry_type: when the user changes the inquiry type selector, the page can update the inquiry_type URL parameter via history.replaceState so that a refreshed or shared URL reopens the page with the same inquiry type; other form values are not written back to the URL. No state is stored in localStorage or other persistent browser storage; the URL and the API responses are the only inputs used for state initialization.

components.functionality: Initializes the Contact & Inquiries page by calling getContactPageConfig, parsing incoming URL parameters for inquiry_type, project_type, budgetRangeOptionId, and referral_source, and deriving the initial page state (selected inquiry type, preselected project type, budget range, and referral source) that is passed down to child components.
Displays the main office address, phone number, and email using the main_office object from getContactPageConfig so users can reach the company directly without using the form.

Renders selectable inquiry types (such as design_build_project, general_inquiry, service_support) from inquiry_types configuration, highlights the currently selected type, and notifies the parent when the user changes the selection so the form can reconfigure which fields are shown.

Shown only when the current inquiry_type is design_build_project; captures project-specific information including project_type, project_size_sq_ft, budget range selection (with options from budget_range_options), desired_start_date, and referral_source; applies URL-derived defaults when provided.
Collects the user’s preferred contact method and preferred contact time window, using the configured preferred_contact_methods and preferred_time_windows options, and binds them to the Inquiry model.

Captures core contact fields that are required across inquiry types, including contact_full_name and contact_email, plus optional contact_phone, and ensures they remain synchronized with the Inquiry state used for submission.
Provides a free-text message or description field bound to Inquiry.message for users to add additional context about their inquiry, regardless of inquiry type.

Orchestrates all form sections (design-build specific fields, contact preferences, contact details, and message), maintains an aggregate draft Inquiry state corresponding to submitInquiry parameters, performs validation when the user submits, and triggers the submitInquiry API call on successful validation.
Displays field-level and global validation messages produced by the inquiry-form component when required fields are missing or invalid for the chosen inquiry_type.

Shows the outcome of the submitInquiry call, including success state with the server-returned status and message, and optionally an error state; after success it also exposes an action to navigate back to index.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12‑column style grid with full-width header bands and a centered content column inside max-width constraints.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit within the white main content band that appears directly beneath the existing dark navigation bar. Inside this band, content is organized into a single centered column following a linear top-to-bottom flow: first a main-office contact information panel as the visual anchor, then the inquiry-type tab selector, then feedback messages, followed by the inquiry form that contains the individual form sections. The loader and feedback panels visually overlay or slot into this same central column rather than creating new structural bands.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit within the white main content band that appears directly beneath the existing dark navigation bar. Inside this band, content is organized into a single centered column following a linear top-to-bottom flow: first a main-office contact information panel as the visual anchor, then the inquiry-type tab selector, then feedback messages, followed by the inquiry form that contains the individual form sections. The loader and feedback panels visually overlay or slot into this same central column rather than creating new structural bands.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header/utility/navigation stack occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport. Below that, the contact & inquiries content uses a centered content column that spans about 70–75% of the viewport width (max-width around 1100–1200px), leaving generous white margins on both sides. Within this column, the main-office contact panel takes the upper portion, the tab selector and feedback panels occupy a relatively shallow strip, and the form sections consume the bulk of the vertical space. Vertical spacing between these major blocks follows the 8px scale, typically 24–32px between distinct groups and 16–24px within grouped content.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is moderately dense at the top, where the main-office contact information is grouped into a compact panel, while still allowing 16–24px padding inside for clarity. The tab selector is tight and efficient, with tabs close together to echo the compact navigation style above. The form body is denser than the hero image from the mockup but remains organized by clearly separated sections with 24–32px gaps and two-column field rows to prevent it from feeling overcrowded. Feedback panels are visually strong but occupy minimal vertical height, appearing as concise alert strips within the central column.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: After the site header, the eye lands on the left-aligned main-office contact panel at the top of the content column, then scans horizontally across its key details (phone/address), mirroring the F-pattern of the mockup. From there, attention drops straight down to the horizontal inquiry-type tabs, scanning left to right for the active red tab. The active tab acts as a visual anchor that leads the eye down into the form: users then scan each section vertically while also doing quick left-to-right passes across two-column rows of fields. Red accents on the active tab, required field markers, validation errors, and the primary submit button keep attention on important interactions while the black/grey text and borders maintain a serious, corporate tone.

layout.overall_description: Below the dark navigation band, a wide white content area stretches full-width across the browser, with a centered content column occupying roughly three-quarters of the width. At the top of this column sits a rectangular main-office contact panel, slightly separated from the nav by about 32px of white space, acting as the hero element for the page. It is horizontally centered but left-aligned in its internal content, with ample padding and clear typography that immediately communicates phone, email, and address details. Directly beneath this, a horizontal strip of inquiry-type tabs runs across the same column width, each tab a flat rectangular block; the currently selected inquiry type is highlighted in red with white or light text, while inactive tabs appear in dark grey on a light grey background, echoing the site’s navigation style. Under the tabs, a narrow band is reserved for feedback panels, which appear as full-width alert boxes when needed, framed by red or neutral borders and aligned with the rest of the content. Below this band, the main inquiry form fills most of the remaining vertical space. It is structured as a stack of clearly labeled sections—contact details, project-specific information, message, and contact preferences—each spanning the column width with a subtle separation of 24–32px of vertical space between them. Within sections, fields are arranged in a comfortable two-column grid on desktop, collapsing to a single column on smaller viewports, with a large, high-contrast submit button anchored at the bottom right of the form area. The entire composition feels like a structured, corporate document transformed into a web form: compact and information-rich at the top, then opening into a deeper, carefully organized input area, all contained within strong horizontal bands and a central alignment that echo the original mockup’s hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual data-loader component is conceptually anchored to the entire white content area for the Contact & Inquiries page. It covers the same centered content column that contains the main-office contact panel, tabs, and form. When active (e.g., during initial page load or submission), it displays a semi-opaque white overlay starting just below the navigation bar and extending down to the bottom of the viewport or content column, whichever is taller. The overlay is aligned to the same max-width as the main content but can softly bleed into the side margins for a full-width feel if desired. A simple spinner and short status text are vertically and horizontally centered within this overlay, maintaining the serious black/grey color tone with a subtle red accent in the spinner. When inactive, the loader occupies no visual space and does not affect the stacking order of the other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute or fixed-position overlay relative to the main content column, covering all interactive elements below the navigation while active; otherwise, it is invisible and takes up no layout space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned at the top of the white content area, the main-office-contact-panel is the first visible block of the page’s main column. It sits approximately 32px below the navigation bar, centered within the page, and spans about 70–75% of the viewport width up to a max-width of around 1100–1200px, matching the overall content column width. The panel is a clean, rectangular card with a white or very light grey background and a subtle grey border or bottom rule, using 24px of internal padding on all sides.
